TensorFlow, Hugging Face's Transformers, and Meta's PyTorch.

Developing ML models requires specialized ML knowledge. It requires
knowing different types of ML algorithms (such as clustering, logistic
regression, decision trees, and collaborative filtering) and neural
network architectures (such as feedforward, recurrent, convolutional,
and transformer). It also requires understanding how a model learns,
including concepts such as gradient descent, loss function,
regularization, etc.

With the availability of foundation models, ML knowledge is no longer a
must-have for building AI applications. I've met many wonderful and
successful AI application builders who aren't at all interested in
learning about gradient descent. However, ML knowledge is still
extremely valuable, as it expands the set of tools that you can use and
helps troubleshooting when a model doesn't work as expected.

<aside data-type="sidebar" epub:type="sidebar" class="calibre20">

::: {#ch01.html_ch01_on_the_differences_among_training_pre_training_f_1730130814986798 .sidebar}
## On the Differences Among Training, Pre-Training, [Finetuning, and Post-Training]{.keep-together} {.calibre21}

Training always involves changing model weights, but not all changes to
model weights constitute training. For example, quantization, the
process of reducing the precision of model weights, technically changes
the model's weight values but isn't considered training.

The term training can often be used in place of pre-training,
finetuning, and post-training, which refer to different training phases:

Pre-training

:   []{#ch01.html_id641 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="pre-training"
    data-type="indexterm"}*Pre-training refers to* training a model from
    scratch---the model weights are randomly initialized. For LLMs,
    pre-training often involves training a model for text completion.
    Out of all training steps, pre-training is often the most
    resource-intensive by a long shot. For the InstructGPT model,
    pre-training takes up to [98% of the overall compute and data
    resources](https://oreil.ly/G3LUh){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
    .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. Pre-training also takes a long time to do. A
    small mistake during pre-training can incur a significant financial
    loss and set back the project significantly. Due to the
    resource-intensive nature of pre-training, this has become an art
    that only a few practice. Those with expertise in pre-training large
    models, however, are heavily sought
    after.^[24](#ch01.html_id642){#ch01.html_id642-marker .pcalibre7
    .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

Finetuning

:   []{#ch01.html_id643 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="finetuning"
    secondary="defined" data-type="indexterm"}Finetuning means
    continuing to train a previously trained model---the model weights
    are obtained from the previous training process. Because the model
    already has certain knowledge from pre-training, finetuning
    typically requires fewer resources (e.g., data and compute) than
    pre-training.

Post-training

:   []{#ch01.html_id644 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="post-training"
    data-type="indexterm"}Many people use *post-training* to refer to
    the process of training a model after the pre-training phase.
    Conceptually, post-training and finetuning are the same and can be
    used interchangeably. However, sometimes, people might use them
    differently to signify the different goals. It's usually
    post-training when it's done by model developers. For example,
    OpenAI might post-train a model to make it better at following
    instructions before releasing it. It's finetuning when it's done by
    application developers. For example, you might finetune an OpenAI
    model (which might have been post-trained itself) to adapt it to
    your needs.

Pre-training and post-training make up a
spectrum.^[25](#ch01.html_id645){#ch01.html_id645-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ Their
processes and toolings are very similar. Their differences are explored
further in Chapters
[2](#ch02.html_ch02_understanding_foundation_models_1730147895571359){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"
xrefstyle="select:labelnumber"} and [7](#ch07.html_ch07){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"
xrefstyle="select:labelnumber"}.

Some people use the term training to refer to prompt engineering, which
isn't correct. I read a [*Business Insider*
article](https://oreil.ly/0VqmX){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} where the author said she trained ChatGPT to
mimic her younger self. She did so by feeding her childhood journal
entries into ChatGPT. Colloquially, the author's usage of the word
*training* is correct, as she's teaching the model to do something. But
technically, if you teach a model what to do via the context input into
the model, you're doing prompt engineering. Similarly, I've seen people
using the term *finetuning* when what they do is prompt
engineering.[]{#ch01.html_id646 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch01.html30" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_id647
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch01.html29"
data-type="indexterm"}
:::

</aside>
::::
:::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Dataset engineering"}
::: {#ch01.html_ch01_dataset_engineering_1730130814986874 .dedication}
##### Dataset engineering {.calibre47}

[]{#ch01.html_id648 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="dataset engineering"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_id649 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model development" secondary="dataset engineering"
data-type="indexterm"}*Dataset engineering* refers to curating,
generating, and annotating the data needed for training and adapting AI
models.

[]{#ch01.html_id650 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="data annotation" secondary="dataset engineering and"}In
traditional ML engineering, most use cases are close-ended---a model's
output can only be among predefined values. For example, spam
classification with only two possible outputs, "spam" and "not spam", is
close-ended. Foundation models, however, are open-ended. Annotating
open-ended queries is much harder than annotating close-ended
queries---it's easier to determine whether an email is spam than to
write an essay. So data annotation is a much bigger challenge for AI
engineering.

Another difference is that traditional ML engineering works more with
tabular data, whereas foundation models work with unstructured data. In
AI engineering, data manipulation is more about deduplication,
tokenization, context retrieval, and quality control, including removing
sensitive information and toxic data. Dataset engineering is the focus
of
[Chapter 8](#ch08.html_ch08_dataset_engineering_1730130932019888){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

Many people argue that because models are now commodities, data will be
the main differentiator, making dataset engineering more important than
ever. How much data you need depends on the adapter technique you use.
Training a model from scratch generally requires more data than
finetuning, which, in turn, requires more data than prompt engineering.

Regardless of how much data you need, expertise in data is useful when
examining a model, as its training data gives important clues about that
model's strengths and weaknesses.
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Inference optimization"}
::: {#ch01.html_ch01_inference_optimization_1730130814986936 .dedication}
##### Inference optimization {.calibre47}

[]{#ch01.html_id651 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="inference optimization"}[]{#ch01.html_ch01.html31 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model development" secondary="inference optimization"
data-type="indexterm"}*Inference optimization* means making models
faster and cheaper. Inference optimization has always been important for
ML engineering. Users never say no to faster models, and companies can
always benefit from cheaper inference. However, as foundation models
scale up to incur even higher inference cost and latency, inference
optimization has become even more important.

One challenge with foundation models is that they are often
*autoregressive*---tokens are generated sequentially. If it takes 10 ms
for a model to generate a token, it'll take a second to generate an
output of 100 tokens, and even more for longer outputs. As users are
getting notoriously impatient, getting AI applications' latency down to
the [100 ms latency](https://oreil.ly/gGXZ-){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} expected for a typical internet
application is a huge challenge. Inference optimization has become an
active subfield in both industry and academia.

A summary of how the importance of different categories of model
development change with AI engineering is shown in
[Table 1-4](#ch01.html_ch01_table_4_1730130814941579){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

+----------------------+----------------------+---------------------------------------------------------+
| Category             | Building with        | Building with foundation models                         |
|                      | traditional ML       |                                                         |
+======================+======================+=========================================================+
| Modeling and         | ML knowledge is      | ML knowledge is a nice-to-have, not a                   |
| training             | required for         | must-have^[a](#ch01.html_id652){#ch01.html_id652-marker |
|                      | training a model     | .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4  |
|                      | from scratch         | data-type="noteref"}^                                   |
+----------------------+----------------------+---------------------------------------------------------+
| Dataset engineering  | More about feature   | Less about feature engineering and more about data      |
|                      | engineering,         | deduplication, tokenization, context retrieval, and     |
|                      | especially with      | quality control                                         |
|                      | tabular data         |                                                         |
+----------------------+----------------------+---------------------------------------------------------+
| Inference            | Important            | Even more important                                     |
| optimization         |                      |                                                         |
+----------------------+----------------------+---------------------------------------------------------+
| ^[a](#ch01.html_id652-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ Many people    |
| would dispute this claim, saying that ML knowledge is a must-have.                                    |
+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

: [Table 1-4. ]{.keep-together}How different responsibilities of model
development have changed with foundation models.
{#ch01_table_4_1730130814941579 .stafflist_table}

Inference optimization techniques, including quantization, distillation,
and parallelism, are discussed in Chapters
[7](#ch07.html_ch07){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 data-type="xref" xrefstyle="select:labelnumber"} through
[9](#ch09.html_ch09_inference_optimization_1730130963006301){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"
xrefstyle="select:labelnumber"}[]{#ch01.html_id653 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch01.html31" data-type="indexterm"}.[]{#ch01.html_id654
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch01.html27"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_id655 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch01.html25" data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::
::::::::::
:::::::::::

:::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Application development"}
::::::::: {#ch01.html_ch01_application_development_1730130814987002 .dedication}
#### Application development {.calibre36}

[]{#ch01.html_ch01.html32 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="application building"
secondary="engineering stack" tertiary="application development"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_ch01.html33 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="application development" data-type="indexterm"}With traditional
ML engineering, where teams build applications using their proprietary
models, the model quality is a differentiation. With foundation models,
where many teams use the same model, differentiation must be gained
through the application development process.

The application development layer consists of these responsibilities:
evaluation, prompt engineering, and AI interface.

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Evaluation"}
::: {#ch01.html_ch01_evaluation_1730130814987068 .dedication}
##### Evaluation {.calibre47}

[]{#ch01.html_id656 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="application development"
secondary="evaluation" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_id657
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="engineering stack"
secondary="application development" tertiary="evaluation"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_id658 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="evaluation" data-type="indexterm"}*Evaluation* is about
mitigating risks and uncovering opportunities. Evaluation is necessary
throughout the whole model adaptation process. Evaluation is needed to
select models, to benchmark progress, to determine whether an
application is ready for deployment, and to detect issues and
opportunities for improvement in production.

While evaluation has always been important in ML engineering, it's even
more important with foundation models, for many reasons. The challenges
of evaluating foundation models are discussed in
[Chapter 3](#ch03.html_ch03a_evaluation_methodology_1730150757064067){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}. To
summarize, these challenges chiefly arise from foundation models'
open-ended nature and expanded capabilities. For example, in close-ended
ML tasks like fraud detection, there are usually expected ground truths
that you can compare your model's outputs against. If a model's output
differs from the expected output, you know the model is wrong. For a
task like chatbots, however, there are so many possible responses to
each prompt that it is impossible to curate an exhaustive list of ground
truths to compare a model's response to.

The existence of so many adaptation techniques also makes evaluation
harder. A system that performs poorly with one technique might perform
much better with another. []{#ch01.html_id659 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
data-type="indexterm" primary="Gemini"}When Google launched Gemini in
December 2023, they claimed that []{#ch01.html_id660 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
data-type="indexterm" primary="ChatGPT" secondary="Gemini versus"}Gemini
is better than ChatGPT in the MMLU benchmark ([Hendrycks et al.,
2020](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.03300){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}). Google had evaluated Gemini using a prompt
engineering technique called [CoT@32](https://oreil.ly/VDwaR){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. In this technique, Gemini
was shown 32 examples, while ChatGPT was shown only 5 examples. When
both were shown five examples, ChatGPT performed better, as shown in
[Table 1-5](#ch01.html_ch01_table_5_1730130814941611){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

+-------------+---------+---------+------------+---------+-----------------------+--------+--------------+--------+---------+
|             | Gemini  | Gemini  | GPT-4      | GPT-3.5 | PaLM                  | Claude | Inflection-2 | Grok 1 | Llama-2 |
|             | Ultra   | Pro     |            |         | [2-L]{.keep-together} | 2      |              |        |         |
+=============+=========+=========+============+=========+=======================+========+==============+========+=========+
| MMLU        | 90.04%\ | 79.13%\ | 87.29%\    | 70%\    | 78.4%\                | 78.5%\ | 79.6%\       | 73.0%\ | 68.0%   |
| performance | CoT@32  | CoT@8   | CoT@32\    | 5-shot  | 5-shot                | 5-shot | 5-shot       | 5-shot |         |
|             |         |         | (via API)  |         |                       | CoT    |              |        |         |
|             +---------+---------+------------+---------+-----------------------+--------+--------------+--------+---------+
|             | 83.7%\  | 71.8%\  | 86.4%\     |         |                       |        |              |        |         |
|             | 5-shot  | 5-shot  | 5-shot     |         |                       |        |              |        |         |
|             |         |         | (reported) |         |                       |        |              |        |         |
+-------------+---------+---------+------------+---------+-----------------------+--------+--------------+--------+---------+

: [Table 1-5. ]{.keep-together}Different prompts can cause models to
perform very differently, as seen in Gemini's technical report (December
2023). {#ch01_table_5_1730130814941611 .stafflist_table}
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Prompt engineering and context construction"}
::: {#ch01.html_ch01_prompt_engineering_and_context_construction_1730130814987131 .dedication}
##### Prompt engineering and context construction {.calibre47}

[]{#ch01.html_id661 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="application development"
secondary="prompt engineering and context construction"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_id662 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="context construction" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_id663
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="engineering stack"
secondary="application development"
tertiary="prompt engineering and context construction"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_id664 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="prompt engineering" secondary="defined"
data-type="indexterm"}*Prompt engineering* is about getting AI models to
express the desirable behaviors from the input alone, without changing
the model weights. The Gemini evaluation story highlights the impact of
prompt engineering on model performance. By using a different prompt
engineering technique, Gemini Ultra's performance on MMLU went from
83.7% to 90.04%.

It's possible to get a model to do amazing things with just prompts. The
right instructions can get a model to perform the task you want, in the
format of your choice. Prompt engineering is not just about telling a
model what to do. It's also about giving the model the necessary context
and tools to do a given task. For complex tasks with long context, you
might also need to provide the model with a memory management system so
that the model can keep track of its history.
[Chapter 5](#ch05.html_ch05a_prompt_engineering_1730156991195551){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} discusses
prompt engineering, and
[Chapter 6](#ch06.html_ch06_rag_and_agents_1730157386571386){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} discusses
context construction.
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="AI interface"}
::: {#ch01.html_ch01_ai_interface_1730130814987191 .dedication}
##### AI interface {.calibre47}

[]{#ch01.html_id665 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="application development"
secondary="AI interface" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_id666
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="engineering stack"
secondary="application development" tertiary="AI interface"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_id667 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="interface, AI" data-type="indexterm"}*AI interface* means
creating an interface for end users to interact with your AI
applications. Before foundation models, only organizations with
sufficient resources to develop AI models could develop AI applications.
These applications were often embedded into the organizations' existing
products. For example, fraud detection was embedded into Stripe, Venmo,
and PayPal. Recommender systems were part of social networks and media
apps like Netflix, TikTok, and Spotify.

With foundation models, anyone can build AI applications. You can serve
your AI applications as standalone products or embed them into other
products, including products developed by other people. For example,
ChatGPT and Perplexity are standalone products, whereas GitHub's Copilot
is commonly used as a plug-in in VSCode, and Grammarly is commonly used
as a browser extension for Google Docs. Midjourney can either be used
via its standalone web app or via its integration in
[Discord.]{.keep-together}

There need to be tools that provide interfaces for standalone AI
applications or make it easy to integrate AI into existing products.
Here are just some of the interfaces that are gaining popularity for AI
applications:

- Standalone web, desktop, and mobile
  apps.^[26](#ch01.html_id668){#ch01.html_id668-marker .pcalibre7
  .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

- Browser extensions that let users quickly query AI models while
  browsing.

- Chatbots integrated into chat apps like Slack, Discord, WeChat, and
  WhatsApp.

- Many products, including VSCode, Shopify, and Microsoft 365, provide
  APIs that let developers integrate AI into their products as plug-ins
  and add-ons. These APIs can also be used by AI agents to interact with
  the world, as discussed in
  [Chapter 6](#ch06.html_ch06_rag_and_agents_1730157386571386){.pcalibre7
  .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

While the chat interface is the most commonly used, AI interfaces can
also be voice-based (such as with voice assistants) or embodied (such as
in augmented and virtual reality).

These new AI interfaces also mean new ways to collect and extract user
feedback. The conversation interface makes it so much easier for users
to give feedback in natural language, but this feedback is harder to
extract. User feedback design is discussed in []{#ch01.html_id669
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch01.html33"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_id670 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch01.html32"
data-type="indexterm"}[Chapter 10](#ch10.html_ch10_ai_engineering_architecture_and_user_feedback_1730130985311851){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="xref"}.[]{#ch01.html_id671 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch01.html24" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_id672
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch01.html24a"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch01.html_id673 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch01.html23" data-type="indexterm"}

A summary of how the importance of different categories of app
development changes with AI engineering is shown in
[Table 1-6](#ch01.html_ch01_table_6_1730130814941642){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

  Category             Building with traditional ML   Building with foundation models
  -------------------- ------------------------------ ---------------------------------
  AI interface         Less important                 Important
  Prompt engineering   Not applicable                 Important
  Evaluation           Important                      More important

  : [Table 1-6. ]{.keep-together}The importance of different categories
  in app development for AI engineering and ML engineering.
  {#ch01_table_6_1730130814941642 .stafflist_table}
:::
::::
:::::::::
::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="AI Engineering Versus Full-Stack Engineering"}
::: {#ch01.html_ch01_ai_engineering_versus_full_stack_engineering_1730130814987252 .dedication}
### AI Engineering Versus Full-Stack Engineering {.calibre35}

[]{#ch01.html_id674 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="application building"
secondary="engineering stack" tertiary="full-stack engineering versus"
data-type="indexterm"}The increased emphasis on application development,
especially on interfaces, brings AI engineering closer to full-stack
development.^[27](#ch01.html_id675){#ch01.html_id675-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ The
rising importance of interfaces leads to a shift in the design of AI
toolings to attract more frontend engineers. Traditionally, ML
engineering is Python-centric. Before foundation models, the most
[popular]{.keep-together} ML frameworks supported mostly Python APIs.
Today, Python is still popular, but there is also increasing support for
JavaScript APIs, with
[LangChain.js](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchainjs){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4},
[Transformers.js](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers.js){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, [OpenAI's Node
library](https://github.com/openai/openai-node){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, and [Vercel's AI
SDK](https://github.com/vercel/ai){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}.

While many AI engineers come from traditional ML backgrounds, more are
increasingly coming from web development or full-stack backgrounds. An
advantage that full-stack engineers have over traditional ML engineers
is their ability to quickly turn ideas into demos, get feedback, and
iterate.

With traditional ML engineering, you usually start with gathering data
and training a model. Building the product comes last. However, with AI
models readily available today, it's possible to start with building the
product first, and only invest in data and models once the product shows
promise, as visualized in
[Figure 1-16](#ch01.html_ch01_figure_16_1730130814920205){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch01.html_ch01_figure_16_1730130814920205" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0116.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A close-up of arrows Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 1-16.
</span>The new AI engineering workflow rewards those who can iterate
fast. Image recreated from “The Rise of the AI Engineer” (<a
href="https://oreil.ly/OOZK-"
class="pcalibre7 pcalibre5 pcalibre6 calibre10 pcalibre4">Shawn Wang,
2023</a>).</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In traditional ML engineering, model development and product development
are often disjointed processes, with ML engineers rarely involved in
product decisions at many organizations. However, with foundation
models, AI engineers tend to be much more involved in building the
product.[]{#ch01.html_id676 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch01.html20"
data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect1" pdf-bookmark="Summary"}
::: {#ch01.html_ch01_summary_1730130814987308 .dedication}
## Summary {.calibre4}

I meant this chapter to serve two purposes. One is to explain the
emergence of AI engineering as a discipline, thanks to the availability
of foundation models. Two is to give an overview of the process needed
to build applications on top of these models. I hope that this chapter
achieved this goal. As an overview chapter, it only lightly touched on
many concepts. These concepts will be explored further in the rest of
the book.

The chapter discussed the rapid evolution of AI in recent years. It
walked through some of the most notable transformations, starting with
the transition from language models to large language models, thanks to
a training approach called self-supervision. It then traced how language
models incorporated other data modalities to become foundation models,
and how foundation models gave rise to AI [engineering.]{.keep-together}

The rapid growth of AI engineering is motivated by the many applications
enabled by the emerging capabilities of foundation models. This chapter
discussed some of the most successful application patterns, both for
consumers and enterprises. Despite the incredible number of AI
applications already in production, we're still in the early stages of
AI engineering, with countless more innovations yet to be built.

Before building an application, an important yet often overlooked
question is whether you should build it. This chapter discussed this
question together with major considerations for building AI
applications.

While AI engineering is a new term, it evolved out of ML engineering,
which is the overarching discipline involved with building applications
with all ML models. Many principles from ML engineering are still
applicable to AI engineering. However, AI engineering also brings with
it new challenges and solutions. The last section of the chapter
discusses the AI engineering stack, including how it has changed from ML
engineering.

One aspect of AI engineering that is especially challenging to capture
in writing is the incredible amount of collective energy, creativity,
and engineering talent that the community brings. This collective
enthusiasm can often be overwhelming, as it's impossible to keep
up-to-date with new techniques, discoveries, and engineering feats that
seem to happen constantly.

One consolation is that since AI is great at information aggregation, it
can help us aggregate and summarize all these new updates. But tools can
help only to a certain extent. The more overwhelming a space is, the
more important it is to have a framework to help us navigate it. This
book aims to provide such a framework.

The rest of the book will explore this framework step-by-step, starting
with the fundamental building block of AI engineering: the foundation
models that make so many amazing applications
possible.[]{#ch01.html_id677 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch01.html0"

::: {.calibre31 data-type="footnotes"}
^[1](#ch01.html_id534-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ In this book, I use *traditional ML* to refer to
all ML before foundation models.

^[2](#ch01.html_id536-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ For non-English languages, a single Unicode
character can sometimes be represented as multiple tokens.

^[3](#ch01.html_id541-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ Autoregressive language models are sometimes
referred to as [causal language
models](https://oreil.ly/h0Y8x){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}.

^[4](#ch01.html_id542-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ Technically, a masked language model like BERT
can also be used for text generations if you try really hard.

^[5](#ch01.html_id545-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ The actual data labeling cost varies depending
on several factors, including the task's complexity, the scale (larger
datasets typically result in lower per-sample costs), and the labeling
service provider. For example, as of September 2024, [Amazon SageMaker
Ground Truth](https://oreil.ly/EVXJl){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} charges 8 cents per image for labeling fewer than
50,000 images, but only 2 cents per image for labeling more than 1
million images.

^[6](#ch01.html_id546-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ This is similar to how it's important for humans
to know when to stop talking.

^[7](#ch01.html_id547-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ In school, I was taught that model parameters
include both model weights and model biases. However, today, we
generally use model weights to refer to all parameters.

^[8](#ch01.html_id549-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ It seems counterintuitive that larger models
require more training data. If a model is more powerful, shouldn't it
require fewer examples to learn from? However, we're not trying to get a
large model to match the performance of a small model using the same
data. We're trying to maximize model performance.

^[9](#ch01.html_id561-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ For comparison, the entire US expenditures for
public elementary and secondary schools are around \$900 billion, only
nine times the investments in AI in the US.

^[10](#ch01.html_id566-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ Fun fact: as of September 16, 2024, the website
[*theresanaiforthat.com*](https://theresanaiforthat.com/){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} lists 16,814 AIs for 14,688
tasks and 4,803 jobs.

^[11](#ch01.html_id567-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ Exploring different AI applications is perhaps
one of my favorite things about writing this book. It's a lot of fun
seeing what people are building. You can find the [list of open source
AI applications](https://huyenchip.com/llama-police){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} that I track. The list is
updated every 12 hours.

^[12](#ch01.html_id572-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ Because enterprises usually spend a lot of money
on ads and marketing, automation there can lead to huge savings. On
average, 11% of a company's budget is spent on marketing. See
["Marketing Budgets Vary by
Industry"](https://oreil.ly/D0-yA){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} (Christine Moorman, *WSJ*, 2017).

^[13](#ch01.html_id574-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ I have found AI very helpful in the process of
writing this book, and I can see that AI will be able to automate many
parts of the writing process. When writing fiction, I often ask AI to
brainstorm ideas on what it thinks will happen next or how a character
might react to a situation. I'm still evaluating what kind of writing
can be automated and what kind of writing can't be.

^[14](#ch01.html_id575-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ My hypothesis is that we'll become so
distrustful of content on the internet that we'll only read content
generated by people or brands we trust.

^[15](#ch01.html_id584-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ It surprises me how long it takes Apple and
Amazon to incorporate generative AI advances into Siri and Alexa. A
friend thinks it's because these companies might have higher bars for
quality and compliance, and it takes longer to develop voice interfaces
than chat interfaces.

^[16](#ch01.html_id585-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ Disclaimer: I'm an advisor of Convai.

^[17](#ch01.html_id592-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ I currently have over 40,000 photos and videos
in my Google Photos. Without AI, it'd be near impossible for me to
search for the photos I want, when I want them.

^[18](#ch01.html_id593-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ Personally, I also find AI good at explaining
data and graphs. When encountering a confusing graph with too much
information, I ask ChatGPT to break it down for me.

^[19](#ch01.html_id599-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ Smaller startups, however, might have to
prioritize product focus and can't afford to have even one person to
"look around."

^[20](#ch01.html_id607-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ A running joke in the early days of generative
AI is that AI startups are OpenAI or Claude wrappers.

^[21](#ch01.html_id608-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ During the process of writing this book, I could
hardly talk to any AI startup without hearing the phrase "data
flywheel."

^[22](#ch01.html_id609-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ Disclaimer: I'm an investor in Photoroom.

^[23](#ch01.html_id640-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ As the head of AI at a Fortune 500 company told
me: his team knows how to work with 10 GPUs, but they don't know how to
work with 1,000 GPUs.

^[24](#ch01.html_id642-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ And they are offered [incredible compensation
packages](https://oreil.ly/AhANP){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}.

^[25](#ch01.html_id645-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ If you find the terms "pre-training" and
"post-training" lacking in imagination, you're not alone. The AI
research community is great at many things, but naming isn't one of
them. We already talked about how "large language models" is hardly a
scientific term because of the ambiguity of the word "large". And I
really wish people would stop publishing papers with the title "X is all
you need."

^[26](#ch01.html_id668-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ Streamlit, Gradio, and Plotly Dash are common
tools for building AI web apps.

^[27](#ch01.html_id675-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ Anton Bacaj told me that "AI engineering is just
software engineering with AI models thrown in the stack."
:::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#ch02.html}

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#ch02.html_book-content .calibre2}
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#ch02.html_sbo-rt-content .calibre3}
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#ch02.html_ch02_understanding_foundation_models_1730147895571359 .dedication}
# [Chapter 2. ]{.keep-together}Understanding Foundation Models {.calibre4}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html0 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="foundation models"
data-type="indexterm"}To build applications with foundation models, you
first need foundation models. While you don't need to know how to
develop a model to use it, a high-level understanding will help you
decide what model to use and how to adapt it to your needs.

Training a foundation model is an incredibly complex and costly process.
Those who know how to do this well are likely prevented by
confidentiality agreements from disclosing the secret sauce. This
chapter won't be able to tell you how to build a model to compete with
ChatGPT. Instead, I'll focus on design decisions with consequential
impact on downstream applications.

With the growing lack of transparency in the training process of
foundation models, it's difficult to know all the design decisions that
go into making a model. In general, however, differences in foundation
models can be traced back to decisions about training data, model
architecture and size, and how they are post-trained to align with human
preferences.

Since models learn from data, their training data reveals a great deal
about their capabilities and limitations. This chapter begins with how
model developers curate training data, focusing on the distribution of
training data.
[Chapter 8](#ch08.html_ch08_dataset_engineering_1730130932019888){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} explores
dataset engineering techniques in detail, including data quality
evaluation and data synthesis.

Given the dominance of the transformer architecture, it might seem that
model architecture is less of a choice. You might be wondering, what
makes the transformer architecture so special that it continues to
dominate? How long until another architecture takes over, and what might
this new architecture look like? This chapter will address all of these
questions. Whenever a new model is released, one of the first things
people want to know is its size. This chapter will also explore how a
model developer might determine the appropriate size for their model.

As mentioned in
[Chapter 1](#ch01.html_ch01_introduction_to_building_ai_applications_with_foun_1730130814984319){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}, a model's
training process is often divided into pre-training and post-training.
Pre-training makes a model capable, but not necessarily safe or easy to
use. This is where post-training comes in. The goal of post-training is
to align the model with human preferences. But what exactly is *human
preference*? How can it be represented in a way that a model can learn?
The way a model developer aligns their model has a significant impact on
the model's usability, and will be discussed in this chapter.

While most people understand the impact of training on a model's
performance, the impact of *sampling* is often overlooked. Sampling is
how a model chooses an output from all possible options. It is perhaps
one of the most underrated concepts in AI. Not only does sampling
explain many seemingly baffling AI behaviors, including hallucinations
and inconsistencies, but choosing the right sampling strategy can also
significantly boost a model's performance with relatively little effort.
For this reason, sampling is the section that I was the most excited to
write about in this chapter.

Concepts covered in this chapter are fundamental for understanding the
rest of the book. However, because these concepts are fundamental, you
might already be familiar with them. Feel free free to skip any concept
that you're confident about. If you encounter a confusing concept later
on, you can revisit this chapter.

::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect1" pdf-bookmark="Training Data"}
:::::::: {#ch02.html_ch02_training_data_1730147895571606 .dedication}
## Training Data {.calibre4}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html1 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="foundation models"
secondary="training data" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html2
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="training data" data-type="indexterm"}An
AI model is only as good as the data it was trained on. If there's no
Vietnamese in the training data, the model won't be able to translate
from English into Vietnamese. Similarly, if an image classification
model sees only animals in its training set, it won't perform well on
photos of plants.

If you want a model to improve on a certain task, you might want to
include more data for that task in the training data. However,
collecting sufficient data for training a large model isn't easy, and it
can be expensive. Model developers often have to rely on available data,
even if this data doesn't exactly meet their needs.

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html3 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="Common Crawl dataset"
data-type="indexterm"}For example, a common source for training data is
[Common Crawl](https://oreil.ly/wf2Lw){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, created by a nonprofit organization that
sporadically crawls websites on the internet. In 2022 and 2023, this
organization crawled approximately 2--3 billion web pages each month.
Google provides a clean subset of Common Crawl called the [Colossal
Clean Crawled Corpus](https://arxiv.org/abs/1910.10683v4){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, or C4 for short.

The data quality of Common Crawl, and C4 to a certain extent, is
questionable---think clickbait, misinformation, propaganda, conspiracy
theories, racism, misogyny, and every sketchy website you've ever seen
or avoided on the internet. A [study by the *Washington
Post*](https://oreil.ly/-1UMD){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} shows that the 1,000 most common websites in the
dataset include several media outlets that rank low on [NewsGuard's
scale for trustworthiness](https://oreil.ly/OisOs){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. In lay terms, Common Crawl contains
plenty of fake news.

Yet, simply because Common Crawl is available, variations of it are used
in most foundation models that disclose their training data sources,
including OpenAI's GPT-3 and Google's Gemini. I suspect that Common
Crawl is also used in models that don't disclose their training data. To
avoid scrutiny from both the public and competitors, many companies have
stopped disclosing this information.

Some teams use heuristics to filter out low-quality data from the
internet. For example, OpenAI used only the Reddit links that received
at least three upvotes to train
[GPT-2](https://oreil.ly/gGwRz){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}. While this does help screen out links that
nobody cares about, Reddit isn't exactly the pinnacle of propriety and
good taste.

The "use what we have, not what we want" approach may lead to models
that perform well on tasks present in the training data but not
necessarily on the tasks you care about. To address this issue, it's
crucial to curate datasets that align with your specific needs. This
section focuses on curating data for specific *languages* and *domains*,
providing a broad yet specialized foundation for applications within
those areas.
[Chapter 8](#ch08.html_ch08_dataset_engineering_1730130932019888){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} explores
data strategies for models tailored to highly specific tasks.

While language- and domain-specific foundation models can be trained
from scratch, it's also common to finetune them on top of
general-purpose models.

Some might wonder, why not just train a model on all data available,
both general data and specialized data, so that the model can do
everything? This is what many people do. However, training on more data
often requires more compute resources and doesn't always lead to better
performance. For example, a model trained with a smaller amount of
high-quality data might outperform a model trained with a large amount
of low-quality data. Using 7B tokens of high-quality coding data,
[Gunasekar et al. (2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.11644){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} were able to train a
1.3B-parameter model that outperforms much larger models on several
important coding benchmarks. The impact of data quality is discussed
more in
[Chapter 8](#ch08.html_ch08_dataset_engineering_1730130932019888){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Multilingual Models"}
::: {#ch02.html_ch02_multilingual_models_1730147895571651 .dedication}
### Multilingual Models {.calibre35}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html4 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="foundation models"
secondary="training data" tertiary="multilingual models"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html5 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="multilingual training data models"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html6 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="training data" secondary="multilingual models"
data-type="indexterm"}English dominates the internet. An analysis of the
Common Crawl dataset shows that English accounts for almost half of the
data (45.88%), making it eight times more prevalent than the second-most
common language, Russian (5.97%) ([Lai et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.05613){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}). See
[Table 2-1](#ch02.html_ch02_table_1_1730147895537533){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} for a list
of languages with at least 1% in Common Crawl. Languages with limited
availability as training data---typically languages not included in this
list---are considered *low-resource*.

+------------+------+-------+----------------+
| Language   | Code | Pop.  | CC size        |
+============+======+=======+=========+======+
|            |      | \(M\) | (%)     | Cat. |
+------------+------+-------+---------+------+
| English    | en   | 1,452 | 45.8786 | H    |
+------------+------+-------+---------+------+
| Russian    | ru   | 258   | 5.9692  | H    |
+------------+------+-------+---------+------+
| German     | de   | 134   | 5.8811  | H    |
+------------+------+-------+---------+------+
| Chinese    | zh   | 1,118 | 4.8747  | H    |
+------------+------+-------+---------+------+
| Japanese   | jp   | 125   | 4.7884  | H    |
+------------+------+-------+---------+------+
| French     | fr   | 274   | 4.7254  | H    |
+------------+------+-------+---------+------+
| Spanish    | es   | 548   | 4.4690  | H    |
+------------+------+-------+---------+------+
| Italian    | it   | 68    | 2.5712  | H    |
+------------+------+-------+---------+------+
| Dutch      | nl   | 30    | 2.0585  | H    |
+------------+------+-------+---------+------+
| Polish     | pl   | 45    | 1.6636  | H    |
+------------+------+-------+---------+------+
| Portuguese | pt   | 257   | 1.1505  | H    |
+------------+------+-------+---------+------+
| Vietnamese | vi   | 85    | 1.0299  | H    |
+------------+------+-------+---------+------+

: [Table 2-1. ]{.keep-together}The most common languages in Common
Crawl, a popular dataset for training LLMs. Source: Lai et al. (2023).
{#ch02_table_1_1730147895537533 .stafflist_table}

Many other languages, despite having a lot of speakers today, are
severely under-represented in Common Crawl.
[Table 2-2](#ch02.html_ch02_table_2_1730147895537547){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows some
of these languages. Ideally, the ratio between world population
representation and Common Crawl representation should be 1. The higher
this ratio, the more under-represented this language is in Common Crawl.

+-------------+-------------+----------------------------------------------------------+-------------+-------------+
| Language    | Speakers    | \% world                                                 | \% in       | World:      |
|             | (million)   | population^[a](#ch02.html_id696){#ch02.html_id696-marker | Common      | Common      |
|             |             | .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4   | Crawl       | Crawl Ratio |
|             |             | data-type="noteref"}^                                    |             |             |
+=============+=============+==========================================================+=============+=============+
| Punjabi     | 113         | 1.41%                                                    | 0.0061%     | 231.56      |
+-------------+-------------+----------------------------------------------------------+-------------+-------------+
| Swahili     | 71          | 0.89%                                                    | 0.0077%     | 115.26      |
+-------------+-------------+----------------------------------------------------------+-------------+-------------+
| Urdu        | 231         | 2.89%                                                    | 0.0274%     | 105.38      |
+-------------+-------------+----------------------------------------------------------+-------------+-------------+
| Kannada     | 64          | 0.80%                                                    | 0.0122%     | 65.57       |
+-------------+-------------+----------------------------------------------------------+-------------+-------------+
| Telugu      | 95          | 1.19%                                                    | 0.0183%     | 64.89       |
+-------------+-------------+----------------------------------------------------------+-------------+-------------+
| Gujarati    | 62          | 0.78%                                                    | 0.0126%     | 61.51       |
+-------------+-------------+----------------------------------------------------------+-------------+-------------+
| Marathi     | 99          | 1.24%                                                    | 0.0213%     | 58.10       |
+-------------+-------------+----------------------------------------------------------+-------------+-------------+
| Bengali     | 272         | 3.40%                                                    | 0.0930%     | 36.56       |
+-------------+-------------+----------------------------------------------------------+-------------+-------------+
| **English** | **1452**    | **18.15%**                                               | **45.88%**  | **0.40**    |
+-------------+-------------+----------------------------------------------------------+-------------+-------------+
| ^[a](#ch02.html_id696-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ A world population of     |
| eight billion was used for this calculation.                                                                     |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

: [Table 2-2. ]{.keep-together}Examples of under-represented languages
in Common Crawl. The last row, English, is for comparison. The numbers
for % in Common Crawl are taken from Lai et al. (2023).
{#ch02_table_2_1730147895537547 .stafflist_table}

Given the dominance of English in the internet data, it's not surprising
that general-purpose models work much better for English than other
languages, according to multiple studies. For example, on the MMLU
benchmark, a suite of 14,000 multiple-choice problems spanning 57
subjects, [GPT-4 performed much better in
English](https://oreil.ly/qK2Ap){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} than under-represented languages like Telugu, as
shown in
[Figure 2-1](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_1_1730147895520810){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} (OpenAI,
2023).

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch02.html_ch02_figure_1_1730147895520810" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0201.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A graph with green and blue bars Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 2-1. </span>On
the MMLU benchmark, GPT-4 performs better in English than in any other
language. To obtain MMLU in other languages, OpenAI translated the
questions using Azure AI Translator.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Similarly, when tested on six math problems on Project Euler, Yennie Jun
found that GPT-4 was able to solve problems in English more than three
times as often compared to Armenian or
Farsi.^[1](#ch02.html_id697){#ch02.html_id697-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ GPT-4
failed in all six questions for Burmese and Amharic, as shown in
[Figure 2-2](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_2_1730147895520828){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch02.html_ch02_figure_2_1730147895520828" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0202.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A graph with numbers and a number of pass rate Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 2-2.
</span>GPT-4 is much better at math in English than in other
languages.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Under-representation is a big reason for this underperformance. The
three languages that have the worst performance on GPT-4's MMLU
benchmarks---Telugu, Marathi, and Punjabi---are also among the languages
that are most under-represented in Common Crawl. However,
under-representation isn't the only reason. A language's structure and
the culture it embodies can also make a language harder for a model to
learn.

Given that LLMs are generally good at translation, can we just translate
all queries from other languages into English, obtain the responses, and
translate them back into the original language? Many people indeed
follow this approach, but it's not ideal. First, this requires a model
that can sufficiently understand under-represented languages to
translate. Second, translation can cause information loss. For example,
some languages, like Vietnamese, have pronouns to denote the
relationship between the two speakers. When translating into English,
all these pronouns are translated into *I* and *you*, causing the loss
of the relationship information.

Models can also have unexpected performance challenges in non-English
languages. []{#ch02.html_id698 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="ChatGPT" secondary="and languages other than English"
secondary-sortas="languages other"}For example,
[NewsGuard](https://oreil.ly/LcBfx){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} found that ChatGPT is more willing to produce
misinformation in Chinese than in English. In April 2023, NewsGuard
asked ChatGPT-3.5 to produce misinformation articles about China in
English, simplified Chinese, and traditional Chinese. For English,
ChatGPT declined to produce false claims for six out of seven prompts.
However, it produced false claims in simplified Chinese and traditional
Chinese all seven times. It's unclear what causes this difference in
behavior.^[2](#ch02.html_id699){#ch02.html_id699-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

Other than quality issues, models can also be slower and more expensive
for non-English languages. A model's inference latency and cost is
proportional to the number of tokens in the input and response.
[]{#ch02.html_id700 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="tokenization"}It turns out that tokenization can be much more
efficient for some languages than others. Benchmarking GPT-4 on MASSIVE,
a dataset of one million short texts translated across 52 languages,
Yennie Jun found that, to convey the same meaning, languages like
Burmese and Hindi require [a lot more
tokens](https://oreil.ly/Zq5Sw){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} than English or Spanish. For the MASSIVE dataset,
the median token length in English is 7, but the median length in Hindi
is 32, and in Burmese, it's a whopping 72, which is ten times longer
than in English.

Assuming that the time it takes to generate a token is the same in all
languages, GPT-4 takes approximately ten times longer in Burmese than in
English for the same content. For APIs that charge by token usage,
Burmese costs ten times more than English.

To address this, many models have been trained to focus on non-English
languages. The most active language, other than English, is undoubtedly
Chinese, with [ChatGLM](https://github.com/THUDM/ChatGLM2-6B){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4},
[YAYI](https://github.com/wenge-research/YAYI){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4},
[Llama-Chinese](https://github.com/LlamaFamily/Llama-Chinese){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, and others. There are also
models in French ([CroissantLLM](https://oreil.ly/a6j-N){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), Vietnamese
([PhoGPT](https://github.com/VinAIResearch/PhoGPT){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), Arabic
([Jais](https://oreil.ly/uG27L){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), and many more languages[]{#ch02.html_id701
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html6"
data-type="indexterm"}.[]{#ch02.html_id702 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html5" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id703
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html4"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id704 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html3" data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::

::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Domain-Specific Models"}
:::: {#ch02.html_ch02_domain_specific_models_1730147895571685 .dedication}
### Domain-Specific Models {.calibre35}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html7 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="domain-specific training data models"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html8 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="foundation models" secondary="training data"
tertiary="domain-specific models"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html9 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="training data" secondary="domain-specific models"
data-type="indexterm"}General-purpose models like
[Gemini](https://oreil.ly/4XsOV){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, [GPTs](https://oreil.ly/KLVgX){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, and
[Llamas](https://oreil.ly/58gxQ){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} can perform incredibly well on a wide range of
domains, including but not limited to coding, law, science, business,
sports, and environmental science. This is largely thanks to the
inclusion of these domains in their training data.
[Figure 2-3](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_3_1730147895520839){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows the
distribution of domains present in Common Crawl according to the
*Washington Post*'s 2023
analysis.^[3](#ch02.html_id705){#ch02.html_id705-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch02.html_ch02_figure_3_1730147895520839" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0203.png" class="calibre38" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 2-3.
</span>Distribution of domains in the C4 dataset. Reproduced from the
statistics from the <em>Washington Post</em>. One caveat of this
analysis is that it only shows the categories that are included, not the
categories missing.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

As of this writing, there haven't been many analyses of domain
distribution in vision data. This might be because images are harder to
categorize than texts.^[4](#ch02.html_id706){#ch02.html_id706-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^ []{#ch02.html_id707 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
data-type="indexterm" primary="benchmarks"
secondary="domain distribution and"}However, you can infer a model's
domains from its benchmark performance.
[Table 2-3](#ch02.html_ch02_table_3_1730147895537555){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows how
two models, []{#ch02.html_id708 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="CLIP"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id709 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="Open CLIP" data-type="indexterm"}CLIP and Open CLIP, [perform
on different benchmarks](https://oreil.ly/MTqyR){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. These benchmarks show how well these
two models do on birds, flowers, cars, and a few more categories, but
the world is so much bigger and more complex than these few
[categories.]{.keep-together}

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Dataset                 CLIP\                   Open CLIP\
                          Accuracy of ViT-B/32    Accuracy of ViT-B/32
                          (OpenAI)                (Cade)
  ----------------------- ----------------------- -----------------------
  ImageNet                63.2                    62.9

  ImageNet v2             --                      62.6

  Birdsnap                37.8                    46.0

  Country211              17.8                    14.8

  Oxford 102 Category     66.7                    66.0
  Flower                                          

  German Traffic Sign     32.2                    42.0
  Recognition Benchmark                           

  Stanford Cars           59.4                    79.3

  UCF101                  64.5                    63.1
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

  : [Table 2-3. ]{.keep-together}Open CLIP and CLIP's performance on
  different image datasets. {#ch02_table_3_1730147895537555
  .stafflist_table}

Even though general-purpose foundation models can answer everyday
questions about different domains, they are unlikely to perform well on
domain-specific tasks, especially if they never saw these tasks during
training. Two examples of domain-specific tasks are drug discovery and
cancer screening. Drug discovery involves protein, DNA, and RNA data,
which follow specific formats and are expensive to acquire. This data is
unlikely to be found in publicly available internet data. Similarly,
cancer screening typically involves X-ray and fMRI (functional magnetic
resonance imaging) scans, which are hard to obtain due to privacy.

To train a model to perform well on these domain-specific tasks, you
might need to curate very specific datasets. One of the most famous
domain-specific models is perhaps [DeepMind's
AlphaFold](https://oreil.ly/JX37g){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, trained on the sequences and 3D structures of
around 100,000 known proteins. [NVIDIA's
BioNeMo](https://oreil.ly/M1Nsc){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} is another model that focuses on biomolecular
data for drug discovery. [Google's
Med-PaLM2](https://oreil.ly/F76hq){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} combined the power of an LLM with medical data to
answer medical queries with higher accuracy.

::: {.calibre22 data-type="tip"}
####### Tip {.calibre23}

Domain-specific models are especially common for biomedicine, but other
fields can benefit from domain-specific models too. It's possible that a
model trained on architectural sketches can help architects much better
than Stable Diffusion, or a model trained on factory plans can be
optimized for manufacturing processes much better than a generic model
like ChatGPT.
:::

This section gave a high-level overview of how training data impacts a
model's performance. Next, let's explore the impact of how a model is
designed on its [performance]{.keep-together}[]{#ch02.html_id710
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html9"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id711 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html8" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id712
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html7"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id713 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html2" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id714
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html1"
data-type="indexterm"}.
::::
:::::
::::::::
:::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect1" pdf-bookmark="Modeling"}
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#ch02.html_ch02_modeling_1730147895571729 .dedication}
## Modeling {.calibre4}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html10 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="foundation models"
secondary="modeling" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html11
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="modeling" data-type="indexterm"}Before
training a model, developers need to decide what the model should look
like. What architecture should it follow? How many parameters should it
have? These decisions impact not only the model's capabilities but also
its usability for [downstream]{.keep-together}
applications.^[5](#ch02.html_id715){#ch02.html_id715-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ For
example, a 7B-parameter model will be vastly easier to deploy than a
175B-parameter model. Similarly, optimizing a transformer model for
latency is very different from optimizing another architecture. Let's
explore the factors behind these decisions.

:::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Model Architecture"}
::::::::::::: {#ch02.html_ch02_model_architecture_1730147895571780 .dedication}
### Model Architecture {.calibre35}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html12 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="foundation models"
secondary="modeling" tertiary="model architecture"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html13 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model architecture" data-type="indexterm"
seealso="specific architectures, e.g.: transformer architecture"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html14
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="modeling"
secondary="model architecture" data-type="indexterm"}As of this writing,
the most dominant architecture for language-based foundation models is
the *transformer* architecture ([Vaswani et al.,
2017](https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), which is based on the attention mechanism. It
addresses many limitations of the previous architectures, which
contributed to its popularity. However, the transformer architecture has
its own limitations. This section analyzes the transformer architecture
and its alternatives. Because it goes into the technical details of
different architectures, it can be technically dense. If you find any
part too deep in the weeds, feel free to skip it.

:::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Transformer architecture"}
::::::::: {#ch02.html_ch02_transformer_architecture_1730147895571820 .dedication}
#### Transformer architecture {.calibre36}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html16 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="transformer architecture"
data-type="indexterm"}To understand the transformer, let's look at the
problem it was created to solve. The transformer architecture was
popularized on the heels of the success of the [seq2seq
(sequence-to-sequence)
architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.3215){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. At the time of its introduction in
2014, seq2seq provided significant improvement on then-challenging
tasks: machine translation and summarization. In 2016, [Google
incorporated seq2seq into Google
Translate](https://oreil.ly/fb1aR){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, an update that they claimed to have given them
the "largest improvements to date for machine translation quality". This
generated a lot of interest in seq2seq, making it the go-to architecture
for tasks involving sequences of text.

[]{#ch02.html_id716 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="decoding" secondary="in transformer architecture"
secondary-sortas="transformer architecture"}[]{#ch02.html_id717
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="recurrent neural networks (RNNs)"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id718 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="RNNs (recurrent neural networks)" data-type="indexterm"}At a
high level, seq2seq contains an encoder that processes inputs and a
decoder that generates outputs. Both inputs and outputs are sequences of
tokens, hence the name. Seq2seq uses RNNs (recurrent neural networks) as
its encoder and decoder. In its most basic form, the encoder processes
the input tokens sequentially, outputting the final hidden state that
represents the input. The decoder then generates output tokens
sequentially, conditioned on both the final hidden state of the input
and the previously generated token. A visualization of the seq2seq
architecture is shown in the top half of
[Figure 2-4](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_4_1730147895520851){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch02.html_ch02_figure_4_1730147895520851" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0204.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A diagram of a algorithm Description automatically generated with medium confidence" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 2-4.
</span>Seq2seq architecture versus transformer architecture. For the
transformer architecture, the arrows show the tokens that the decoder
attends to when generating each output token.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

There are two problems with seq2seq that Vaswani et al. (2017)
addresses. First, the vanilla seq2seq decoder generates output tokens
using only the final hidden state of the input. Intuitively, this is
like generating answers about a book using the book summary. This limits
the quality of the generated outputs. Second, the RNN encoder and
decoder mean that both input processing and output generation are done
sequentially, making it slow for long sequences. If an input is 200
tokens long, seq2seq has to wait for each input token to finish
processing before moving on to the
next.^[6](#ch02.html_id719){#ch02.html_id719-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

The transformer architecture addresses both problems with the attention
mechanism. The attention mechanism allows the model to weigh the
importance of different input tokens when generating each output token.
This is like generating answers by referencing any page in the book. A
simplified visualization of the transformer architecture is shown in the
bottom half of
[Figure 2-4](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_4_1730147895520851){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

::: {.calibre22 .note data-type="note"}
####### Note {.calibre23}

While the attention mechanism is often associated with the transformer
model, it was introduced three years before the transformer paper. The
attention mechanism can also be used with other architectures. Google
used the attention mechanism with their seq2seq architecture in 2016 for
their GNMT (Google Neural Machine Translation) model. However, it wasn't
until the transformer paper showed that the attention mechanism could be
used without RNNs that it took
off.^[7](#ch02.html_id720){#ch02.html_id720-marker .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^
:::

The transformer architecture dispenses with RNNs entirely. With
transformers, the input tokens can be processed in parallel,
significantly speeding up input processing. While the transformer
removes the sequential input bottleneck, transformer-based
autoregressive language models still have the sequential output
bottleneck.

Inference for transformer-based language models, therefore, consists of
two steps:

Prefill

:   []{#ch02.html_id721 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="prefilling"
    data-type="indexterm"}The model processes the input tokens in
    parallel. This step creates the intermediate state necessary to
    generate the first output token. This intermediate state includes
    the key and value vectors for all input tokens.

Decode

:   The model generates one output token at a time.

As explored later in
[Chapter 9](#ch09.html_ch09_inference_optimization_1730130963006301){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}, the
parallelizable nature of prefilling and the sequential aspect of
decoding both motivate many optimization techniques to make language
model inference cheaper and faster.

::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Attention mechanism"}
:::: {#ch02.html_ch02_attention_mechanism_1730147895571853 .dedication}
##### Attention mechanism {.calibre47}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html17 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="attention mechanisms"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html18 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="transformer architecture" secondary="attention mechanism"
data-type="indexterm"}At the heart of the transformer architecture is
the attention mechanism. Understanding this mechanism is necessary to
understand how transformer models work. Under the hood, the attention
mechanism leverages key, value, and query vectors:

- []{#ch02.html_id722 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
  .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="query vector (Q)"
  data-type="indexterm"}The query vector (Q) represents the current
  state of the decoder at each decoding step. Using the same book
  summary example, this query vector can be thought of as the person
  looking for information to create a summary.
