- []{#ch02.html_id723 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
  .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="key vector (K)"
  data-type="indexterm"}Each key vector (K) represents a previous token.
  If each previous token is a page in the book, each key vector is like
  the page number. Note that at a given decoding step, previous tokens
  include both input tokens and previously generated tokens.

- []{#ch02.html_id724 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
  .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="value vector (V)"
  data-type="indexterm"}Each value vector (V) represents the actual
  value of a previous token, as learned by the model. Each value vector
  is like the page's content.

[]{#ch02.html_id725 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="dot products"
data-type="indexterm"}The attention mechanism computes how much
attention to give an input token by performing a [*dot
product*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dot_product){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} between the query vector
and its key vector. A high score means that the model will use more of
that page's content (its value vector) when generating the book's
summary. A visualization of the attention mechanism with the key, value,
and query vectors is shown in
[Figure 2-5](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_5_1730147895520861){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}. In this
visualization, the query vector is seeking information from the previous
tokens `How, are, you, ?, ¿`{.calibre26} to generate the next token.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch02.html_ch02_figure_5_1730147895520861" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0205.png" class="calibre38" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 2-5. </span>An
example of the attention mechanism in action next to its high-level
visualization from the famous transformer paper, “Attention Is All You
Need” (Vaswani et al., 2017).</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Because each previous token has a corresponding key and value vector,
the longer [the sequence]{.keep-together}, the more key and value
vectors need to be computed and stored. This [is one]{.keep-together}
reason why it's so hard to extend context length for transformer models.
How to efficiently compute and store key and value vectors comes up
again in Chapters [7](#ch07.html_ch07){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref" xrefstyle="select:labelnumber"}
and
[9](#ch09.html_ch09_inference_optimization_1730130963006301){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"
xrefstyle="select:labelnumber"}.

Let's look into how the attention function works. Given an input
`x`{.calibre26}, the key, value, and query vectors are computed by
applying key, value, and query matrices to the input. Let
`W`{.calibre26}~K~`, W`{.calibre26}~V~`, and W`{.calibre26}~Q~ be the
key, value, and query matrices. The key, value, and query vectors are
computed as follows:

``` {.calibre51 data-type="programlisting"}

K = xWK
V = xWV
Q = xWQ
```

The query, key, and value matrices have dimensions corresponding to the
model's hidden dimension. []{#ch02.html_id726 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
data-type="indexterm" primary="Llama" secondary="attention function"}For
example, in Llama 2-7B ([Touvron et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.09288){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), the model's hidden dimension size is 4096,
meaning that each of these matrices has a
`4096 `{.calibre26}×` 4096`{.calibre26} dimension. Each resulting
`K`{.calibre26}, `V`{.calibre26}, `Q`{.calibre26} vector has the
dimension of
`4096`{.calibre26}.^[8](#ch02.html_id727){#ch02.html_id727-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^

The attention mechanism is almost always multi-headed. Multiple heads
allow the model to attend to different groups of previous tokens
simultaneously. With multi-headed attention, the query, key, and value
vectors are split into smaller vectors, each corresponding to an
attention head. In the case of Llama 2-7B, because it has
`32`{.calibre26} attention heads, each `K`{.calibre26}, `V`{.calibre26},
and `Q`{.calibre26} vector will be split into `32`{.calibre26} vectors
of the dimension `128`{.calibre26}. This is because
`4096 / 32 = 128`{.calibre26}.

::: {.calibre52 data-type="equation"}
$\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = {softmax}\left( \frac{QK^{T}}{\sqrt{d}} \right)V$
:::

The outputs of all attention heads are then concatenated. An output
projection matrix is used to apply another transformation to this
concatenated output before it's fed to the model's next computation
step. The output projection matrix has the same dimension as the model's
hidden dimension.[]{#ch02.html_id728 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html18" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id729
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html17"
data-type="indexterm"}
::::
:::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Transformer block"}
::: {#ch02.html_ch02_transformer_block_1730147895571882 .dedication}
##### Transformer block {.calibre47}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html19 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="transformer architecture"
secondary="transformer blocks" data-type="indexterm"}Now that we've
discussed how attention works, let's see how it's used in a model. A
transformer architecture is composed of multiple transformer blocks. The
exact content of the block varies between models, but, in general, each
transformer block contains the attention module and the MLP (multi-layer
perceptron) module:

Attention module

:   []{#ch02.html_id730 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="attention mechanisms"
    secondary="attention modules"
    data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id731 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
    primary="attention modules"
    data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id732 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
    primary="transformer architecture" secondary="attention mechanism"
    tertiary="attention modules"
    data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id733 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
    primary="transformer architecture" secondary="transformer blocks"
    tertiary="attention modules" data-type="indexterm"}Each attention
    module consists of four weight matrices: query, key, value, and
    output projection.

MLP module

:   []{#ch02.html_id734 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="attention mechanisms"
    secondary="MLP modules" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id735
    .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
    contenteditable="false" primary="MLP modules"
    data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id736 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
    primary="transformer architecture" secondary="attention mechanism"
    tertiary="MLP modules" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id737
    .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
    contenteditable="false" primary="transformer architecture"
    secondary="transformer blocks" tertiary="MLP modules"
    data-type="indexterm"}An MLP module consists of linear layers
    separated by *nonlinear activation functions*. Each linear layer is
    a weight matrix that is used for linear transformations, whereas an
    activation function allows the linear layers to learn nonlinear
    patterns. []{#ch02.html_id738 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
    .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
    primary="feedforward layer"}A linear layer is also called a
    feedforward layer.

:   Common nonlinear functions are ReLU, Rectified Linear Unit ([Agarap,
    2018](https://arxiv.org/abs/1803.08375){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), and GELU ([Hendrycks and Gimpel,
    2016](https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.08415){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), which was used by GPT-2 and
    GPT-3, respectively. Action functions are very
    simple.^[9](#ch02.html_id739){#ch02.html_id739-marker .pcalibre7
    .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^
    For example, all ReLU does is convert negative values to 0.
    Mathematically, it's written as:

    ::: {.calibre52 data-type="equation"}
    ReLU(x) = max(0, x)
    :::

The number of transformer blocks in a transformer model is often
referred to as that model's number of layers. A transformer-based
language model is also outfitted with a module before and after all the
transformer blocks:

An embedding module before the transformer blocks

:   []{#ch02.html_id740 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
    primary="transformer architecture" secondary="transformer blocks"
    tertiary="embedding modules" data-type="indexterm"}This module
    consists of the embedding matrix and the positional embedding
    matrix, which convert tokens and their positions into embedding
    vectors, respectively. Naively, the number of position indices
    determines the model's maximum context length. For example, if a
    model keeps track of 2,048 positions, its maximum context length is
    2,048. However, there are techniques that increase a model's context
    length without increasing the number of position indices.

An output layer after the transformer blocks

:   []{#ch02.html_id741 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
    primary="transformer architecture" secondary="transformer blocks"
    tertiary="output layers" data-type="indexterm"}This module maps the
    model's output vectors into token probabilities used to sample model
    outputs (discussed in
    ["Sampling"](#ch02.html_ch02_sampling_1730147895572256){.pcalibre7
    .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}). This
    module typically consists of one matrix, which is also called the
    *unembedding layer*. Some people refer to the output layer as the
    model *head*, as it's the model's last layer before output
    generation.

[Figure 2-6](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_6_1730147895520869){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} visualizes
a transformer model architecture. The size of a transformer model is
determined by the dimensions of its building blocks. Some of the key
values are:

- The model's dimension determines the sizes of the key, query, value,
  and output projection matrices in the transformer block.

- The number of transformer blocks.

- The dimension of the feedforward layer.

- The vocabulary size.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch02.html_ch02_figure_6_1730147895520869" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0206.png" class="calibre38" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 2-6. </span>A
visualization of the weight composition of a transformer model.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Larger dimension values result in larger model sizes.
[Table 2-4](#ch02.html_ch02_table_4_1730147895537562){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows
these dimension values for different Llama 2 ([Touvron et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.09288){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}) and Llama 3 ([Dubey et al.,
2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.21783){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}) models. Note that while the increased context
length impacts the model's memory footprint, it doesn't impact the
model's total number of []{#ch02.html_id742 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html19"
data-type="indexterm"}parameters.[]{#ch02.html_id743 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="" startref="ch02.html16" data-type="indexterm"}

  Model          \# transformer blocks   Model dim   Feedforward dim   Vocab size   Context length
  -------------- ----------------------- ----------- ----------------- ------------ ----------------
  Llama 2-7B     32                      4,096       11,008            32K          4K
  Llama 2-13B    40                      5,120       13,824            32K          4K
  Llama 2-70B    80                      8,192       22,016            32K          4K
  Llama 3-7B     32                      4,096       14,336            128K         128K
  Llama 3-70B    80                      8,192       28,672            128K         128K
  Llama 3-405B   126                     16,384      53,248            128K         128K

  : [Table 2-4. ]{.keep-together}The dimension values of different Llama
  models. {#ch02_table_4_1730147895537562 .stafflist_table}
:::
::::
:::::::::
::::::::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Other model architectures"}
::: {#ch02.html_ch02_other_model_architectures_1730147895571910 .dedication}
#### Other model architectures {.calibre36}

While the transformer model dominates the landscape, it's not the only
architecture. Since [AlexNet](https://oreil.ly/1spG5){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} revived the interest in
deep learning in 2012, many architectures have gone in and out of
fashion. Seq2seq was in the limelight for four years (2014--2018).
[GANs](https://arxiv.org/abs/1406.2661){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} (generative adversarial networks) captured the
collective imagination a bit longer (2014--2019). Compared to
architectures that came before it, the transformer is sticky. It's been
around since 2017.^[10](#ch02.html_id744){#ch02.html_id744-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^ How long until something better comes along?

Developing a new architecture to outperform transformers isn't
easy.^[11](#ch02.html_id745){#ch02.html_id745-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ The
transformer has been heavily optimized since 2017. A new architecture
that aims to replace the transformer will have to perform at the scale
that people care about, on the hardware that people care
about.^[12](#ch02.html_id746){#ch02.html_id746-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

However, there's hope. While transformer-based models are dominating, as
of this writing, several alternative architectures are gaining traction.

One popular model is
[RWKV](https://github.com/BlinkDL/RWKV-LM){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (Peng et al., 2023), an RNN-based
model that can be parallelized for training. Due to its RNN nature, in
theory, it doesn't have the same context length limitation that
transformer-based models have. However, in practice, having no context
length limitation doesn't guarantee good performance with long context.

Modeling long sequences remains a core challenge in developing LLMs. An
architecture that has shown a lot of promise in long-range memory is
SSMs (state space models) ([Gu et al.,
2021a](https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.13985){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}). Since the architecture's
introduction in 2021, multiple techniques have been introduced to make
the architecture more efficient, better at long sequence processing, and
scalable to larger model sizes. Here are a few of these techniques, to
illustrate the evolution of a new architecture:

- []{#ch02.html_id747 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
  .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="S4 architecture"
  data-type="indexterm"}*S4*, introduced in "Efficiently Modeling Long
  Sequences with Structured State Spaces" ([Gu et al.,
  2021b](https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.00396){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
  .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), was developed to make SSMs more
  efficient.

- []{#ch02.html_id748 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
  .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="H3 architecture"
  data-type="indexterm"}*H3*, introduced in "Hungry Hungry Hippos:
  Towards Language Modeling with State Space Models" ([Fu et al.,
  2022](https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.14052){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
  .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), incorporates a mechanism that
  allows the model to recall early tokens and compare tokens across
  sequences. This mechanism's purpose is akin to that of the attention
  mechanism in the transformer architecture, but it is more efficient.

- []{#ch02.html_id749 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
  .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="Mamba architecture"
  data-type="indexterm"}*Mamba*, introduced in "Mamba: Linear-Time
  Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces" ([Gu and Dao,
  2023](https://oreil.ly/n7wYO){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
  .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), scales SSMs to three billion parameters. On
  language modeling, Mamba-3B outperforms transformers of the same size
  and matches transformers twice its size. The authors also show that
  Mamba's inference computation scales linearly with sequence length
  (compared to quadratic scaling for transformers). Its performance
  shows improvement on real data up to million-length sequences.

- []{#ch02.html_id750 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
  .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="Jamba architecture"
  data-type="indexterm"}*Jamba*, introduced in "Jamba: A Hybrid
  Transformer--Mamba Language Model" ([Lieber et al.,
  2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.19887){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
  .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), interleaves blocks of transformer
  and Mamba layers to scale up SSMs even further. The authors released a
  mixture-of-experts model with [52B total available
  parameters](https://oreil.ly/uyiBH){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
  .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (12B active parameters) designed to fit in a
  single 80 GB GPU. Jamba shows strong performance on standard language
  model benchmarks and long-context evaluations for up to a context
  length of 256K tokens. It also has a small memory footprint compared
  to vanilla transformers.

[Figure 2-7](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_7_1730147895520878){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} visualizes
the transformer, Mamba, and Jamba blocks.

While it's challenging to develop an architecture that outperforms the
transformer, given its many limitations, there are a lot of incentives
to do so. If another architecture does indeed overtake the transformer,
some of the model adaptation techniques discussed in this book might
change. However, just as the shift from ML engineering to AI engineering
has kept many things unchanged, changing the underlying model
architecture won't alter the fundamental approaches.[]{#ch02.html_id751
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html14"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id752 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html13" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id753
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html12"
data-type="indexterm"}

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch02.html_ch02_figure_7_1730147895520878" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0207.png" class="calibre38" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 2-7. </span>A
visualization of the transformer, Mamba, and Jamba layers. Image adapted
from “Jamba: A Hybrid Transformer–Mamba Language Model” (Lieber et al.,
2024).</h6>
</div>
</figure>
:::
::::
:::::::::::::
::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Model Size"}
:::::::::::::::: {#ch02.html_ch02_model_size_1730147895571946 .dedication}
### Model Size {.calibre35}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html23 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="foundation models"
secondary="modeling" tertiary="model size"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html24 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model size" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html25
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="modeling" secondary="model size"
data-type="indexterm"}Much of AI progress in recent years can be
attributed to increased model size. It's hard to talk about foundation
models without talking about their number of parameters. The number of
parameters is usually appended at the end of a model name. For example,
Llama-13B refers to the version of Llama, a model family developed by
Meta, with 13 billion parameters.

In general, increasing a model's parameters increases its capacity to
learn, resulting in better models. Given two models of the same model
family, the one with 13 billion parameters is likely to perform much
better than the one with 7 billion parameters.

::: {.calibre22 .note data-type="note"}
####### Note {.calibre23}

As the community better understands how to train large models,
newer-generation models tend to outperform older-generation models of
the same size. For example, [Llama 3-8B
(2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.21783){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} outperforms even [Llama 2-70B
(2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.09288){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} on the MMLU benchmark.
:::

The number of parameters helps us estimate the compute resources needed
to train and run this model. For example, if a model has 7 billion
parameters, and each parameter is stored using 2 bytes (16 bits), then
we can calculate that the GPU memory needed to do inference using this
model will be at least 14 billion bytes (14
GB).^[13](#ch02.html_id754){#ch02.html_id754-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

[]{#ch02.html_id755 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="sparse models"}The number of parameters can be misleading if
the model is *sparse*. A sparse model has a large percentage of
zero-value parameters. A 7B-parameter model that is 90% sparse only has
700 million non-zero parameters. Sparsity allows for more efficient data
storage and computation. This means that a large sparse model can
require less compute than a small dense model.

[]{#ch02.html_id756 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="mixture-of-experts (MoE) models"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id757 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="tokens" data-type="indexterm"}A type of sparse model that has
gained popularity in recent years is mixture-of-experts (MoE) ([Shazeer
et al., 2017](https://arxiv.org/abs/1701.06538){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}). An MoE model is divided into
different groups of parameters, and each group is an *expert*. Only a
subset of the experts is *active* for (used to) process each token.

For example, [Mixtral 8x7B](https://oreil.ly/VvXbu){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} is a mixture of eight
experts, each expert with seven billion parameters. If no two experts
share any parameter, it should have 8 × 7 billion = 56 billion
parameters. However, due to some parameters being shared, it has only
46.7 billion parameters.

At each layer, for each token, only two experts are active. This means
that only 12.9 billion parameters are active for each token. While this
model has 46.7 billion parameters, its cost and speed are the same as a
12.9-billion-parameter model.

A larger model can also underperform a smaller model if it's not trained
on enough data. Imagine a 13B-param model trained on a dataset
consisting of a single sentence: "I like pineapples." This model will
perform much worse than a much smaller model trained on more data.

When discussing model size, it's important to consider the size of the
data it was trained on. For most models, dataset sizes are measured by
the number of training samples. For example, Google's Flamingo ([Alayrac
et al., 2022](https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.14198){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}) was trained using four datasets---one
of them has 1.8 billion (image, text) pairs and one has 312 million
(image, text) pairs.

For language models, a training sample can be a sentence, a Wikipedia
page, a chat conversation, or a book. A book is worth a lot more than a
sentence, so the number of training samples is no longer a good metric
to measure dataset sizes. A better measurement is the number of tokens
in the dataset.

[]{#ch02.html_id758 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="tokenization"}The number of tokens isn't a perfect measurement
either, as different models can have different tokenization processes,
resulting in the same dataset having different numbers of tokens for
different models. Why not just use the number of words or the number of
letters? Because a token is the unit that a model operates on, knowing
the number of tokens in a dataset helps us measure how much a model can
potentially learn from that data.

As of this writing, LLMs are trained using datasets in the order of
trillions of tokens. Meta used increasingly larger datasets to train
their Llama models:

- 1.4 trillion tokens for [Llama
  1](https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.13971){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
  .calibre10 .pcalibre4}

- 2 trillion tokens for [Llama
  2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.09288){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
  .calibre10 .pcalibre4}

- 15 trillion tokens for [Llama 3](https://oreil.ly/vfSQw){.pcalibre7
  .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}

Together's open source dataset RedPajama-v2 has [30 trillion
tokens](https://oreil.ly/SfB4g){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}. This is equivalent to 450 million
books^[14](#ch02.html_id759){#ch02.html_id759-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ or
5,400 times the size of Wikipedia. However, since RedPajama-v2 consists
of indiscriminate content, the amount of high-quality data is much
lower.

*The number of tokens in a model's dataset isn't the same as its number
of training tokens.* The number of training tokens measures the tokens
that the model is trained on. If a dataset contains 1 trillion tokens
and a model is trained on that dataset for two epochs---an *epoch* is a
pass through the dataset---the number of training tokens is 2
trillion.^[15](#ch02.html_id760){#ch02.html_id760-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ See
[Table 2-5](#ch02.html_ch02_table_5_1730147895537573){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} for
examples of the number of training tokens for models with different
numbers of parameters.

  Model                              Size (# parameters)   Training tokens
  ---------------------------------- --------------------- -----------------
  LaMDA (Thoppilan et al., 2022)     137 billion           168 billion
  GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020)         175 billion           300 billion
  Jurassic (Lieber et al., 2021)     178 billion           300 billion
  Gopher (Rae et al., 2021)          280 billion           300 billion
  MT-NLG 530B (Smith et al., 2022)   530 billion           270 billion
  Chinchilla                         70 billion            1.4 trillion

  : [Table 2-5. ]{.keep-together}Examples of the number of training
  tokens for models with different numbers of parameters. Source:
  "Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models" ([DeepMind,
  2022](https://oreil.ly/A3K90){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
  .calibre10 .pcalibre4}). {#ch02_table_5_1730147895537573
  .stafflist_table}

::: {.calibre22 .note data-type="note"}
####### Note {.calibre23}

While this section focuses on the scale of data, quantity isn't the only
thing that matters. Data quality and data diversity matter, too.
Quantity, quality, and diversity are the three golden goals for training
data. They are discussed further in
[Chapter 8](#ch08.html_ch08_dataset_engineering_1730130932019888){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.
:::

[]{#ch02.html_id761 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="FLOP (floating point operation)"
data-type="indexterm"}Pre-training large models requires compute. One
way to measure the amount of compute needed is by considering the number
of machines, e.g., GPUs, CPUs, and TPUs. However, different machines
have very different capacities and costs. An [NVIDIA]{.keep-together}
A10 GPU is different from an NVIDIA H100 GPU and an Intel Core Ultra
[Processor.]{.keep-together}

A more standardized unit for a model's compute requirement is *FLOP*, or
*floating point operation*. FLOP measures the number of floating point
operations performed for a certain task. Google's largest PaLM-2 model,
for example, was trained using `10`{.calibre26}^22^ FLOPs ([Chowdhery et
al., 2022](https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.02311){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}). GPT-3-175B was trained using
`3.14 × 10`{.calibre26}^23^ FLOPs ([Brown et al.,
2020](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}).

*The plural form of FLOP, FLOPs, is often confused with FLOP/s, floating
point operations per Second.* FLOPs measure the compute requirement for
a task, whereas FLOP/s measures a machine's peak performance. For
example, an NVIDIA H100 NVL GPU can deliver a maximum of [60
TeraFLOP/s](https://oreil.ly/HcFYz){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}: `6 × 10`{.calibre26}^13^ FLOPs a second or
`5.2 × 10`{.calibre26}^18^ FLOPs a
day.^[16](#ch02.html_id762){#ch02.html_id762-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

::: {.calibre22 .warning data-type="warning"}
####### Warning {.calibre29}

Be alert for confusing notations. FLOP/s is often written as FLOPS,
which looks similar to FLOPs. To avoid this confusion, some companies,
including OpenAI, use FLOP/s-day in place of FLOPs to measure compute
requirements:

``` {.calibre51 data-type="programlisting"}

1 FLOP/s-day = 60 × 60 × 24 = 86,400 FLOPs
```

This book uses FLOPs for counting floating point operations and FLOP/s
for FLOPs per second.
:::

Assume that you have 256 H100s. If you can use them at their maximum
capacity and make no training mistakes, it'd take you
`(3.14 × 10`{.calibre26}^`23`{.calibre26}^`) / (256 × 5.2 × 10`{.calibre26}^`18`{.calibre26}^`) = ~236 days`{.calibre26},
or approximately 7.8 months, to train GPT-3-175B.

However, it's unlikely you can use your machines at their peak capacity
all the time. Utilization measures how much of the maximum compute
capacity you can use. What's considered good utilization depends on the
model, the workload, and the hardware. Generally, if you can get half
the advertised performance, 50% utilization, you're doing okay. Anything
above 70% utilization is considered great. Don't let this rule stop you
from getting even higher utilization.
[Chapter 9](#ch09.html_ch09_inference_optimization_1730130963006301){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} discusses
hardware metrics and utilization in more detail.

At 70% utilization and \$2/h for one
H100,^[17](#ch02.html_id763){#ch02.html_id763-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^
training GPT-3-175B would cost over \$4 million:

``` {.calibre51 data-type="programlisting"}

$2/H100/hour × 256 H100 × 24 hours × 256 days / 0.7 = $4,142,811.43
```

::: {.calibre22 data-type="tip"}
####### Tip {.calibre23}

In summary, three numbers signal a model's scale:

- Number of parameters, which is a proxy for the model's learning
  capacity.

- Number of tokens a model was trained on, which is a proxy for how much
  a model learned.

- Number of FLOPs, which is a proxy for the training cost.
:::

<aside data-type="sidebar" epub:type="sidebar" class="calibre20">

::: {#ch02.html_ch02_inverse_scaling_1730147895571978 .sidebar}
## Inverse Scaling {.calibre21}

[]{#ch02.html_id764 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="foundation models"
secondary="inverse scaling" data-type="indexterm"}We've assumed that
bigger models are better. Are there scenarios for which bigger models
perform worse? []{#ch02.html_id765 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="Anthropic"
secondary="inverse scaling and alignment training"}In 2022, Anthropic
discovered that, counterintuitively, more alignment training (discussed
in
["Post-Training"](#ch02.html_ch02_post_training_1730147895572108){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}) leads to
models that align less with human preference ([Perez et al.,
2022](https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.09251){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}). According to their paper, models trained to be
more aligned "are much more likely to express specific political views
(pro-gun rights and immigration) and religious views (Buddhist),
self-reported conscious experience and moral self-worth, and a desire to
not be shut down."

In 2023, a group of researchers, mostly from New York University,
launched the [Inverse Scaling
Prize](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.09479){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} to find tasks where larger language
models perform worse. They offered \$5,000 for each third prize,
\$20,000 for each second prize, and \$100,000 for one first prize. They
received a total of 99 submissions, of which 11 were awarded third
prizes. They found that larger language models are sometimes (only
sometimes) worse on tasks that require memorization and tasks with
strong priors. However, they didn't award any second or first prizes
because even though the submitted tasks show failures for a small test
set, none demonstrated failures in the real world.
:::

</aside>

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Scaling law: Building compute-optimal models"}
::: {#ch02.html_ch02_scaling_law_building_compute_optimal_models_1730147895572004 .dedication}
#### Scaling law: Building compute-optimal models {.calibre36}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html26 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="compute-optimal models"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html27 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model size"
secondary="scaling law: building compute-optimal models"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html28 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="scaling law" data-type="indexterm"}I hope that the last section
has convinced you of three things:

1.  Model performance depends on the model size and the dataset size.

2.  Bigger models and bigger datasets require more compute.

3.  Compute costs money.

Unless you have unlimited money, budgeting is essential. You don't want
to start with an arbitrarily large model size and see how much it would
cost. You start with a budget---how much money you want to spend---and
work out the best model performance you can afford. As compute is often
the limiting factor---compute infrastructure is not only expensive but
also hard to set up---teams often start with a compute budget. Given a
fixed amount of FLOPs, what model size and dataset size would give the
best performance? A model that can achieve the best performance given a
fixed compute budget is *compute-optional*.

[]{#ch02.html_id766 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="Chinchilla scaling law"
data-type="indexterm"}Given a compute budget, the rule that helps
calculate the optimal model size and dataset size is called the
Chinchilla *scaling law*, proposed in the Chinchilla paper ["Training
Compute-Optimal Large Language
Models"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.15556){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (DeepMind, 2022). To study the
relationship between model size, dataset size, compute budget, and model
performance, the authors trained 400 language models ranging from 70
million to over 16 billion parameters on 5 to 500 billion tokens.
[]{#ch02.html_id767 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="compute-optimal training"}They found that for compute-optimal
training, you need the number of training tokens to be approximately 20
times the model size. This means that a 3B-parameter model needs
approximately 60B training tokens. The model size and the number of
training tokens should be scaled equally: for every doubling of the
model size, the number of training tokens should also be doubled.

We've come a long way from when the training process was treated like
alchemy.
[Figure 2-8](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_8_1730147895520888){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows that
we can predict not only the optimal number of parameters and tokens for
each FLOP budget but also the expected training loss from these settings
(assuming we do things right).

This compute-optimal calculation assumes that the cost of acquiring data
is much cheaper than the cost of compute. The same Chinchilla paper
proposes another calculation for when the cost of training data is
nontrivial.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch02.html_ch02_figure_8_1730147895520888" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0208.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A graph with dots and lines Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 2-8.
</span>Graphs that depict the relationships between training loss, a
model’s number of parameters, FLOPs, and number of training tokens.
Source: “Training Compute-Optional Large Language Models” (DeepMind,
2022).</h6>
</div>
</figure>

The scaling law was developed for dense models trained on predominantly
human-generated data. Adapting this calculation for sparse models, such
as mixture-of-expert models, and synthetic data is an active research
area.

The scaling law optimizes model quality given a compute budget. However,
it's important to remember that for production, model quality isn't
everything. []{#ch02.html_id768 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="Llama" secondary="scaling law and"}Some models, most notably
Llama, have suboptimal performance but better usability. Given their
compute budget, Llama authors could've chosen bigger models that would
perform better, but they opted for smaller models. Smaller models are
easier to work with and cheaper to run inference on, which helped their
models gain wider adoption. [Sardana et al.
(2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.00448){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} modified the Chinchilla scaling law to
calculate the optimal LLM parameter count and pre-training data size to
account for this inference demand.

On the topic of model performance given a compute budget, it's worth
noting that the cost of achieving a given model performance is
decreasing. For example, on the ImageNet dataset, the cost to achieve
93% accuracy halved from 2019 to 2021, according to the [*Artificial
Intelligence Index Report 2022* (Stanford University
HAI)](https://oreil.ly/oq-LE){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}.

*While the cost for the same model performance is decreasing, the cost
for model performance improvement remains high.* Similar to the last
mile challenge discussed in
[Chapter 1](#ch01.html_ch01_introduction_to_building_ai_applications_with_foun_1730130814984319){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}, improving
a model's accuracy from 90 to 95% is more expensive than improving it
from 85 to 90%. As Meta's paper ["Beyond Neural Scaling Laws: Beating
Power Law Scaling via Data Pruning"](https://oreil.ly/kO41d){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} pointed out, this means a
model with a 2% error rate might require an order of magnitude more
data, compute, or energy than a model with a 3% error rate.

In language modeling, a drop in cross entropy loss from about 3.4 to 2.8
nats requires 10 times more training data. Cross entropy and its units,
including nats, are discussed in
[Chapter 3](#ch03.html_ch03a_evaluation_methodology_1730150757064067){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}. For large
vision models, increasing the number of training samples from 1 billion
to 2 billion leads to an accuracy gain on ImageNet of only a few
percentage points.

However, small performance changes in language modeling loss or ImageNet
accuracy can lead to big differences in the quality of downstream
applications. If you switch from a model with a cross-entropy loss of
3.4 to one with a loss of 2.8, you'll notice a
difference.[]{#ch02.html_id769 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html28" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id770
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html27"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id771 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html26" data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::

::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Scaling extrapolation"}
:::: {#ch02.html_ch02_scaling_extrapolation_1730147895572029 .dedication}
#### Scaling extrapolation {.calibre36}

[]{#ch02.html_id772 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="hyperparameters"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id773 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model size" secondary="scaling extrapolation"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id774 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="scaling extrapolation" data-type="indexterm"}The performance of
a model depends heavily on the values of its *hyperparameters*. When
working with small models, it's a common practice to train a model
multiple times with different sets of hyperparameters and pick the
best-performing one. This is, however, rarely possible for large models
as training them once is resource-draining enough.

<aside data-type="sidebar" epub:type="sidebar" class="calibre20">

::: {#ch02.html_ch02_parameter_versus_hyperparameter_1730147895572053 .sidebar}
## Parameter Versus Hyperparameter {.calibre21}

[]{#ch02.html_id775 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="foundation models"
secondary="parameter versus hyperparameter" data-type="indexterm"}A
parameter can be learned by the model during the training process. A
hyperparameter is set by users to configure the model and control how
the model learns. Hyperparameters to configure the model include the
number of layers, the model dimension, and vocabulary size.
Hyperparameters to control how a model learns include batch size, number
of epochs, learning rate, per-layer initial variance, and more.
:::

</aside>

This means that for many models, you might have only one shot of getting
the right set of hyperparameters. As a result, *scaling extrapolation*
(also called *hyperparameter transferring*) has emerged as a research
subfield that tries to predict, for large models, what hyperparameters
will give the best performance. The current approach is to study the
impact of hyperparameters on models of different sizes, usually much
smaller than the target model size, and then extrapolate how these
hyperparameters would work on the target model
size.^[18](#ch02.html_id776){#ch02.html_id776-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ A
[2022 paper](https://oreil.ly/sHwbw){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} by Microsoft and OpenAI shows that it was
possible to transfer hyperparameters from a 40M model to a 6.7B model.

Scaling extrapolation is still a niche topic, as few people have the
experience and resources to study the training of large models. It's
also difficult to do due to the sheer number of hyperparameters and how
they interact with each other. If you have ten hyperparameters, you'd
have to study 1,024 hyperparameter combinations. You would have to study
each hyperparameter individually, then two of them together, and three
of them together, and so on.

In addition, emergent abilities ([Wei et al.,
2022](https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.07682){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}) make the extrapolation less accurate. Emergent
abilities refer to those that are only present at scale might not be
observable on smaller models trained on smaller datasets. To learn more
about scaling extrapolation, check out this excellent blog post: "On the
Difficulty of Extrapolation with NN Scaling" ([Luke Metz,
2022](https://oreil.ly/kuG3J){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}).
::::
:::::

::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Scaling bottlenecks"}
:::: {#ch02.html_ch02_scaling_bottlenecks_1730147895572077 .dedication}
#### Scaling bottlenecks {.calibre36}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html29 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="bottlenecks"
secondary="scaling" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html30
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="model size"
secondary="scaling bottlenecks"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html31 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="scaling bottlenecks" data-type="indexterm"}Until now, every
order of magnitude increase in model size has led to an increase in
model performance. GPT-2 has an order of magnitude more parameters than
GPT-1 (1.5 billion versus 117 million). GPT-3 has two orders of
magnitude more than GPT-2 (175 billion versus 1.5 billion). This means a
three-orders-of-magnitude increase in model sizes between 2018 and 2021.
Three more orders of magnitude growth would result in
100-trillion-parameter
models.^[19](#ch02.html_id777){#ch02.html_id777-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

How many more orders of magnitude can model sizes grow? Would there be a
point where the model performance plateaus regardless of its size? While
it's hard to answer these questions, there are already two visible
bottlenecks for scaling: training data and electricity.

Foundation models use so much data that there's a realistic concern
we'll run out of internet data in the next few years. The rate of
training dataset size growth is much faster than the rate of new data
being generated ([Villalobos et al.,
2022](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.04325){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), as illustrated in
[Figure 2-9](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_9_1730147895520897){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}. *If
you've ever put anything on the internet, you should assume that it
already is or will be included in the training data for some language
models,* whether you consent or not. This is similar to how, if you post
something on the internet, you should expect it to be indexed by Google.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch02.html_ch02_figure_9_1730147895520897" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0209.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A graph of data being measured Description automatically generated with medium confidence" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 2-9.
</span>Projection of historical trend of training dataset sizes and
available data stock. Source: Villalobos et al., 2024.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Some people are leveraging this fact to inject data they want into the
training data of future models. They do this simply by publishing the
text they want on the internet, hoping it will influence future models
to generate the responses they desire. Bad actors can also leverage this
approach for prompt injection attacks, as discussed in
[Chapter 5](#ch05.html_ch05a_prompt_engineering_1730156991195551){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

::: {.calibre22 .note data-type="note"}
####### Note {.calibre23}

An open research question is how to make a model forget specific
information it has learned during training. Imagine you published a blog
post that you eventually deleted. If that blog post was included in a
model's training data, the model might still reproduce the post's
content. As a result, people could potentially access removed content
without your consent.
:::

On top of that, the internet is being rapidly populated with data
generated by AI models. If companies continue using internet data to
train future models, these [new models]{.keep-together} will be
partially trained on AI-generated data. In December 2023, Grok, a model
trained by X, was caught refusing a request by saying that it goes
against OpenAI's use case policy. This caused some people to speculate
that Grok was trained using ChatGPT outputs. [Igor Babuschkin, a core
developer behind
Grok](https://x.com/ibab/status/1733558576982155274){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, responded that it was
because Grok was trained on web data, and "the web is full of ChatGPT
outputs."^[20](#ch02.html_id778){#ch02.html_id778-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

Some researchers worry that recursively training new AI models on
AI-generated data causes the new models to gradually forget the original
data patterns, degrading their performance over time ([Shumailov et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.17493){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}). However, the impact of AI-generated data on
models is more nuanced and is discussed in
[Chapter 8](#ch08.html_ch08_dataset_engineering_1730130932019888){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

Once the publicly available data is exhausted, the most feasible paths
for more human-generated training data is proprietary data. Unique
proprietary data---copyrighted books, translations, contracts, medical
records, genome sequences, and so forth---will be a competitive
advantage in the AI race. This is a reason why OpenAI negotiated
[deals](https://oreil.ly/AkAyI){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} with publishers and media outlets including Axel
Springer and the Associated Press.

It's not surprising that in light of ChatGPT, many companies, including
[Reddit](https://oreil.ly/o7WB3){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} and [Stack
Overflow](https://oreil.ly/xNuju){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, have changed their data terms to prevent other
companies from scraping their data for their models. [Longpre et al.
(2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.14933){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} observed that between 2023 and 2024,
the rapid crescendo of data restrictions from web sources rendered over
28% of the most critical sources in the popular public dataset
[C4](https://github.com/google-research/text-to-text-transfer-transformer#c4){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} fully restricted from use.
Due to changes in its Terms of Service and crawling restrictions, a full
45% of C4 is now restricted.

The other bottleneck, which is less obvious but more pressing, is
electricity. Machines require electricity to run. As of this writing,
data centers are estimated to consume 1--2% of global electricity. This
number is estimated to reach between [4% and 20% by
2030](https://oreil.ly/0DKHL){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} (Patel, Nishball, and Ontiveros, 2024). Until we
can figure out a way to produce more energy, data centers can grow at
most 50 times, which is less than two orders of magnitude. This leads to
a concern about a power shortage in the near future, which will drive up
the cost of electricity.

Now that we've covered two key modeling decisions---architecture and
scale---let's move on to the next critical set of design choices: how to
align models with human []{#ch02.html_id779 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html31" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id780
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html30"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id781 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html29"
data-type="indexterm"}preferences[]{#ch02.html_id782 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="" startref="ch02.html25"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id783 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html24" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id784
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html23"
data-type="indexterm"}.[]{#ch02.html_id785 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html11" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id786
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html10"
data-type="indexterm"}
::::
:::::
::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect1" pdf-bookmark="Post-Training"}
:::::::::::: {#ch02.html_ch02_post_training_1730147895572108 .dedication}
## Post-Training {.calibre4}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html32 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="foundation models"
secondary="post-training"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html33 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="post-training" data-type="indexterm"}Post-training starts with
a pre-trained model. Let's say that you've pre-trained a foundation
model using self-supervision. Due to how pre-training works today, a
pre-trained model typically has two issues. First, self-supervision
optimizes the model for text completion, not
conversations.^[21](#ch02.html_id787){#ch02.html_id787-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ If you
find this unclear, don't worry, ["Supervised
Finetuning"](#ch02.html_ch02_supervised_finetuning_1730147895572140){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} will have
examples. Second, if the model is pre-trained on data indiscriminately
scraped from the internet, its outputs can be racist, sexist, rude, or
just wrong. The goal of post-training is to address both of these
issues.

Every model's post-training is different. However, in general,
post-training consists of two steps:

1.  *Supervised finetuning* (*SFT*): Finetune the pre-trained model on
    high-quality instruction data to optimize models for conversations
    instead of completion.[]{#ch02.html_id788 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
    primary="SFT (supervised finetuning)"
    data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id789 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
    primary="supervised finetuning (SFT)" data-type="indexterm"}

2.  *Preference finetuning*: Further finetune the model to output
    responses that align with human preference. Preference finetuning is
    typically done with reinforcement learning
    (RL).^[22](#ch02.html_id790){#ch02.html_id790-marker .pcalibre7
    .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^
    Techniques for preference finetuning include [*reinforcement
    learning from human feedback*](https://oreil.ly/iJG1q){.pcalibre7
    .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (RLHF) (used by
    [GPT-3.5](https://oreil.ly/tbgTi){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
    .calibre10 .pcalibre4} and [Llama
    2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.09288){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}),[]{#ch02.html_id791 .pcalibre7
    .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
    data-type="indexterm" primary="Llama"
    secondary="preference finetuning"}
    [DPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.18290){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (Direct Preference Optimization)
    (used by [Llama 3](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.21783){.pcalibre7
    .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), and [*reinforcement
    learning from AI
    feedback*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.00267){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (RLAIF) (potentially used by
    [Claude](https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.08073){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}).

Let me highlight the difference between pre-training and post-training
another way. For language-based foundation models, pre-training
optimizes token-level quality, where the model is trained to predict the
next token accurately. However, users don't care about token-level
quality---they care about the quality of the entire response.
Post-training, in general, optimizes the model to generate responses
that users prefer. Some people compare pre-training to reading to
acquire knowledge, while post-training is like learning how to use that
knowledge.

::: {.calibre22 .warning data-type="warning"}
####### Warning {.calibre29}

Watch out for terminology ambiguity. Some people use the term
*instruction finetuning* to refer to supervised finetuning, while some
other people use this term to refer to both supervised finetuning and
preference finetuning. To avoid ambiguity, I will avoid the term
instruction finetuning in this book.
:::

As post-training consumes a small portion of resources compared to
pre-training ([InstructGPT](https://oreil.ly/9bbzX){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} used only 2% of compute for
post-training and 98% for pre-training), you can think of post-training
as unlocking the capabilities that the pre-trained model already has but
are hard for users to access via prompting alone.

[Figure 2-10](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_10_1730147895520905){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows the
overall workflow of pre-training, SFT, and preference finetuning,
assuming you use RLHF for the last step. You can approximate how well a
model aligns with human preference by determining what steps the model
creators have taken.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch02.html_ch02_figure_10_1730147895520905" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0210.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A diagram of a data analysis Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 2-10.
</span>The overall training workflow with pre-training, SFT, and
RLHF.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

If you squint,
[Figure 2-10](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_10_1730147895520905){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} looks very
similar to the meme depicting the monster
[Shoggoth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shoggoth){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} with a smiley face in
[Figure 2-11](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_11_1730147895520913){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}:

1.  Self-supervised pre-training results in a rogue model that can be
    considered an untamed monster because it uses indiscriminate data
    from the internet.

2.  This monster is then supervised finetuned on higher-quality
    data---Stack Overflow, Quora, or human annotations---which makes it
    more socially acceptable.

3.  This finetuned model is further polished using preference finetuning
    to make it customer-appropriate, which is like giving it a smiley
    face.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch02.html_ch02_figure_11_1730147895520913" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0211.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A drawing of a monster Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 2-11.
</span>Shoggoth with a smiley face. Adapted from an original image
shared by <a href="https://x.com/anthrupad/status/1622349563922362368"
class="pcalibre7 pcalibre5 pcalibre6 calibre10 pcalibre4">anthrupad</a>.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Note that a combination of pre-training, SFT, and preference finetuning
is the popular solution for building foundation models today, but it's
not the only solution. You can skip any of the steps, as you'll see
shortly.

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Supervised Finetuning"}
::: {#ch02.html_ch02_supervised_finetuning_1730147895572140 .dedication}
### Supervised Finetuning {.calibre35}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html34 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="foundation models"
secondary="post-training" tertiary="supervised finetuning"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html35 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="post-training" secondary="supervised finetuning"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html36 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="SFT (supervised finetuning)"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html37 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="supervised finetuning (SFT)" data-type="indexterm"}As discussed
in
[Chapter 1](#ch01.html_ch01_introduction_to_building_ai_applications_with_foun_1730130814984319){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}, the
pre-trained model is likely optimized for completion rather than
conversing. If you input "How to make pizza" into the model, the model
will continue to complete this sentence, as the model has no concept
that this is supposed to be a conversation. Any of the following three
options can be a valid completion:

1.  Adding more context to the question: "for a family of six?"

2.  Adding follow-up questions: "What ingredients do I need? How much
    time would it take?"

3.  Giving the instructions on how to make pizza.

If the goal is to respond to users appropriately, the correct option is
3.

We know that a model mimics its training data. To encourage a model to
generate the appropriate responses, you can show examples of appropriate
responses. Such examples follow the format (*prompt, response*) and are
called []{#ch02.html_id792 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="demonstration data"
data-type="indexterm"}*demonstration data*. Some people refer to this
process as *behavior cloning*: you demonstrate how the model should
behave, and the model clones this behavior.

Since different types of requests require different types of responses,
your demonstration data should contain the range of requests you want
your model to handle, such as question answering, summarization, and
translation.
[Figure 2-12](#ch02.html_ch02_figure_12_1730147895520920){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows a
distribution of types of tasks OpenAI used to finetune their model
[InstructGPT](https://oreil.ly/8U2z8){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}. Note that this distribution doesn't contain
multimodal tasks, as InstructGPT is a text-only model.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch02.html_ch02_figure_12_1730147895520920" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0212.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A colorful circle with text on it Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 2-12.
</span>The distribution of prompts used to finetune InstructGPT. The
graph is created based on the numbers from the OpenAI paper.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Good teachers are important for humans to learn. Similarly, good
labelers are important for AIs to learn how to conduct intelligent
conversations. Unlike traditional data labeling, which can often be done
with little or no domain expertise, demonstration data may contain
complex prompts whose responses require critical thinking, information
gathering, and judgment about the appropriateness of the user's
requests.
[Table 2-6](#ch02.html_ch02_table_6_1730147895537582){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows
examples of (prompt, response) pairs created by labelers for
InstructGPT.

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Prompt                                                                                                                                         Labeler's response
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  `Serendipity means the occurrence and development of events by chance in a happy or beneficial way. Use the word in a sentence.`{.calibre45}   `Running into Margaret and being introduced to Tom was a fortunate stroke of`{.calibre45}\
                                                                                                                                                 `serendipity.`{.calibre45}

  `Read the following text and answer the three questions at the end.`{.calibre53}\                                                              `1. …`{.calibre53}\
  \                                                                                                                                              `2. …`{.calibre53}\
  `[OMITTED FOR LENGTH - Article about Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg,`{.calibre53}\                                                                `3. Ruth Bader Ginsburg was the leader of the liberal wing, and because the court is about to open a new term, the chief`{.calibre53}\
  `Champion Of Gender Equality, Dies At 87]`{.calibre53}\                                                                                        `justice no longer holds the controlling vote in contested cases.`{.calibre53}
  \                                                                                                                                              
  `1. …`{.calibre53}\                                                                                                                            
  `2. …`{.calibre53}\                                                                                                                            
  `3. Why will Ginsburg’s death have`{.calibre53}\                                                                                               
  `profound consequences for the court and the country?`{.calibre53}                                                                             

  `ELI5: What’s the cause of the`{.calibre45}\                                                                                                   `The anxiety lump in your throat is caused by muscular tension keeping your glottis dilated to maximize airflow. The clenched chest or heartache feeling is caused by the vagus nerve which tells the organs to pump blood faster, stop digesting, and`{.calibre45}\
  `"anxiety lump" in our chest during stressful or disheartening`{.calibre45}\                                                                   `produce adrenaline and cortisol.`{.calibre45}
  `experiences?`{.calibre45}                                                                                                                     
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  : [Table 2-6. ]{.keep-together}Examples of demonstration data used for
  [InstructGPT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.02155){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
  .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. {#ch02_table_6_1730147895537582
  .stafflist_table}

Companies, therefore, often use highly educated labelers to generate
demonstration data. Among those who labeled demonstration data for
InstructGPT, [\~90% have at least a college
degree](https://oreil.ly/SF_X9){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} and more than one-third have a master's degree.
If labeling objects in an image might take only seconds, generating one
(prompt, response) pair can take up to 30 minutes, especially for tasks
that involve long contexts like summarization. If it costs \$10 for one
(prompt, response) pair, the 13,000 pairs that OpenAI used for
InstructGPT would cost \$130,000. That doesn't yet include the cost of
designing the data (what tasks and prompts to include), recruiting
labelers, and data quality control.

Not everyone can afford to follow the high-quality human annotation
approach. LAION, a non-profit organization, mobilized 13,500 volunteers
worldwide to generate 10,000 conversations, which consist of 161,443
messages in 35 different languages, annotated with 461,292 quality
ratings. Since the data was generated by volunteers, there wasn't much
control for biases. In theory, the labelers that teach models the human
preference should be representative of the human population. The
demographic of labelers for LAION is skewed. For example, in a
self-reported survey, 90% of volunteer labelers identified as male
([Köpf et al., 2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.07327){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}).

DeepMind used [simple
heuristics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.11446){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} to filter for conversations from
internet data to train their model Gopher. They claimed that their
heuristics reliably yield high-quality dialogues. Specifically, they
looked for texts that look like the following format:

> `[A]: [Short paragraph]`{.calibre26}
>
> `[B]: [Short paragraph]`{.calibre26}
>
> `[A]: [Short paragraph]`{.calibre26}
>
> `[B]: [Short paragraph]`{.calibre26}
>
> `…`{.calibre26}

To reduce their dependence on high-quality human annotated data, many
teams are turning to AI-generated data. Synthetic data is discussed in
[Chapter 8](#ch08.html_ch08_dataset_engineering_1730130932019888){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

Technically, you can train a model from scratch on the demonstration
data instead of finetuning a pre-trained model, effectively eliminating
the self-supervised pre-training step. However, the pre-training
approach often has returned superior results.[]{#ch02.html_id793
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html37"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id794 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html36" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id795
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch02.html35"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id796 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch02.html34" data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::

:::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Preference Finetuning"}
::::::: {#ch02.html_ch02_preference_finetuning_1730147895572167 .dedication}
### Preference Finetuning {.calibre35}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html38 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="foundation models"
secondary="post-training" tertiary="preference finetuning"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html39 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="post-training" secondary="preference finetuning"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html40 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="preference finetuning"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html41 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF)"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html42 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback)"
data-type="indexterm"}With great power comes great responsibilities. A
model that can assist users in achieving great things can also assist
users in achieving terrible things. Demonstration data teaches the model
to have a conversation but doesn't teach the model what kind of
conversations it should have. For example, if a user asks the model to
write an essay about why one race is inferior or how to hijack a plane,
should the model [comply?]{.keep-together}

In both of the preceding examples, it's straightforward to most people
what a model should do. However, many scenarios aren't as clear-cut.
People from different cultural, political, socioeconomic, gender, and
religious backgrounds disagree with each other all the time. How should
AI respond to questions about abortion, gun control, the
Israel--Palestine conflict, disciplining children, marijuana legality,
universal basic income, or immigration? How do we define and detect
potentially controversial issues? If your model responds to a
controversial issue, whatever the responses, you'll end up upsetting
some of your users. If a model is censored too much, your model [may
become boring](https://oreil.ly/5oSEJ){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, [driving away
users](https://oreil.ly/D1S6y){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}.

Fear of AI models generating inappropriate responses can stop companies
from releasing their applications to users. The goal of preference
finetuning is to get AI models to behave according to human
preference.^[23](#ch02.html_id797){#ch02.html_id797-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ This
is an ambitious, if not impossible, goal. Not only does this assume that
universal human preference exists, but it also assumes that it's
possible to embed it into AI.

Had the goal been simple, the solution could've been elegant. However,
given the ambitious nature of the goal, the solution we have today is
complicated. The earliest successful preference finetuning algorithm,
which is still popular today, is RLHF. RLHF consists of two parts:

1.  Train a reward model that scores the foundation model's outputs.

2.  Optimize the foundation model to generate responses for which the
    reward model will give maximal scores.

[]{#ch02.html_id798 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch02.html_id799 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="DPO (Direct Preference Optimization)"
data-type="indexterm"}While RLHF is still used today, newer approaches
like DPO ([Rafailov et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.18290){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}) are gaining traction. For example,
[]{#ch02.html_id800 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm" primary="Llama"
secondary="prefer"}Meta switched from RLHF for Llama 2 to DPO for Llama
3 to reduce complexity. I won't be able to cover all the different
approaches in this book. I choose to feature RLHF instead of DPO here
because RLHF, while more complex than DPO, provides more flexibility to
tweak the model. Llama 2's authors posited that "the superior writing
abilities of LLMs, as manifested in surpassing human annotators in
certain tasks, are fundamentally driven by RLHF" ([Touvron et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.09288){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}).

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Reward model"}
::: {#ch02.html_ch02_reward_model_1730147895572194 .dedication}
#### Reward model {.calibre36}

[]{#ch02.html_ch02.html44 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="reward models"
data-type="indexterm"}RLHF relies on a reward model. Given a pair of
(prompt, response), the *reward model* outputs a score for how good the
response is. Training a model to score a given input is a common ML
task. The challenge, similar to that of SFT, is to obtain reliable data.
If we ask labelers to score each response directly, the scores will
vary. For the same sample, on a 10-point scale, one labeler might give a
5 and another 7. Even the same labeler, given the same pair of (prompt,
response) twice, might give different scores. []{#ch02.html_id801
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="pointwise evaluation"}Evaluating each sample independently is
also called *pointwise* evaluation.

An easier task is to ask labelers to compare two responses and decide
which one is better. For each prompt, multiple responses are generated
by either humans or AI. The resulting labeled data is
[]{#ch02.html_id802 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="comparison data"
data-type="indexterm"}*comparison data*, which follows the format
(prompt, winning_response, losing_response).
[Table 2-7](#ch02.html_ch02_table_7_1730147895537589){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows an
example of comparison data used by Anthropic for one of their models. Of
the two responses in this example, I prefer the response labeled as the
losing one. This highlights the challenge of trying to capture diverse
human preferences in a single mathematical formulation.
