:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Model Selection Workflow"}
::: {#ch04.html_ch04_model_selection_workflow_1730130866189327 .dedication}
### Model Selection Workflow {.calibre35}

[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html21 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="model selection"
secondary="model selection workflow"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html22 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="systems evaluation" secondary="model selection"
tertiary="model selection workflow" data-type="indexterm"}When looking
at models, it's important to differentiate between hard attributes (what
is impossible or impractical for you to change) and soft attributes
(what you can and are willing to change).

[]{#ch04.html_id1034 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="hard attributes"
data-type="indexterm"}Hard attributes are often the results of decisions
made by model providers (licenses, training data, model size) or your
own policies (privacy, control). For some use cases, the hard attributes
can reduce the pool of potential models significantly.

[]{#ch04.html_id1035 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="soft attributes"
data-type="indexterm"}Soft attributes are attributes that can be
improved upon, such as accuracy, toxicity, or factual consistency. When
estimating how much you can improve on a certain attribute, it can be
tricky to balance being optimistic and being realistic. I've had
situations where a model's accuracy hovered around 20% for the first few
prompts. However, the accuracy jumped to 70% after I decomposed the task
into two steps. At the same time, I've had situations where a model
remained unusable for my task even after weeks of tweaking, and I had to
give up on that model.

What you define as hard and soft attributes depends on both the model
and your use case. For example, latency is a soft attribute if you have
access to the model to optimize it to run faster. It's a hard attribute
if you use a model hosted by someone else.

At a high level, the evaluation workflow consists of four steps (see
[Figure 4-5](#ch04.html_ch04_figure_5_1730130866113641){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}):

1.  Filter out models whose hard attributes don't work for you. Your
    list of hard attributes depends heavily on your own internal
    policies, whether you want to use commercial APIs or host your own
    models.

2.  Use publicly available information, e.g., benchmark performance and
    leaderboard ranking, to narrow down the most promising models to
    experiment with, balancing different objectives such as model
    quality, latency, and cost.

3.  Run experiments with your own evaluation pipeline to find the best
    model, again, balancing all your objectives.

4.  Continually monitor your model in production to detect failure and
    collect feedback to improve your application.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch04.html_ch04_figure_5_1730130866113641" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0405.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A diagram of a diagram Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 4-5. </span>An
overview of the evaluation workflow to evaluate models for your <span
class="keep-together">application.</span></h6>
</div>
</figure>

These four steps are iterative---you might want to change the decision
from a previous step with newer information from the current step. For
example, you might initially want to host open source models. However,
after public and private evaluation, you might realize that open source
models can't achieve the level of performance you want and have to
switch to commercial APIs.

[Chapter 10](#ch10.html_ch10_ai_engineering_architecture_and_user_feedback_1730130985311851){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} discusses
monitoring and collecting user feedback. The rest of this chapter will
discuss the first three steps. First, let's discuss a question that most
teams will visit more than once: to use model APIs or to host models
themselves. We'll then continue to how to navigate the dizzying number
of public benchmarks and why you can't trust them. This will set the
stage for the last section in the chapter. Because public benchmarks
can't be trusted, you need to design your own evaluation pipeline with
prompts and metrics you can trust.[]{#ch04.html_id1036 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="" startref="ch04.html22"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1037 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch04.html21" data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Model Build Versus Buy"}
::::::::::::::::::::::: {#ch04.html_ch04_model_build_versus_buy_1730130866189401 .dedication}
### Model Build Versus Buy {.calibre35}

[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html23 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="model selection"
secondary="model build versus buy"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html24 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="systems evaluation" secondary="model selection"
tertiary="model build versus buy" data-type="indexterm"}An evergreen
question for companies when leveraging any technology is whether to
build or buy. Since most companies won't be building foundation models
from scratch, the question is whether to use commercial model APIs or
host an open source model yourself. The answer to this question can
significantly reduce your candidate model pool.

Let's first go into what exactly open source means when it comes to
models, then discuss the pros and cons of these two approaches.

::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Open source, open weight, and model licenses"}
:::: {#ch04.html_ch04_open_source_open_weight_and_model_licenses_1730130866189479 .dedication}
#### Open source, open weight, and model licenses {.calibre36}

[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html25 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="model selection"
secondary="model build versus buy"
tertiary="open source, open weight, and model licenses"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html26 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="open source licenses" data-type="indexterm"}The term "open
source model" has become contentious. Originally, open source was used
to refer to any model that people can download and use. For many use
cases, being able to download the model is sufficient. However, some
people argue that since a model's performance is largely a function of
what data it was trained on, [*a model*]{.keep-together} *should be
considered open only if its training data is also made publicly*
[*available*.]{.keep-together}

Open data allows more flexible model usage, such as retraining the model
from scratch with modifications in the model architecture, training
process, or the training data itself. Open data also makes it easier to
understand the model. Some use cases also required access to the
training data for auditing purposes, for example, to make sure that the
model wasn't trained on compromised or illegally acquired
data.^[10](#ch04.html_id1038){#ch04.html_id1038-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

[]{#ch04.html_id1039 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="open weight models"
data-type="indexterm"}To signal whether the data is also open, the term
"open weight" is used for models that don't come with open data, whereas
the term "open model" is used for models that come with open data.

::: {.calibre22 .note data-type="note"}
####### Note {.calibre23}

Some people argue that the term open source should be reserved only for
fully open models. In this book, for simplicity, I use open source to
refer to all models whose weights are made public, regardless of their
training data's availability and licenses.
:::

As of this writing, the vast majority of open source models are open
weight only. Model developers might hide training data information on
purpose, as this information can open model developers to public
scrutiny and potential lawsuits.

Another important attribute of open source models is their licenses.
Before foundation models, the open source world was confusing enough,
with so many different licenses, such as MIT (Massachusetts Institute of
Technology), Apache 2.0, GNU General Public License (GPL), BSD (Berkely
Software Distribution), Creative [Commons,]{.keep-together} etc. Open
source models made the licensing situation worse. Many
[models]{.keep-together} are released under their own unique licenses.
[]{#ch04.html_id1040 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm" primary="Llama"
secondary="open source models"}For example, Meta released Llama 2 under
the [Llama 2 Community License
Agreement](https://oreil.ly/wRlEh){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} and Llama 3 under the [Llama 3 Community License
Agreement](https://oreil.ly/FL-1Z){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}. Hugging Face released their model BigCode under
the [BigCode Open RAIL-M v1](https://oreil.ly/yED-R){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} license. However, I hope
that, over time, the community will converge toward some standard
licenses. Both [Google's
Gemma](https://github.com/google-deepmind/gemma/blob/main/LICENSE){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} and
[Mistral-7B](https://oreil.ly/uTBwP){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} were released under Apache 2.0.

Each license has its own conditions, so it'll be up to you to evaluate
each license for your needs. However, here are a few questions that I
think everyone should ask:

- Does the license allow commercial use? When Meta's first Llama model
  was released, it was under a [noncommercial
  license](https://oreil.ly/V1P8X){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
  .calibre10 .pcalibre4}.

- If it allows commercial use, are there any restrictions? Llama-2 and
  Llama-3 specify that applications with more than 700 million monthly
  active users require a special license from
  Meta.^[11](#ch04.html_id1041){#ch04.html_id1041-marker .pcalibre7
  .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

- Does the license allow using the model's outputs to train or improve
  upon other models? Synthetic data, generated by existing models, is an
  important source [of data to]{.keep-together} train future models
  (discussed together with other data synthesis topics in
  [Chapter 8](#ch08.html_ch08_dataset_engineering_1730130932019888){.pcalibre7
  .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}).
  []{#ch04.html_id1042 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
  .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
  primary="distillation" secondary="model distillation"}A use case of
  data synthesis is *model distillation*: teaching a [student
  (typically]{.keep-together} a much smaller model) to mimic the
  behavior of a teacher (typically a much larger model). Mistral didn't
  allow this originally but later changed its
  [license](https://x.com/arthurmensch/status/1734470462451732839){.pcalibre7
  .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. As of this writing, the
  Llama licenses still don't allow
  it.^[12](#ch04.html_id1043){#ch04.html_id1043-marker .pcalibre7
  .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

Some people use the term []{#ch04.html_id1044 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="restricted weight" data-type="indexterm"}*restricted weight* to
refer to open source models with restricted licenses. However, I find
this term ambiguous, since all sensible licenses have restrictions
(e.g., you shouldn't be able to use the model to commit
genocide).[]{#ch04.html_id1045 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch04.html26" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1046
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch04.html25"
data-type="indexterm"}
::::
:::::

::::::::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Open source models versus model APIs"}
:::::::::::::::::: {#ch04.html_ch04_open_source_models_versus_model_apis_1730130866189555 .dedication}
#### Open source models versus model APIs {.calibre36}

[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html27 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="model selection"
secondary="model build versus buy"
tertiary="open source models versus model APIs"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html28 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="open source models, model APIs versus"
data-type="indexterm"}For a model to be accessible to users, a machine
needs to host and run it. []{#ch04.html_id1047 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
data-type="indexterm" primary="inference service"
secondary="defined"}The service that hosts the model and receives user
queries, runs the model to generate responses for queries, and returns
these responses to the users is called an inference service. The
interface users interact with is called the *model API*, as shown in
[Figure 4-6](#ch04.html_ch04_figure_6_1730130866113662){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}. The term
*model API* is typically used to refer to the API of the inference
service, but there are also APIs for other model services, such as
finetuning APIs and evaluation APIs.
[Chapter 9](#ch09.html_ch09_inference_optimization_1730130963006301){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} discusses
how to optimize inference services.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch04.html_ch04_figure_6_1730130866113662" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0406.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A diagram of a service Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 4-6. </span>An
inference service runs the model and provides an interface for users to
access the model.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

After developing a model, a developer can choose to open source it, make
it accessible via an API, or both. Many model developers are also model
service providers. Cohere and Mistral open source some models and
provide APIs for some. []{#ch04.html_id1048 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
data-type="indexterm" primary="OpenAI"
secondary="open source APIs"}OpenAI is typically known for their
commercial models, but they've also open sourced models (GPT-2, CLIP).
Typically, model providers open source weaker models and keep their best
models behind paywalls, either via APIs or to power their products.

Model APIs can be available through model providers (such as OpenAI and
Anthropic), cloud service providers (such as Azure and GCP \[Google
Cloud Platform\]), or third-party API providers (such as Databricks
Mosaic, Anyscale, etc.). The same model can be available through
different APIs with different features, constraints, and pricings. For
example, GPT-4 is available through both OpenAI and Azure APIs. There
might be slight differences in the performance of the same model
provided through different APIs, as different APIs might use different
techniques to optimize this model, so make sure to run thorough tests
when you switch between model APIs.

Commercial models are only accessible via APIs licensed by the model
developers.^[13](#ch04.html_id1049){#ch04.html_id1049-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ Open
source models can be supported by any API provider, allowing you to pick
and choose the provider that works best for you. For commercial model
providers, *models are their competitive advantages*. For API providers
that don't have their own models, *APIs are their competitive
advantages*. This means API providers might be more motivated to provide
better APIs with better pricing.

Since building scalable inference services for larger models is
nontrivial, many companies don't want to build them themselves. This has
led to the creation of many third-party inference and finetuning
services on top of open source models. Major cloud providers like AWS,
Azure, and GCP all provide API access to popular open source models. A
plethora of startups are doing the same.

::: {.calibre22 .note data-type="note"}
####### Note {.calibre23}

There are also commercial API providers that can deploy their services
within your private networks. In this discussion, I treat these
privately deployed commercial APIs similarly to self-hosted models.
:::

The answer to whether to host a model yourself or use a model API
depends on the use case. And the same use case can change over time.
Here are seven axes to consider: data privacy, data lineage,
performance, functionality, costs, control, and on-device deployment.

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Data privacy"}
::: {#ch04.html_ch04_data_privacy_1730130866189626 .dedication}
##### Data privacy {.calibre47}

[]{#ch04.html_id1050 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="data privacy"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1051 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="open source models, model APIs versus" secondary="data privacy"
data-type="indexterm"}Externally hosted model APIs are out of the
question for companies with strict data privacy policies that can't send
data outside of the
organization.^[14](#ch04.html_id1052){#ch04.html_id1052-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^ []{#ch04.html_id1053 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
data-type="indexterm" primary="ChatGPT"
secondary="data privacy issues"}One of the most notable early incidents
was when Samsung employees put Samsung's proprietary information into
ChatGPT, accidentally leaking the company's
secrets.^[15](#ch04.html_id1054){#ch04.html_id1054-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ It's
unclear how Samsung discovered this leak and how the leaked information
was used against Samsung. However, the incident was serious enough for
[Samsung to ban ChatGPT](https://oreil.ly/fWs9H){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} in May 2023.

Some countries have laws that forbid sending certain data outside their
borders. If a model API provider wants to serve these use cases, they
will have to set up servers in these countries.

If you use a model API, there's a risk that the API provider will use
your data to train its models. Even though most model API providers
claim they don't do that, their policies can change. In August 2023,
[Zoom faced a backlash](https://oreil.ly/xndQu){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} after people found out the company had
quietly changed its terms of service to let Zoom use users'
service-generated data, including product usage data and diagnostics
data, to train its AI models.

What's the problem with people using your data to train their models?
While research in this area is still sparse, some studies suggest that
AI models can [memorize their]{.keep-together} training samples. For
example, it's been found that [Hugging Face's StarCoder
model](https://x.com/dhuynh95/status/1713917852162424915){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} memorizes 8% of its
training set. These memorized samples can be accidentally leaked to
users or intentionally exploited by bad actors, as demonstrated in
[Chapter 5](#ch05.html_ch05a_prompt_engineering_1730156991195551){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Data lineage and copyright"}
::: {#ch04.html_ch04_data_lineage_and_copyright_1730130866189685 .dedication}
##### Data lineage and copyright {.calibre47}

[]{#ch04.html_id1055 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="data lineage"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1056 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="open source models, model APIs versus"
secondary="data lineage and copyright" data-type="indexterm"}Data
lineage and copyright concerns can steer a company in many directions:
toward open source models, toward proprietary models, or away from both.

For most models, there's little transparency about what data a model is
trained on. In [Gemini's technical
report](https://oreil.ly/AhHI_){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, Google went into detail about the models'
performance but said nothing about the models' training data other than
that "all data enrichment workers are paid at least a local living
wage". [OpenAI's
CTO](https://x.com/JoannaStern/status/1768306032466428291){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} wasn't able to provide a
satisfactory answer when asked what data was used to train their models.

On top of that, the IP laws around AI are actively evolving. While the
[US Patent and Trademark Office
(USPTO)](https://oreil.ly/p23MQ){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} made clear in 2024 that "AI-assisted inventions
are not categorically unpatentable", an AI application's patentability
depends on "whether the human contribution to an innovation is
significant enough to qualify for a patent." []{#ch04.html_id1057
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="copyright, model training and"
data-type="indexterm"}It's also unclear whether, if a model was trained
on copyrighted data, and you use this model to create your product, you
can defend your product's IP. Many companies whose existence depends
upon their IPs, such as gaming and movie studios, are [hesitant to use
AI](https://oreil.ly/-qEXt){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4} to aid in the creation of their products, at least until IP
laws around AI are clarified (James Vincent, *The Verge,* November 15,
2022).

Concerns over data lineage have driven some companies toward fully open
models, whose training data has been made publicly available. The
argument is that this allows the community to inspect the data and make
sure that it's safe to use. While it sounds great in theory, in
practice, it's challenging for any company to thoroughly inspect a
dataset of the size typically used to train foundation models.

Given the same concern, many companies opt for commercial models
instead. Open source models tend to have limited legal resources
compared to commercial models. If you use an open source model that
infringes on copyrights, the infringed party is unlikely to go after the
model developers, and more likely to go after you. However, if you use a
commercial model, the contracts you sign with the model providers can
potentially protect you from data lineage
risks.^[16](#ch04.html_id1058){#ch04.html_id1058-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Performance"}
::: {#ch04.html_ch04_performance_1730130866189741 .dedication}
##### Performance {.calibre47}

[]{#ch04.html_id1059 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="open source models, model APIs versus" secondary="performance"
data-type="indexterm"}Various benchmarks have shown that the gap between
open source models and proprietary models is closing.
[Figure 4-7](#ch04.html_ch04_figure_7_1730130866113682){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows this
gap decreasing on the MMLU benchmark over time. This trend has made many
people believe that one day, there will be an open source model that
performs just as well, if not better, than the strongest proprietary
model.

As much as I want open source models to catch up with proprietary
models, I don't think the incentives are set up for it. If you have the
strongest model available, would you rather open source it for other
people to capitalize on it, or would you try to capitalize on it
yourself?^[17](#ch04.html_id1060){#ch04.html_id1060-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ It's a
common practice for companies to keep their strongest models behind APIs
and open source their weaker models.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch04.html_ch04_figure_7_1730130866113682" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0407.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A graph showing a number of sources Description automatically generated with medium confidence" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 4-7. </span>The
gap between open source models and proprietary models is decreasing on
the MMLU benchmark. Image by Maxime Labonne.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

For this reason, it's likely that the strongest open source model will
lag behind the strongest proprietary models for the foreseeable future.
However, for many use cases that don't need the strongest models, open
source models might be sufficient.

Another reason that might cause open source models to lag behind is that
open source developers don't receive feedback from users to improve
their models, the way commercial models do. Once a model is open
sourced, model developers have no idea how the model is being used, and
how well the model works in the wild.
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Functionality"}
::: {#ch04.html_ch04_functionality_1730130866189798 .dedication}
##### Functionality {.calibre47}

[]{#ch04.html_id1061 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="open source models, model APIs versus"
secondary="functionality" data-type="indexterm"}Many functionalities are
needed around a model to make it work for a use case. Here are some
examples of these functionalities:

- Scalability: making sure the inference service can support your
  application's traffic while maintaining the desirable latency and
  cost.

- Function calling: giving the model the ability to use external tools,
  which is essential for RAG and agentic use cases, as discussed in
  [Chapter 6](#ch06.html_ch06_rag_and_agents_1730157386571386){.pcalibre7
  .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

- Structured outputs, such as asking models to generate outputs in JSON
  format.

- Output guardrails: mitigating risks in the generated responses, such
  as making sure the responses aren't racist or sexist.

Many of these functionalities are challenging and time-consuming to
implement, which makes many companies turn to API providers that provide
the functionalities they want out of the box.

The downside of using a model API is that you're restricted to the
functionalities that the API provides. A functionality that many use
cases need is logprobs, which are very useful for classification tasks,
evaluation, and interpretability. However, commercial model providers
might be hesitant to expose logprobs for fear of others using logprobs
to replicate their models. In fact, many model APIs don't expose
logprobs or expose only limited logprobs.

You can also only finetune a commercial model if the model provider lets
you. Imagine that you've maxed out a model's performance with prompting
and want to finetune that model. If this model is proprietary and the
model provider doesn't have a finetuning API, you won't be able to do
it. However, if it's an open source model, you can find a service that
offers finetuning on that model, or you can finetune it yourself. Keep
in mind that there are multiple types of finetuning, such as partial
finetuning and full finetuning, as discussed in
[Chapter 7](#ch07.html_ch07){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}. A commercial model provider might support
only some types of finetuning, not all.
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="API cost versus engineering cost"}
::: {#ch04.html_ch04_api_cost_versus_engineering_cost_1730130866189856 .dedication}
##### API cost versus engineering cost {.calibre47}

[]{#ch04.html_id1062 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="open source models, model APIs versus"
secondary="API cost versus engineering cost" data-type="indexterm"}Model
APIs charge per usage, which means that they can get prohibitively
expensive with heavy usage. At a certain scale, a company that is
bleeding its resources using APIs might consider hosting their own
models.^[18](#ch04.html_id1063){#ch04.html_id1063-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

However, hosting a model yourself requires nontrivial time, talent, and
engineering effort. You'll need to optimize the model, scale and
maintain the inference service as needed, and provide guardrails around
your model. APIs are expensive, but engineering can be even more so.

On the other hand, using another API means that you'll have to depend on
their SLA, service-level agreement. If these APIs aren't reliable, which
is often the case with early startups, you'll have to spend your
engineering effort on guardrails around that.

In general, you want a model that is easy to use and manipulate.
Typically, proprietary models are easier to get started with and scale,
but open models might be easier to manipulate as their components are
more accessible.

Regardless of whether you go with open or proprietary models, you want
this model to follow a standard API, which makes it easier to swap
models. Many model developers try to make their models mimic the API of
the most popular models. As of this writing, many API providers mimic
OpenAI's API.

You might also prefer models with good community support. The more
capabilities a model has, the more quirks it has. A model with a large
community of users means that any issue you encounter may already have
been experienced by others, who might have shared solutions
online.^[19](#ch04.html_id1064){#ch04.html_id1064-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Control, access, and transparency"}
::: {#ch04.html_ch04_control_access_and_transparency_1730130866189915 .dedication}
##### Control, access, and transparency {.calibre47}

[]{#ch04.html_id1065 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="open source models, model APIs versus"
secondary="control, access, and transparency" data-type="indexterm"}A
[2024 study by a16z](https://oreil.ly/Zj1GZ){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} shows two key reasons that enterprises
care about open source models are control and customizability, as shown
in [Figure 4-8](#ch04.html_ch04_figure_8_1730130866113701){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch04.html_ch04_figure_8_1730130866113701" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0408.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A screenshot of a graph Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 4-8. </span>Why
enterprises care about open source models. Image from the 2024 study by
a16z.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

If your business depends on a model, it's understandable that you would
want some control over it, and API providers might not always give you
the level of control you want. When using a service provided by someone
else, you're subject to their terms and conditions, and their rate
limits. You can access only what's made available to you by this
provider, and thus might not be able to tweak the model as needed.

[]{#ch04.html_id1066 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="guardrails"}To protect their users and themselves from
potential lawsuits, model providers use safety guardrails such as
blocking requests to tell racist jokes or generate photos of real
people. Proprietary models are more likely to err on the side of
over-censoring. These safety guardrails are good for the vast majority
of use cases but can be a limiting factor for certain use cases. For
example, if your application requires generating real faces (e.g., to
aid in the production of a music video) a model that refuses to generate
real faces won't work. A company I advise,
[Convai](https://convai.com){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4}, builds 3D AI characters that can interact in 3D
environments, including picking up objects. When working with commercial
models, they ran into an issue where the models kept responding: *"As an
AI model, I don't have physical abilities"*. Convai ended up finetuning
open source models.

There's also the risk of losing access to a commercial model, which can
be painful if you've built your system around it. You can't freeze a
commercial model the way you can with open source models. Historically,
commercial models lack transparency in model changes, versions, and
roadmaps. Models are frequently updated, but not all changes are
announced in advance or even announced at all. Your prompts might stop
working as expected and you have no idea. Unpredictable changes also
make commercial models unusable for strictly regulated applications.
However, I suspect that this historical lack of transparency in model
changes might just be an unintentional side effect of a fast-growing
industry. I hope that this will change as the industry matures.

A less common situation that unfortunately exists is that a model
provider can stop supporting your use case, your industry, or your
country, or your country can ban your model provider, as [Italy briefly
banned OpenAI in 2023](https://oreil.ly/pY1FF){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. A model provider can also go out of
business altogether.
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="On-device deployment"}
::: {#ch04.html_ch04_on_device_deployment_1730130866189978 .dedication}
##### On-device deployment {.calibre47}

[]{#ch04.html_id1067 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="on-device deployment"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1068 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="open source models, model APIs versus"
secondary="on-device deployment" data-type="indexterm"}If you want to
run a model on-device, third-party APIs are out of the question. In many
use cases, running a model locally is desirable. It could be because
your use case targets an area without reliable internet access. It could
be for privacy reasons, such as when you want to give an AI assistant
access to all your data, but don't want your data to leave your device.
[Table 4-4](#ch04.html_ch04_table_4_1730130866138383){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} summarizes
the pros and cons of using model APIs and self-hosting models.

+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+
|                       | Using model APIs      | Self-hosting models   |
+=======================+=======================+=======================+
| Data                  | - *Have to send your  | - Don't have to send  |
|                       |   data to model       |   your data           |
|                       |   providers, which    |   externally          |
|                       |   means your team can |                       |
|                       |   accidentally leak   | - *Fewer checks and   |
|                       |   confidential info*  |   balances for data   |
|                       |                       |   lineage/training    |
|                       |                       |   data copyright*     |
+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+
| Performance           | - Best-performing     | - *The best open      |
|                       |   model will likely   |   source models will  |
|                       |   be closed source    |   likely be a bit     |
|                       |                       |   behind commercial   |
|                       |                       |   models*             |
+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+
| Functionality         | - More likely to      | - *No/limited support |
|                       |   support scaling,    |   for function        |
|                       |   function calling,   |   calling and         |
|                       |   structured outputs  |   structured outputs* |
|                       |                       |                       |
|                       | - *Less likely to     | - Can access logprobs |
|                       |   expose logprobs*    |   and intermediate    |
|                       |                       |   outputs, which are  |
|                       |                       |   helpful for         |
|                       |                       |   classification      |
|                       |                       |   tasks, evaluation,  |
|                       |                       |   and                 |
|                       |                       |   interpretability    |
+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+
| Cost                  | - *API cost*          | - *Talent, time,      |
|                       |                       |   engineering effort  |
|                       |                       |   to optimize, host,  |
|                       |                       |   maintain* (can be   |
|                       |                       |   mitigated by using  |
|                       |                       |   model hosting       |
|                       |                       |   services)           |
+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+
| Finetuning            | - *Can only finetune  | - Can finetune,       |
|                       |   models that model   |   quantize, and       |
|                       |   providers let you*  |   optimize models (if |
|                       |                       |   their licenses      |
|                       |                       |   allow), *but it can |
|                       |                       |   be hard to do so*   |
+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+
| Control,\             | - *Rate limits*       | - Easier to inspect   |
| access, and\          |                       |   changes in open     |
| transparency          | - *Risk of losing     |   source models       |
|                       |   access to the       |                       |
|                       |   model*              | - You can freeze a    |
|                       |                       |   model to maintain   |
|                       | - *Lack of            |   its access, *but    |
|                       |   transparency in     |   you're responsible  |
|                       |   model changes and   |   for building and    |
|                       |   versioning*         |   maintaining model   |
|                       |                       |   APIs*               |
+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+
| Edge use cases        | - *Can't run on       | - Can run on device,  |
|                       |   device without      |   *but again, might   |
|                       |   internet access*    |   be hard to do so*   |
+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+

: [Table 4-4. ]{.keep-together}Pros and cons of using model APIs and
self-hosting models (cons in italics). {#ch04_table_4_1730130866138383
.stafflist_table}

The pros and cons of each approach hopefully can help you decide whether
to use a commercial API or to host a model yourself. This decision
should significantly narrow your options. Next, you can further refine
your selection using publicly available model performance
data[]{#ch04.html_id1069 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch04.html28"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1070 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch04.html27" data-type="indexterm"}.[]{#ch04.html_id1071
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch04.html24"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1072 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch04.html23" data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Navigate Public Benchmarks"}
::::::::::::::::: {#ch04.html_ch04_navigate_public_benchmarks_1730130866190058 .dedication}
### Navigate Public Benchmarks {.calibre35}

[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html31a .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="benchmarks"
secondary="navigating public benchmarks"}[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html31
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="model selection"
secondary="navigating public benchmarks"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html32 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="systems evaluation" secondary="model selection"
tertiary="navigating public benchmarks" data-type="indexterm"}There are
thousands of benchmarks designed to evaluate a model's different
capabilities. [Google's BIG-bench
(2022)](https://github.com/google/BIG-bench/blob/main/bigbench/benchmark_tasks/README.md){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} alone has 214 benchmarks.
The number of benchmarks rapidly grows to match the rapidly growing
number of AI use cases. In addition, as AI models improve, old
benchmarks saturate, necessitating the introduction of new benchmarks.

[]{#ch04.html_id1073 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="evaluation harnesses"
data-type="indexterm"}A tool that helps you evaluate a model on multiple
benchmarks is an *evaluation harness*. As of this writing, [EleutherAI's
lm-evaluation-harness](https://github.com/EleutherAI/lm-evaluation-harness/blob/master/docs/task_table.md){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} supports over 400
benchmarks. []{#ch04.html_id1074 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="OpenAI" secondary="evaluation harnesses"}[OpenAI's
evals](https://github.com/openai/evals){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} lets you run any of the approximately 500
existing benchmarks and register new benchmarks to evaluate OpenAI
models. Their benchmarks evaluate a wide range of capabilities, from
doing math and solving puzzles to identifying ASCII art that represents
words.

:::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Benchmark selection and aggregation"}
::::::::: {#ch04.html_ch04_benchmark_selection_and_aggregation_1730130866190150 .dedication}
#### Benchmark selection and aggregation {.calibre36}

[]{#ch04.html_id1075 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="model selection"
secondary="navigating public benchmarks"
tertiary="benchmark selection and aggregation"
data-type="indexterm"}Benchmark results help you identify promising
models for your use cases. []{#ch04.html_ch04.html32a .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
data-type="indexterm" primary="leaderboards"}Aggregating benchmark
results to rank models gives you a leaderboard. There are two questions
to consider:

- What benchmarks to include in your leaderboard?

- How to aggregate these benchmark results to rank models?

Given so many benchmarks out there, it's impossible to look at them all,
let alone aggregate their results to decide which model is the best.
Imagine that you're considering two models, A and B, for code
generation. If model A performs better than model B on a coding
benchmark but worse on a toxicity benchmark, which model would you
choose? Similarly, which model would you choose if one model performs
better in one coding benchmark but worse in another coding benchmark?

For inspiration on how to create your own leaderboard from public
benchmarks, it's useful to look into how public leaderboards do so.

::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Public leaderboards"}
:::: {#ch04.html_ch04_public_leaderboards_1730130866190233 .dedication}
##### Public leaderboards {.calibre47}

[]{#ch04.html_id1076 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="model selection"
secondary="navigating public benchmarks" tertiary="public leaderboards"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1077 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="public leaderboards" data-type="indexterm"}Many public
leaderboards rank models based on their aggregated performance on a
subset of benchmarks. These leaderboards are immensely helpful but far
from being comprehensive. First, due to the compute
constraint---evaluating a model on a benchmark requires compute---most
leaderboards can incorporate only a small number of benchmarks. Some
leaderboards might exclude an important but expensive benchmark. For
example, HELM (Holistic Evaluation of Language Models) Lite left out an
information retrieval benchmark (MS MARCO, Microsoft Machine Reading
Comprehension) because it's [expensive to
run](https://oreil.ly/7PFUy){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4}. Hugging Face opted out of HumanEval due to its [large
compute requirements](https://oreil.ly/pgGZ0){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}---you need to generate a lot of
completions.

When [Hugging Face first launched Open LLM Leaderboard in
2023](https://oreil.ly/-uhru){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, it consisted of four benchmarks. By the end of
that year, they extended it to six benchmarks. A small set of benchmarks
is not nearly enough to represent the vast capabilities and different
failure modes of foundation models.

Additionally, while leaderboard developers are generally thoughtful
about how they select benchmarks, their decision-making process isn't
always clear to users. Different leaderboards often end up with
different benchmarks, making it hard to compare and interpret their
rankings. For example, in late 2023, Hugging Face updated their Open LLM
Leaderboard to use the average of six different benchmarks to rank
models:

1.  []{#ch04.html_id1078 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="ARC-C"
    data-type="indexterm"}ARC-C ([Clark et al.,
    2018](https://arxiv.org/abs/1803.05457){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}): Measuring the ability to solve
    complex, grade school-level science questions.

2.  []{#ch04.html_id1079 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
    primary="Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU)"
    data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1080 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
    primary="MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding)"
    data-type="indexterm"}MMLU ([Hendrycks et al.,
    2020](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.03300){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}): Measuring knowledge and
    reasoning capabilities in 57 subjects, including elementary
    mathematics, US history, computer science, and law.

3.  []{#ch04.html_id1081 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="HellaSwag"
    data-type="indexterm"}HellaSwag ([Zellers et al.,
    2019](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.07830){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}): Measuring the ability to predict
    the completion of a sentence or a scene in a story or video. The
    goal is to test common sense and understanding of everyday
    activities.

4.  []{#ch04.html_id1082 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="TruthfulQA"
    data-type="indexterm"}TruthfulQA ([Lin et al.,
    2021](https://arxiv.org/abs/2109.07958){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}): Measuring the ability to
    generate responses that are not only accurate but also truthful and
    non-misleading, focusing on a model's understanding of facts.

5.  []{#ch04.html_id1083 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="WinoGrande"
    data-type="indexterm"}WinoGrande ([Sakaguchi et al.,
    2019](https://arxiv.org/abs/1907.10641){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}): Measuring the ability to solve
    challenging pronoun resolution problems that are designed to be
    difficult for language models, requiring sophisticated commonsense
    reasoning.

<!-- -->

6.  GSM-8K ([Grade School Math, OpenAI,
    2021](https://github.com/openai/grade-school-math){.pcalibre7
    .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}): Measuring the ability
    to solve a diverse set of math problems typically encountered in
    grade school curricula.

At around the same time, [Stanford's HELM
Leaderboard](https://oreil.ly/CQ52G){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} used ten benchmarks, only two of which (MMLU and
GSM-8K) were in the Hugging Face leaderboard. The other eight benchmarks
are:

- A benchmark for competitive math
  ([MATH](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.03874){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
  .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4})

- One each for legal ([LegalBench](https://oreil.ly/jCo7o){.pcalibre7
  .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), medical
  ([MedQA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.13081){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
  .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), and translation ([WMT
  2014](https://oreil.ly/bdGKm){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
  .calibre10 .pcalibre4})

- Two for reading comprehension---answering questions based on a book or
  a [long story]{.keep-together}
  ([NarrativeQA](https://arxiv.org/abs/1712.07040){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
  .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} and
  [OpenBookQA](https://arxiv.org/abs/1809.02789){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
  .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4})

- Two for general question answering ([Natural
  Questions](https://oreil.ly/QB4XP){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
  .calibre10 .pcalibre4} under two settings, with and without Wikipedia
  pages in the input)

Hugging Face explained they chose these benchmarks because "they test a
variety of reasoning and general knowledge across a wide variety of
fields."^[20](#ch04.html_id1084){#ch04.html_id1084-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ The
HELM website explained that their benchmark list was "inspired by the
simplicity" of the Hugging Face's leaderboard but with a broader set of
scenarios.

Public leaderboards, in general, try to balance coverage and the number
of benchmarks. They try to pick a small set of benchmarks that cover a
wide range of capabilities, typically including reasoning, factual
consistency, and domain-specific capabilities such as math and science.

At a high level, this makes sense. However, there's no clarity on what
coverage means or why it stops at six or ten benchmarks. For example,
why are medical and legal tasks included in HELM Lite but not general
science? Why does HELM Lite have two math tests but no coding? Why does
neither have tests for summarization, tool use, toxicity detection,
image search, etc.? These questions aren't meant to criticize these
public leaderboards but to highlight the challenge of selecting
benchmarks to rank models. If leaderboard developers can't explain their
benchmark selection processes, it might be because it's really hard to
do so.

An important aspect of benchmark selection that is often overlooked is
benchmark correlation. It is important because if two benchmarks are
perfectly correlated, you don't want both of them. Strongly correlated
benchmarks can exaggerate
biases.^[21](#ch04.html_id1085){#ch04.html_id1085-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

::: {.calibre22 .note data-type="note"}
####### Note {.calibre23}

While I was writing this book, many benchmarks became saturated or close
to being saturated. In June 2024, less than a [year
after]{.keep-together} [their leaderboard's]{.keep-together} last
revamp, Hugging Face updated their [leaderboard]{.keep-together} again
with an entirely new set of benchmarks that [are more]{.keep-together}
challenging and focus on more practical capabilities. [For
example,]{.keep-together} [GSM-8K was replaced by MATH lvl
5](https://x.com/polynoamial/status/1803812369237528825){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, which consists of the most
challenging questions from the competitive math benchmark
[MATH](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.03874){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. MMLU was replaced [by
MMLU-PRO]{.keep-together} ([Wang et al.,
2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.01574){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}). They also included the following benchmarks:

- GPQA ([Rein et al., 2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.12022){.pcalibre7
  .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}): a graduate-level Q&A
  benchmark^[22](#ch04.html_id1086){#ch04.html_id1086-marker .pcalibre7
  .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

- MuSR ([Sprague et al.,
  2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.16049){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
  .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}): a chain-of-thought, multistep
  reasoning [benchmark]{.keep-together}

- BBH (BIG-bench Hard) ([Srivastava et al.,
  2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.04615){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
  .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}): another reasoning benchmark

- IFEval ([Zhou et al.,
  2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.07911){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
  .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}): an instruction-following
  [benchmark]{.keep-together}

I have no doubt that these benchmarks will soon become saturated.
However, discussing specific benchmarks, even if outdated, can still be
useful as examples to evaluate and interpret
benchmarks.^[23](#ch04.html_id1087){#ch04.html_id1087-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^
:::

[Table 4-5](#ch04.html_ch04_table_5_1730130866138411){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows the
Pearson correlation scores among the six benchmarks used on Hugging
Face's leaderboard, computed in January 2024 by [Balázs
Galambosi](https://x.com/gblazex){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}. The three benchmarks WinoGrande, MMLU, and ARC-C
are strongly correlated, which makes sense since they all test reasoning
capabilities. TruthfulQA is only moderately correlated to other
benchmarks, suggesting that improving a model's reasoning and math
capabilities doesn't always improve its truthfulness.

               ARC-C        HellaSwag   MMLU         TruthfulQA   WinoGrande   GSM-8K
  ------------ ------------ ----------- ------------ ------------ ------------ --------
  ARC-C        1.0000       0.4812      **0.8672**   0.4809       **0.8856**   0.7438
  HellaSwag    0.4812       1.0000      0.6105       0.4809       0.4842       0.3547
  MMLU         0.8672       0.6105      1.0000       0.5507       **0.9011**   0.7936
  TruthfulQA   0.4809       0.4228      0.5507       1.0000       0.4550       0.5009
  WinoGrande   **0.8856**   0.4842      **0.9011**   0.4550       1.0000       0.7979
  GSM-8K       0.7438       0.3547      0.7936       0.5009       0.7979       1.0000

  : [Table 4-5. ]{.keep-together}The correlation between the six
  benchmarks used on Hugging Face's leaderboard, computed in January
  2024. {#ch04_table_5_1730130866138411 .stafflist_table}

The results from all the selected benchmarks need to be aggregated to
rank models. As of this writing, Hugging Face averages a model's scores
on all these benchmarks to get the final score to rank that model.
Averaging means treating all benchmark scores equally, i.e., treating an
80% score on TruthfulQA the same as an 80% score on GSM-8K, even if an
80% score on TruthfulQA might be much harder to achieve than an 80%
score on GSM-8K. This also means giving all benchmarks the same weight,
even if, for some tasks, truthfulness might weigh a lot more than being
able to solve grade school math problems.

[HELM authors](https://oreil.ly/MLlDD){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, on the other hand, decided to shun averaging in
favor of mean win rate, which they defined as "the fraction of times a
model obtains a better score than another model, averaged across
scenarios".

While public leaderboards are useful to get a sense of models' broad
performance, it's important to understand what capabilities a
leaderboard is trying to capture. A model that ranks high on a public
leaderboard will likely, but far from always, perform well for your
application. If you want a model for code generation, a public
leaderboard that doesn't include a code generation benchmark might not
help you as much.
::::
:::::

::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Custom leaderboards with public benchmarks"}
:::: {#ch04.html_ch04_custom_leaderboards_with_public_benchmarks_1730130866190306 .dedication}
##### Custom leaderboards with public benchmarks {.calibre47}

When evaluating models for a specific application, you're basically
creating a private leaderboard that ranks models based on your
evaluation criteria. The first step is to gather a list of benchmarks
that [evaluate]{.keep-together} the capabilities important to your
application. If you want to build a coding agent, look at code-related
benchmarks. If you build a writing assistant, look into creative writing
benchmarks. As new benchmarks are constantly introduced and old
[benchmarks]{.keep-together} become saturated, you should look for the
latest benchmarks. Make sure to evaluate how reliable a benchmark is.
Because anyone can create and publish a benchmark, many benchmarks might
not be measuring what you expect them to measure.

<aside data-type="sidebar" epub:type="sidebar" class="calibre20">

::: {#ch04.html_ch04_are_openai_s_models_getting_worse_1730130866190378 .sidebar}
## Are OpenAI's Models Getting Worse? {.calibre21}

[]{#ch04.html_id1088 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="systems evaluation"
secondary="OpenAI model quality"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1089 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
data-type="indexterm" primary="OpenAI"
secondary="quality of updated models"}Every time OpenAI updates its
models, people complain that their models seem to be getting worse. For
example, a study by Stanford and UC Berkeley ([Chen et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.09009){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}) found that for many benchmarks, both GPT-3.5 and
GPT-4's performances changed significantly between March 2023 and June
2023, as shown in
[Figure 4-9](#ch04.html_ch04_figure_9_1730130866113721){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

<figure class="width">
<div id="ch04.html_ch04_figure_9_1730130866113721" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0409.png" class="calibre60 pcalibre8"
alt="A screenshot of a graph Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre61"><span class="keep-together">Figure 4-9.
</span>Changes in the performances of GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 from March 2023
to June 2023 on certain benchmarks (Chen et al., 2023).</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Assuming that OpenAI doesn't intentionally release worse models, what
might be the reason for this perception? One potential reason is that
evaluation is hard, and no one, not even OpenAI, knows for sure if a
model is getting better or worse. While evaluation is definitely hard, I
doubt that OpenAI would fly completely
blind.^[24](#ch04.html_id1090){#ch04.html_id1090-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ If the
second reason is true, it reinforces the idea that the best model
overall might not be the best model for your
[application.]{.keep-together}
:::

</aside>

Not all models have publicly available scores on all benchmarks. If the
model you care about doesn't have a publicly available score on your
benchmark, you will need to run the evaluation
yourself.^[25](#ch04.html_id1091){#ch04.html_id1091-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^
Hopefully, an evaluation harness can help you with that. Running
benchmarks can be expensive. For example, Stanford spent approximately
\$80,000--\$100,000 to evaluate 30 models on their [full HELM
suite](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.09110){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4}.^[26](#ch04.html_id1092){#ch04.html_id1092-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ The
more models you want to evaluate and the more benchmarks you want to
use, the more expensive it gets.

Once you've selected a set of benchmarks and obtained the scores for the
models you care about on these benchmarks, you then need to aggregate
these scores to rank models. Not all benchmark scores are in the same
unit or scale. One benchmark might use accuracy, another F1, and another
BLEU score. You will need to think about how important each benchmark is
to you and weigh their scores accordingly.[]{#ch04.html_id1093
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch04.html32a"
data-type="indexterm"}

As you evaluate models using public benchmarks, keep in mind that the
goal of this process is to select a small subset of models to do more
rigorous experiments using your own benchmarks and metrics. This is not
only because public benchmarks are unlikely to represent your
application's needs perfectly, but also because they are likely
contaminated. How public benchmarks get contaminated and how to handle
data contamination will be the topic of the next
section.[]{#ch04.html_id1094 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch04.html32"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1095 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch04.html31a" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1096
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch04.html31"
data-type="indexterm"}
::::
:::::
:::::::::
::::::::::

:::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Data contamination with public benchmarks"}
::::::: {#ch04.html_ch04_data_contamination_with_public_benchmarks_1730130866190550 .dedication}
#### Data contamination with public benchmarks {.calibre36}

[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html33 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="data contamination"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html34 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="systems evaluation" secondary="model selection"
tertiary="data contamination with public benchmarks"
data-type="indexterm"}Data contamination is so common that there are
many different names for it, including *data leakage*, *training on the
test set*, or simply *cheating*. *Data contamination* happens when a
model was trained on the same data it's evaluated on. If so, it's
possible that the model just memorizes the answers it saw during
training, causing it to achieve higher evaluation scores than it should.
A model that is trained on the MMLU benchmark can achieve high MMLU
scores without being useful.

Rylan Schaeffer, a PhD student at Stanford, demonstrated this
beautifully in his 2023 satirical paper ["Pretraining on the Test Set Is
All You Need"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.08632){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. By training exclusively on data from
several benchmarks, his one-million-parameter model was able to achieve
near-perfect scores and outperformed much larger models on all these
benchmarks.

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="How data contamination happens"}
::: {#ch04.html_ch04_how_data_contamination_happens_1730130866190612 .dedication}
##### How data contamination happens {.calibre47}

While some might intentionally train on benchmark data to achieve
misleadingly high scores, most data contamination is unintentional. Many
models today are trained on data scraped from the internet, and the
scraping process can accidentally pull data from publicly available
benchmarks. Benchmark data published before the training of a model is
likely included in the model's training
data.^[27](#ch04.html_id1097){#ch04.html_id1097-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ It's
one of the reasons existing benchmarks become saturated so quickly, and
why model developers often feel the need to create new benchmarks to
evaluate their new models.

Data contamination can happen indirectly, such as when both evaluation
and training data come from the same source. For example, you might
include math textbooks in the training data to improve the model's math
capabilities, and someone else might use questions from the same math
textbooks to create a benchmark to evaluate the model's capabilities.

Data contamination can also happen intentionally for good reasons. Let's
say you want to create the best possible model for your users.
Initially, you exclude benchmark data from the model's training data and
choose the best model based on these benchmarks. However, because
high-quality benchmark data can improve the model's performance, you
then continue training your best model on benchmark data before
releasing it to your users. So the released model is contaminated, and
your users won't be able to evaluate it on contaminated benchmarks, but
this might still be the right thing to do.
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Handling data contamination"}
::: {#ch04.html_ch04_handling_data_contamination_1730130866190669 .dedication}
##### Handling data contamination {.calibre47}

The prevalence of data contamination undermines the trustworthiness of
evaluation benchmarks. Just because a model can achieve high performance
on bar exams doesn't mean it's good at giving legal advice. It could
just be that this model has been trained on many bar exam questions.

To deal with data contamination, you first need to detect the
contamination, and then decontaminate your data. You can detect
contamination using heuristics like n-gram overlapping and perplexity:

N-gram overlapping

:   For example, if a sequence of 13 tokens in an evaluation sample is
    also in the training data, the model has likely seen this evaluation
    sample during training. This evaluation sample is considered
    *dirty*.

<!-- -->

Perplexity

:   Recall that perplexity measures how difficult it is for a model to
    predict a given text. If a model's perplexity on evaluation data is
    unusually low, meaning the model can easily predict the text, it's
    possible that the model has seen this data before during training.

The n-gram overlapping approach is more accurate but can be
time-consuming and expensive to run because you have to compare each
benchmark example with the entire training data. It's also impossible
without access to the training data. The perplexity approach is less
accurate but much less resource-intensive.

In the past, ML textbooks advised removing evaluation samples from the
training data. The goal is to keep evaluation benchmarks standardized so
that we can compare different models. However, with foundation models,
most people don't have control over training data. Even if we have
control over training data, we might not want to remove all benchmark
data from the training data, because high-quality benchmark data can
help improve the overall model performance. Besides, there will always
be benchmarks created after models are trained, so there will always be
contaminated evaluation samples.

For model developers, a common practice is to remove benchmarks they
care about from their training data before training their models.
Ideally, when reporting your model performance on a benchmark, it's
helpful to disclose what percentage of this benchmark data is in your
training data, and what the model's performance is on both the overall
benchmark and the clean samples of the benchmark. Sadly, because
detecting and removing contamination takes effort, many people find it
easier to just skip it.

OpenAI, when analyzing GPT-3's contamination with common benchmarks,
found 13 benchmarks with at least 40% in the training data ([Brown et
al., 2020](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}). The relative difference in
performance between evaluating only the clean sample and evaluating the
whole benchmark is shown in
[Figure 4-10](#ch04.html_ch04_figure_10_1730130866113746){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch04.html_ch04_figure_10_1730130866113746" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0410.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A table of numbers with text Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 4-10.
</span>Relative difference in GPT-3’s performance when evaluating using
only the clean sample compared to evaluating using the whole
benchmark.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

To combat data contamination, leaderboard hosts like Hugging Face plot
standard deviations of models' performance on a given benchmark [to spot
outliers](https://oreil.ly/LghFT){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}. Public benchmarks should keep part of their data
private and provide a tool for model developers to automatically
evaluate models against the private hold-out data.

Public benchmarks will help you filter out bad models, but they won't
help you find the best models for your application. After using public
benchmarks to narrow them to a set of promising models, you'll need to
run your own evaluation pipeline to find the best one for your
application. How to design a custom evaluation pipeline will be our next
topic[]{#ch04.html_id1098 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch04.html34"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1099 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch04.html33" data-type="indexterm"}.[]{#ch04.html_id1100
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch04.html20"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1101 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch04.html19" data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect1" pdf-bookmark="Design Your Evaluation Pipeline"}
:::::::::::::::::::::::: {#ch04.html_ch04_design_your_evaluation_pipeline_1730130866190749 .dedication}
## Design Your Evaluation Pipeline {.calibre4}

[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html35 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="evaluation pipeline design"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html36 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="systems evaluation" secondary="evaluation pipeline design"
data-type="indexterm"}The success of an AI application often hinges on
the ability to differentiate good outcomes from bad outcomes. To be able
to do this, you need an evaluation pipeline that you can rely upon. With
an explosion of evaluation methods and techniques, it can be confusing
to pick the right combination for your evaluation pipeline. This section
focuses on evaluating open-ended tasks. Evaluating close-ended tasks is
easier, and its pipeline can be inferred from this process.

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Step 1. Evaluate All Components in a System"}
::: {#ch04.html_ch04_step_1_evaluate_all_components_in_a_system_1730130866190824 .dedication}
### Step 1. Evaluate All Components in a System {.calibre35}

[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html38 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="evaluation pipeline design"
secondary="step 2: evaluating all components in a system"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html39 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="system components evaluation"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html40 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="systems evaluation" secondary="evaluation pipeline design"
tertiary="step 2: evaluating all components in a system"
data-type="indexterm"}Real-world AI applications are complex. Each
application might consist of many components, and a task might be
completed after many turns. Evaluation can happen at different levels:
per task, per turn, and per intermediate output.

You should evaluate the end-to-end output and each component's
intermediate output independently. Consider an application that extracts
a person's current employer from their resume PDF, which works in two
steps:

1.  Extract all the text from the PDF.

2.  Extract the current employer from the extracted text.

If the model fails to extract the right current employer, it can be
because of either step. If you don't evaluate each component
independently, you don't know exactly where your system fails. The first
PDF-to-text step can be evaluated using similarity between the extracted
text and the ground truth text. The second step can be evaluated using
accuracy: given the correctly extracted text, how often does the
application correctly extract the current employer?

If applicable, evaluate your application both per turn and per task. A
turn can consist of multiple steps and messages. If a system takes
multiple steps to generate an output, it's still considered a turn.

Generative AI applications, especially chatbot-like applications, allow
back-and-forth between the user and the application, as in a
conversation, to accomplish a task. Imagine you want to use an AI model
to debug why your Python code is failing. The model responds by asking
for more information about your hardware or the Python version you're
using. Only after you've provided this information can the model help
you debug.

[]{#ch04.html_id1102 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="task-based evaluation"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1103 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="turn-based evaluation" data-type="indexterm"}*Turn-based*
evaluation evaluates the quality of each output. *Task-based* evaluation
evaluates whether a system completes a task. Did the application help
you fix the bug? How many turns did it take to complete the task? It
makes a big difference if a system is able to solve a problem in two
turns or in twenty turns.

Given that what users really care about is whether a model can help them
accomplish their tasks, task-based evaluation is more important.
However, a challenge of task-based evaluation is it can be hard to
determine the boundaries between tasks. Imagine a conversation you have
with ChatGPT. You might ask multiple questions at the same time. When
you send a new query, is this a follow-up to an existing task or a new
task?

One example of task-based evaluation is the
`twenty_questions`{.calibre26} benchmark, inspired by the classic game
Twenty Questions, in the [BIG-bench benchmark
suite](https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.04615){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. One instance of the model (Alice)
chooses a concept, such as apple, car, or computer. Another instance of
the model (Bob) asks Alice a series of questions to try to identify this
concept. Alice can only answer yes or no. The score is based on whether
Bob successfully guesses the concept, and how many questions it takes
for Bob to guess it. Here's an example of a plausible conversation in
this task, taken from the [BIG-bench's GitHub
repository](https://github.com/google/BIG-bench/blob/main/bigbench/benchmark_tasks/twenty_questions/README.md){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}:

> `Bob: Is the concept an animal?`{.calibre26}
>
> `Alice: No.`{.calibre26}
>
> `Bob: Is the concept a plant?`{.calibre26}
>
> `Alice: Yes.`{.calibre26}
>
> `Bob: Does it grow in the ocean?`{.calibre26}
>
> `Alice: No.`{.calibre26}
>
> `Bob: Does it grow in a tree?`{.calibre26}
>
> `Alice: Yes.`{.calibre26}
>
> `Bob: Is it an apple?`{.calibre26}
>
> `[Bob’s guess is correct, and the task is completed.]`{.calibre26}[]{#ch04.html_id1104
> .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
> contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch04.html40"
> data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1105 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
> .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
> startref="ch04.html39" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_id1106
> .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
> contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch04.html38"
> data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::

:::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Step 2. Create an Evaluation Guideline "}
::::::::: {#ch04.html_ch04_step_2_create_an_evaluation_guideline_1730130866190887 .dedication}
### Step 2. Create an Evaluation Guideline {.calibre35}

[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html41 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="evaluation pipeline design"
secondary="step 1: creating an evaluation guideline"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch04.html_ch04.html42 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="systems evaluation" secondary="evaluation pipeline design"
tertiary="step 1: creating an evaluation guideline"
