:::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_memory_needed_for_training_1730159634259460 .dedication}
#### Memory needed for training {.calibre36}

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html17 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="memory bottlenecks"
secondary="memory math" tertiary="memory needed for training"
data-type="indexterm"}To train a model, you need memory for the model's
weights and activations, which has already been discussed. Additionally,
you need memory for gradients and optimizer states, which scales with
the number of trainable parameters.

Overall, the memory needed for training is calculated as:

- Training memory = model weights + activations + gradients + optimizer
  states

::: {.calibre22 data-type="tip"}
####### Tip {.calibre23}

During the backward pass, each trainable parameter requires one value
for gradient plus zero to two values for optimizer states, depending on
the optimizer:

- A vanilla SGD optimizer has no state.

- A momentum optimizer stores one value per trainable
  [parameter.]{.keep-together}

- An Adam optimizer stores two values per trainable parameter.
:::

Imagine you're updating all parameters in a 13B-parameter model using
the Adam optimizer. Because each trainable parameter has three values
for its gradient and optimizer states, if it takes two bytes to store
each value, the memory needed for gradients and optimizer states will
be:

``` {.calibre51 data-type="programlisting"}
13 billion × 3 × 2 bytes = 78 GB
```

However, if you only have 1B trainable parameters, the memory needed for
gradients and optimizer states will be only:

``` {.calibre51 data-type="programlisting"}
1 billion × 3 × 2 bytes = 6 GB
```

One important thing to note is that in the previous formula, I assumed
that the memory needed for activations is less than the memory needed
for the model's weights. However, in reality, the activation memory can
be much larger. If activations are stored for gradient computation, the
memory needed for activations can dwarf the memory needed for the
model's weights.
[Figure 7-5](#ch07.html_ch07b_figure_2_1730159634220278){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows the
memory needed for activations compared to the memory needed for the
model's weights for different Megatron models at different scales,
according to the paper ["Reducing Activation Recomputation in Large
Transformer Models"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.05198){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, by Korthikanti et al.
(2022).

One way to reduce the memory needed for activations is not to store
them. Instead of storing activations for reuse, you recompute
activations when necessary. This technique is called *gradient
checkpointing* or *activation recomputation*. While this reduces the
memory requirements, it increases the time needed for training due to
the [recomputation]{.keep-together}[]{#ch07.html_id1391 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="" startref="ch07.html17"
data-type="indexterm"}.[]{#ch07.html_id1392 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html16" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1393
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html15"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1394 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html14"
data-type="indexterm"}^[10](#ch07.html_id1395){#ch07.html_id1395-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch07.html_ch07b_figure_2_1730159634220278" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0705.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A graph of a graph Description automatically generated with medium confidence" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 7-5. </span>The
memory needed for activations can dwarf the memory needed for the
model’s weights. Image from Korthikanti et al., 2022.</h6>
</div>
</figure>
::::
:::::
:::::::::
::::::::::

::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Numerical Representations"}
:::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_numerical_representations_1730159634259493 .dedication}
### Numerical Representations {.calibre35}

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html18 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="finetuning"
secondary="memory bottlenecks" tertiary="numerical representations"
data-type="indexterm"}In the memory calculation so far, I've assumed
that each value takes up two bytes of memory. The memory required to
represent each value in a model contributes directly to the model's
overall memory footprint. If you reduce the memory needed for each value
by half, the memory needed for the model's weights is also reduced by
half.

Before discussing how to reduce the memory needed for each value, it's
useful to understand numerical representations. Numerical values in
neural networks are traditionally represented as [float
numbers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Floating-point_arithmetic){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. The most common family of
floating point formats is the FP family, which adheres to the Institute
of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) standard for
Floating-Point Arithmetic ([IEEE
754](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IEEE_754){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}):

- FP32 uses 32 bits (4 bytes) to represent a float. This format is
  called single [precision.]{.keep-together}

- FP64 uses 64 bits (8 bytes) and is called double precision.

- FP16 uses 16 bits (2 bytes) and is called half precision.

While FP64 is still used in many computations---as of this writing, FP64
is the default format for NumPy and pandas---it's rarely used in neural
networks because of its memory footprint. FP32 and FP16 are more common.
Other popular floating point formats in AI workloads include *BF16*
(BFloat16) and *TF32* (TensorFloat-32). BF16 was designed by Google to
optimize AI performance on [TPUs](https://oreil.ly/BGXtn){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} and TF32 was designed by
NVIDIA for [GPUs](https://oreil.ly/0pZgw){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4}.^[11](#ch07.html_id1396){#ch07.html_id1396-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

Numbers can also be represented as integers. Even though not yet as
common as floating formats, integer representations are becoming
increasingly popular. Common integer formats are INT8 (8-bit integers)
and INT4 (4-bit
integers).^[12](#ch07.html_id1397){#ch07.html_id1397-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

Each float format usually has 1 bit to represent the number's sign,
i.e., negative or positive. The rest of the bits are split between
*range* and
*precision*:^[13](#ch07.html_id1398){#ch07.html_id1398-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

Range

:   []{#ch07.html_id1399 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="range bits"
    data-type="indexterm"}The number of range bits determines the range
    of values the format can represent. More bits means a wider range.
    This is similar to how having more digits lets you represent a wider
    range of numbers.

Precision

:   []{#ch07.html_id1400 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="precision bits"
    data-type="indexterm"}The number of precision bits determines how
    precisely a number can be represented. Reducing the number of
    precision bits makes a number less precise. For example, if you
    convert 10.1234 to a format that can support only two decimal
    digits, this value becomes 10.12, which is less precise than the
    original value.

[Figure 7-6](#ch07.html_ch07b_figure_3_1730159634220288){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows
different floating point formats along with their range and precision
bits.^[14](#ch07.html_id1401){#ch07.html_id1401-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch07.html_ch07b_figure_3_1730159634220288" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0706.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A graph with numbers and text Description automatically generated with medium confidence" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 7-6.
</span>Different numerical formats with their range and precision.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Formats with more bits are considered *higher precision*. Converting a
number with a high-precision format into a low-precision format (e.g.,
from FP32 to FP16) means *reducing its precision*. Reducing precision
can cause a value to change or result in errors.
[Table 7-3](#ch07.html_ch07b_table_1_1730159634233580){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows how
FP32 values can be converted into FP16, BF16, and TF32.

+----------------+-------------------------------------------------------+----------------+----------------------+
| FP32           | FP16                                                  | BF16           | TF32                 |
+================+=======================================================+================+======================+
| 0.0123456789   | 0.01234*43603515625*                                  | 0.0123*291*    | 0.01234*43603515625* |
+----------------+-------------------------------------------------------+----------------+----------------------+
| 0.123456789    | 0.1234*7412109375*                                    | 0.123*535*     | 0.1234*130859375*    |
+----------------+-------------------------------------------------------+----------------+----------------------+
| 1.23456789     | 1.234*375*                                            | 1.234*38*      | 1.234*375*           |
+----------------+-------------------------------------------------------+----------------+----------------------+
| 12.3456789     | 12.34*375*                                            | 12.3*75*       | 12.34*375*           |
+----------------+-------------------------------------------------------+----------------+----------------------+
| 123.456789     | 123.4*375*                                            | 123.*5*        | 123.4*375*           |
+----------------+-------------------------------------------------------+----------------+----------------------+
| 1234.56789     | 123*5.0*                                              | 123*2.0*       | 1234.*0*             |
+----------------+-------------------------------------------------------+----------------+----------------------+
| 12345.6789     | 1234*4.0*                                             | 123*52.0*      | 1234*4.0*            |
+----------------+-------------------------------------------------------+----------------+----------------------+
| 123456.789     | *INF*^[a](#ch07.html_id1402){#ch07.html_id1402-marker | 123*392.0*     | 123456.*0*           |
|                | .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10           |                |                      |
|                | .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^                      |                |                      |
+----------------+-------------------------------------------------------+----------------+----------------------+
| 1234567.89     | *INF*                                                 | 123*6990.0*    | 123*3920.0*          |
+----------------+-------------------------------------------------------+----------------+----------------------+
| ^[a](#ch07.html_id1402-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ Values out of bound in |
| FP16 are rounded to infinity.                                                                                  |
+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

: [Table 7-3. ]{.keep-together}Convert from FP32 values to
lower-precision formats. Resultant inaccuracies are in italics.
{#ch07b_table_1_1730159634233580 .stafflist_table}

Note in
[Table 7-3](#ch07.html_ch07b_table_1_1730159634233580){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} that even
though BF16 and FP16 have the same number of bits, BF16 has more bits
for range and fewer bits for precision. This allows BF16 to represent
large values that are out-of-bound for FP16. However, this also makes
BF16 less precise than FP16. For example, 1234.56789 is 1235.0 in FP16
(0.035% value change) but 1232.0 in BF16 (0.208% value change).

::: {.calibre22 .warning data-type="warning"}
####### Warning {.calibre29}

When using a model, make sure to load the model in the format it's
intended for. Loading a model into the wrong numerical [format
can]{.keep-together} cause the model to change significantly. For
example, Llama 2 had its weights set to BF16 when it came out. However,
many teams loaded the model in FP16 and were subsequently frustrated to
find the model's quality much worse than advertised[]{#ch07.html_id1403
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html18"
data-type="indexterm"}.^[15](#ch07.html_id1404){#ch07.html_id1404-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^ While this misunderstanding wasted a lot of
people's time, the upside is that it forced many people to learn about
numerical [representations.]{.keep-together}
:::

The right format for you depends on the distribution of numerical values
of your workload (such as the range of values you need), how sensitive
your workload is to small numerical changes, and the underlying
hardware.^[16](#ch07.html_id1405){#ch07.html_id1405-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^
::::
:::::

::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Quantization"}
:::::::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_quantization_1730159634259532 .dedication}
### Quantization {.calibre35}

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html19 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="finetuning"
secondary="memory bottlenecks" tertiary="quantization"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html20 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="memory bottlenecks" secondary="quantization"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html21 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="quantization" data-type="indexterm"}The fewer bits needed to
represent a model's values, the lower the model's memory footprint will
be. A 10B-parameter model in a 32-bit format requires 40 GB for its
weights, but the same model in a 16-bit format will require only 20 GB.
Reducing precision, also known as quantization, is a cheap and extremely
effective way to reduce a model's memory footprint. It's straightforward
to do and generalizes over tasks and architectures. In the context of
ML, low precision generally refers to any format with fewer bits than
the standard FP32.

<aside data-type="sidebar" epub:type="sidebar" class="calibre20">

::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_quantization_versus_reduced_precision_1730159634259565 .sidebar}
## Quantization Versus Reduced Precision {.calibre21}

Strictly speaking, it's quantization only if the target format is
integer. However, in practice, quantization is used to refer to all
techniques that convert values to a lower-precision format. In this
book, I use quantization to refer to precision reduction, to keep it
consistent with the literature.
:::

</aside>

To do quantization, you need to decide what to quantize and when:

What to quantize

:   Ideally, you want to quantize whatever is consuming most of your
    memory, but it also depends on what you can quantize without hurting
    performance too much. As discussed in ["Memory
    Math"](#ch07.html_ch07b_memory_math_1730159634259402){.pcalibre7
    .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}, major
    contributors to a model's memory footprint during inference are the
    model's weights and
    activations.^[17](#ch07.html_id1406){#ch07.html_id1406-marker
    .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
    data-type="noteref"}^ Weight quantization is more common than
    activation quantization, since weight activation tends to have a
    more stable impact on performance with less accuracy loss.

When to quantize

:   Quantization can happen during training or post-training.
    Post-training quantization (PTQ) means quantizing a model after it's
    been fully trained. PTQ is by far the most common. It's also more
    relevant to AI application developers who don't usually train
    models.

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Inference quantization"}
::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_inference_quantization_1730159634259617 .dedication}
#### Inference quantization {.calibre36}

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html22 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="inference quantization"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html23 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="memory bottlenecks" secondary="quantization"
tertiary="inference quantization"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html24 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="quantization" secondary="inference quantization"
data-type="indexterm"}In the early days of deep learning, it was
standard to train and serve models using 32 bits with FP32. Since the
late 2010s, it has become increasingly common to serve models in 16 bits
and in even lower precision. For example, [Dettmers et al.
(2022)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2208.07339){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} have done excellent work quantizing
LLMs into 8 bits with LLM.int8() and 4 bits with QLoRA ([Dettmers et
al., 2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.14314){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}).

A model can also be served in *mixed precision*, where values are
reduced in precision when possible and maintained in higher precision
when necessary. To serve models on the devices,
[Apple](https://oreil.ly/lqLfv){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} (2024) leveraged a quantization scheme that uses
a mixture of 2-bit and 4-bit formats, averaging 3.5 bits-per-weight.
Also in 2024, in anticipation of 4-bit neural networks, NVIDIA announced
their new GPU architecture,
[Blackwell](https://oreil.ly/FIP9V){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, that supports model inference in 4-bit float.

Once you get to 8 bits and under, numerical representations get more
tricky. You can keep parameter values as floats using one of the
[minifloat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minifloat){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} formats, such as FP8 (8
bits) and FP4 (4 bits).^[18](#ch07.html_id1407){#ch07.html_id1407-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^ More commonly, however, parameter values are
converted into an integer format, such as INT8 or INT4.

Quantization is effective, but there's a limit to how far it can go. You
can't have fewer than 1 bit per value, and some have attempted the 1-bit
representation, e.g., BinaryConnect ([Courbariaux et al.,
2015](https://arxiv.org/abs/1511.00363){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), Xnor-Net ([Rastegari et al.,
2016](https://arxiv.org/abs/1603.05279){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), and BitNet ([Wang et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.11453){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10
.pcalibre4}).^[19](#ch07.html_id1408){#ch07.html_id1408-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^

In 2024, Microsoft researchers ([Ma et
al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.17764){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}) declared that we're entering the era of 1-bit
LLMs by introducing BitNet b1.58, a transformer-based language model
that requires only 1.58 bits per parameter and whose performance is
comparable to 16-bit []{#ch07.html_id1409 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
data-type="indexterm" primary="Llama"
secondary="inference quantization"}Llama 2 ([Touvron et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.09288){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}) up to 3.9B parameters, as shown in
[Table 7-4](#ch07.html_ch07b_table_2_1730159634233604){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

  Model          Size   ARCe   ARCc   HS     BQ     OQ     PQ     WGe    Avg.
  -------------- ------ ------ ------ ------ ------ ------ ------ ------ ------
  Llama LLM      700M   54.7   23.0   37.0   60.0   20.2   68.9   54.8   45.5
  BitNet b1.58   700M   51.8   21.4   35.1   58.2   20.0   68.1   55.2   44.3
  Llama LLM      1.3B   56.9   23.5   38.5   59.1   21.6   70.0   53.9   46.2
  BitNet b1.58   1.3B   54.9   24.2   37.7   56.7   19.6   68.8   55.8   45.4
  Llama LLM      3B     62.1   25.6   43.3   61.8   24.6   72.1   58.2   49.7
  BitNet b1.58   3B     61.4   28.3   42.9   61.5   26.6   71.5   59.3   50.2
  BitNet b1.58   3.9B   64.2   28.7   44.2   63.5   24.2   73.2   60.5   51.2

  : [Table 7-4. ]{.keep-together}BitNet b1.58's performance compared to
  that of Llama 2 16-bit on different benchmarks and at different model
  sizes, up to 3.9B parameters. Results from Ma et al. (2024).
  {#ch07b_table_2_1730159634233604 .stafflist_table}

Reduced precision not only reduces the memory footprint but also often
improves computation speed. First, it allows a larger batch size,
enabling the model to process more inputs in parallel. Second, reduced
precision speeds up computation, which further reduces inference latency
and training time. To illustrate this, consider the addition of two
numbers. If we perform the addition bit by bit, and each takes *t*
nanoseconds, it'll take *32t* nanoseconds for 32 bits but only *16t*
nanoseconds for 16 bits. However, reducing precision doesn't always
reduce latency due to the added computation needed for format
conversion.

There are downsides to reduced precision. Each conversion often causes a
small value change, and many small changes can cause a big performance
change. If a value is outside the range the reduced precision format can
represent, it might be converted to infinity or an arbitrary value,
causing the model's quality to further degrade. How to reduce precision
with minimal impact on model performance is an active area of research,
pursued by model developers as well as by hardware makers and
application developers.

Inference in lower precision has become a standard. A model is trained
using a higher-precision format to maximize performance, then its
precision is reduced for inference. Major ML frameworks, including
PyTorch, TensorFlow, and Hugging Face's transformers, offer PTQ for free
with a few lines of code.

Some edge devices only support quantized inference. Therefore,
frameworks for on-device inference, such as TensorFlow Lite and PyTorch
Mobile, also offer PTQ.[]{#ch07.html_id1410 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html24" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1411
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html23"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1412 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html22" data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Training quantization"}
::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_training_quantization_1730159634259647 .dedication}
#### Training quantization {.calibre36}

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html25 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="memory bottlenecks"
secondary="quantization" tertiary="training quantization"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html26 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="quantization" secondary="training quantization"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html27 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="training quantization" data-type="indexterm"}Quantization
during training is not yet as common as PTQ, but it's gaining traction.
There are two distinct goals for training quantization:

1.  To produce a model that can perform well in low precision during
    inference. This is to address the challenge that a model's quality
    might degrade during post-training quantization.

2.  To reduce training time and cost. Quantization reduces a model's
    memory footprint, allowing a model to be trained on cheaper hardware
    or allowing the training of a larger model on the same hardware.
    Quantization also speeds up computation, which further reduces
    costs.

A quantization technique might help achieve one or both of these goals.

[]{#ch07.html_id1413 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="QAT (quantization-aware training)"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1414 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="quantization-aware training (QAT)"
data-type="indexterm"}Quantization-aware training (QAT) aims to create a
model with high quality in low precision for inference. With QAT, the
model simulates low-precision (e.g., 8-bit) behavior during training,
which allows the model to learn to produce high-quality outputs in low
precision. However, QAT doesn't reduce a model's training time since its
computations are still performed in high precision. QAT can even
increase training time due to the extra work of simulating low-precision
behavior.

On the other hand, training a model directly in lower precision can help
with both goals. People attempted to train models in reduced precision
as early as 2016; see [Hubara et al.
(2016)](https://oreil.ly/D-wIG){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} and [Jacob et al.
(2017)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1712.05877){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. [Character.AI
(2024)](https://oreil.ly/J7kVB){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} shared that they were able to train their models
entirely in INT8, which helped eliminate the training/serving precision
mismatch while also significantly improving training
[efficiency.]{.keep-together} However, training in lower precision is
harder to do, as backpropgation is more sensitive to lower
precision.^[20](#ch07.html_id1415){#ch07.html_id1415-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

Lower-precision training is often done in [*mixed
precision*](https://oreil.ly/pBaQM){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, where a copy of the weights is kept in higher
precision but other values, such as [gradients]{.keep-together} and
activations, are kept in lower
precision.^[21](#ch07.html_id1416){#ch07.html_id1416-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ You
can also have less-sensitive weight values computed in lower precision
and more-sensitive weight values computed in higher precision. For
example, LLM-QAT ([Liu et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.17888){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}) quantizes weights and activations into 4 bits
but keeps embeddings in 16 bits.

The portions of the model that should be in lower precision can be set
automatically using the [*automatic mixed
precision*](https://oreil.ly/JZRsd){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}[]{#ch07.html_id1417 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="AMP (automatic mixed precision)"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1418 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="automatic mixed precision (AMP)" data-type="indexterm"} (AMP)
functionality offered by many ML frameworks.

It's also possible to have different phases of training in different
precision levels. For example, a model can be trained in higher
precision but finetuned in lower precision. This is especially common
with foundation models, where the team training a model from scratch
might be an organization with sufficient compute for higher precision
training. Once the model is published, developers with less compute
access can finetune that model in lower []{#ch07.html_id1419 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="" startref="ch07.html27"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1420 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html26" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1421
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html25"
data-type="indexterm"}precision[]{#ch07.html_id1422 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="" startref="ch07.html21"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1423 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html20" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1424
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html19"
data-type="indexterm"}.[]{#ch07.html_id1425 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html10" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1426
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html9"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1427 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html8" data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::
::::::::
:::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect1" pdf-bookmark="Finetuning Techniques"}
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_finetuning_techniques_1730159634259695 .dedication}
## Finetuning Techniques {.calibre4}

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html28 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="finetuning"
secondary="techniques" data-type="indexterm"}I hope that the previous
section has made clear why finetuning large-scale models is so
memory-intensive. The more memory finetuning requires, the fewer people
who can afford to do it. Techniques that reduce a model's memory
footprint make finetuning more accessible, allowing more people to adapt
models to their applications. This section focuses on memory-efficient
finetuning techniques, which centers around parameter-efficient
finetuning.

I'll also cover model merging, an exciting but more experimental
approach to creating custom models. While model merging is generally not
considered finetuning, I include it in this section because it's
complementary to finetuning. Finetuning tailors one model to specific
needs. Model merging combines multiple models, often finetuned models,
for the same purpose.

While combining multiple models isn't a new concept, new types of models
and finetuning techniques have inspired many creative model-merging
techniques, making this section especially fun to write about.

::::::::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Parameter-Efficient Finetuning"}
:::::::::::::::::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_parameter_efficient_finetuning_1730159634259757 .dedication}
### Parameter-Efficient Finetuning {.calibre35}

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html29 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="finetuning"
secondary="techniques" tertiary="parameter-efficient finetuning"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html30 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="full finetuning"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html31 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="parameter-efficient finetuning" data-type="indexterm"}In the
early days of finetuning, models were small enough that people could
finetune entire models. This approach is called *full finetuning*. In
full finetuning, the number of trainable parameters is exactly the same
as the number of parameters.

Full finetuning can look similar to training. The main difference is
that training starts with randomized model weights, whereas finetuning
starts with model weights that have been previously trained.

As discussed in ["Memory
Math"](#ch07.html_ch07b_memory_math_1730159634259402){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}, the more
trainable parameters there are, the more memory is needed. Consider a
7B-parameter model:

- If you use a 16-bit format like FP16, loading the model's weights
  alone requires 14 GB for memory.

- Full finetuning this model with the Adam optimizer, also in a 16-bit
  format, requires an additional 7B × 3 × 2 bytes = 42 GB of memory.

- The total memory needed for the model's weights, gradients, and
  optimizer states is then 14 GB + 42 GB = 56 GB.

56 GB exceeds the memory capacity of most consumer GPUs, which typically
come with 12--24 GB of memory, with higher-end GPUs offering up to 48
GB. And this memory estimation doesn't yet take into account the memory
required for [activations.]{.keep-together}

::: {.calibre22 .note data-type="note"}
####### Note {.calibre23}

To fit a model on a given hardware, you can either reduce the model's
memory footprint or find ways to use the hardware's memory more
efficiently. Techniques like quantization and PEFT help minimize the
total memory footprint. Techniques that focus on making better use of
hardware memory include *CPU offloading*. Instead of trying to fit the
whole model on GPUs, you can offload the excess memory onto CPUs, as
demonstrated by DeepSpeed ([Rasley et al.,
2020](https://oreil.ly/Np1Hn){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}).
:::

[]{#ch07.html_id1428 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="partial finetuning"
data-type="indexterm"}We also haven't touched on the fact that full
finetuning, especially supervised finetuning and preference finetuning,
typically requires a lot of high-quality annotated data that most people
can't afford. Due to the high memory and data requirements of full
finetuning, people started doing *partial finetuning*. In partial
finetuning, only some of the model's parameters are updated. For
example, if a model has ten layers, you might freeze the first nine
layers and finetune only the last
layer,^[22](#ch07.html_id1429){#ch07.html_id1429-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^
reducing the number of trainable parameters to 10% of full finetuning.

While partial finetuning can reduce the memory footprint, it's
*parameter-inefficient*. Partial finetuning requires many trainable
parameters to achieve performance close to that of full finetuning. A
study by [Houlsby et al.
(2019)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1902.00751){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} shows that with BERT large ([Devlin et
al., 2018](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), you'd need to update approximately
25% of the parameters to achieve performance comparable to that of full
finetuning on the GLUE benchmark ([Wang et al.,
2018](https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.07461){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}).
[Figure 7-7](#ch07.html_ch07b_figure_4_1730159634220299){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows the
performance curve of partial finetuning with different numbers of
trainable parameters.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch07.html_ch07b_figure_4_1730159634220299" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0707.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A graph of a number of objects Description automatically generated with medium confidence" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 7-7. </span>The
blue line shows that partial finetuning requires many trainable
parameters to achieve a performance comparable to full finetuning. Image
from Houlsby et al. (2019).</h6>
</div>
</figure>

This brings up the question: How to achieve performance close to that of
full finetuning while using significantly fewer trainable parameters?
Finetuning techniques resulting from this quest are parameter-efficient.
There's no clear threshold that a finetuning method has to pass to be
considered parameter-efficient. However, in general, a technique is
considered parameter-efficient if it can achieve performance close to
that of full finetuning while using several orders of magnitude fewer
trainable parameters.

The idea of PEFT (parameter-efficient finetuning) was introduced by
Houlsby et al. (2019). The authors showed that by inserting additional
parameters into the model in the right places, you can achieve strong
finetuning performance using a small number of trainable parameters.
They inserted two adapter modules into each transformer block of a BERT
model, as shown in
[Figure 7-8](#ch07.html_ch07b_figure_5_1730159634220312){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch07.html_ch07b_figure_5_1730159634220312" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0708.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A diagram of a layer Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 7-8. </span>By
inserting two adapter modules into each transformer layer for a BERT
model and updating only the adapters, Houlsby et al. (2019) were able to
achieve strong finetuning performance using a small number of trainable
parameters.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

During finetuning, they kept the model's original parameters unchanged
and only updated the adapters. The number of trainable parameters is the
number of parameters in the adapters. On the GLUE benchmark, they
achieved a performance within 0.4% of full finetuning using only 3% of
the number of trainable parameters. The orange line in
[Figure 7-7](#ch07.html_ch07b_figure_4_1730159634220299){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows the
performance delta between full finetuning and finetuning using different
adapter sizes.

However, the downside of this approach is that it increases the
inference latency of the finetuned model. The adapters introduce
additional layers, which add more computational steps to the forward
pass, slowing inference.

PEFT enables finetuning on more affordable hardware, making it
accessible to many more developers. PEFT methods are generally not only
parameter-efficient but also sample-efficient. While full finetuning may
need tens of thousands to millions of examples to achieve notable
quality improvements, some PEFT methods can deliver strong performance
with just a few thousand examples.

Given PEFT's obvious appeal, PEFT techniques are being rapidly
developed. The next section will give an overview of these techniques
before diving deeper into the most common PEFT technique: LoRA.

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="PEFT techniques"}
::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_peft_techniques_1730159634259797 .dedication}
#### PEFT techniques {.calibre36}

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html32a .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="adapters" secondary="PEFT techniques"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html32
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="finetuning" secondary="techniques"
tertiary="PEFT techniques"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html33 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="parameter-efficient finetuning"
secondary="adapter-based/soft-prompt techniques"
data-type="indexterm"}The existing prolific world of PEFT generally
falls into two buckets: *adapter-based methods* and *soft prompt-based
methods*. However, it's likely that newer buckets will be introduced in
the future.

[]{#ch07.html_id1430 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="adapter-based methods"
data-type="indexterm"}*Adapter-based methods* refer to all methods that
involve additional modules to the model weights, such as the one
developed by [Houlsby et al.
(2019)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1902.00751){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. Because adapter-based methods involve
adding parameters, they are also called *additive methods*.

As of this writing, LoRA ([Hu et al.,
2021](https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.09685){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}) is by far the most popular adapter-based method,
and it will be the topic of the following section. Other adapter-based
methods include BitFit ([Zaken et al.,
2021](https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.10199){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), which came out around the same time LoRA did.
Newer adapter methods include IA3 ([Liu et al.,
2022](https://oreil.ly/avDPk){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), whose efficient mixed-task batching strategy
makes it particularly attractive for multi-task finetuning. It's been
shown to outperform LoRA and even full finetuning in some cases.
LongLoRA ([Chen et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.12307){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}) is a LoRA variant that incorporates
attention-modification techniques to expand context length.

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html34 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="soft prompt-based PEFT methods" data-type="indexterm"}If
adapter-based methods add trainable parameters to the model's
architecture, soft prompt-based methods modify how the model processes
the input by introducing special trainable tokens. These additional
tokens are fed into the model alongside the input tokens. They are
called *soft prompts* because, like the inputs (hard prompts), soft
prompts also guide the model's behaviors. However, soft prompts differ
from hard prompts in two ways:

- Hard prompts are human-readable. They typically contain *discrete*
  tokens such as "I", "write", "a", and "lot". In contrast, soft prompts
  are continuous vectors, resembling embedding vectors, and are not
  human-readable.

- Hard prompts are static and not trainable, whereas soft prompts can be
  optimized through backpropagation during the tuning process, allowing
  them to be adjusted for specific tasks.

Some people describe soft prompting as a crossover between prompt
engineering and finetuning.
[Figure 7-9](#ch07.html_ch07b_figure_6_1730159634220324){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} visualizes
how you can use soft prompts together with hard prompts to guide a
model's behaviors.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch07.html_ch07b_figure_6_1730159634220324" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0709.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A diagram of a model Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 7-9.
</span>Hard prompts and soft prompts can be combined to change a model’s
behaviors.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Soft prompt tuning as a subfield is characterized by a series of
similar-sounding techniques that can be confusing, such as prefix-tuning
([Li and Liang, 2021](https://arxiv.org/abs/2101.00190){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), P-Tuning ([Liu et al.,
2021](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.10385){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), and prompt tuning ([Lester et al.,
2021](https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.08691){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10
.pcalibre4}).^[23](#ch07.html_id1431){#ch07.html_id1431-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^ They differ mainly on the locations where the soft
prompts are inserted. For example, prefix tuning prepends soft prompt
tokens to the input at every transformer layer, whereas prompt tuning
prepends soft prompt tokens to only the embedded input. If you want to
use any of them, many PEFT frameworks will implement them out of the box
for you.

To get a sense of what PEFT methods are being used, I analyzed over
1,000 open issues on the [GitHub repository
huggingface/peft](https://github.com/huggingface/peft){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} in October 2024. The
assumption is that if someone uses a technique, they are more likely to
report issues or ask questions about it.
[Figure 7-10](#ch07.html_ch07b_figure_7_1730159634220334){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows the
result. For "P-Tuning", I searched for keywords "p_tuning" and "p
tuning" to account for different spellings.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch07.html_ch07b_figure_7_1730159634220334" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0710.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A graph of a graph with different colored bars Description automatically generated with medium confidence" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 7-10.
</span>The number of issues corresponding to different finetuning
techniques from the GitHub repository huggingface/peft. This is a proxy
to estimate the popularity of each technique.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

From this analysis, it's clear that LoRA dominates. Soft prompts are
less common, but there seems to be growing interest from those who want
more customization than what is afforded by prompt engineering but who
don't want to invest in
[finetuning.]{.keep-together}[]{#ch07.html_id1432 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html34" data-type="indexterm"}

Because of LoRA's popularity, the next section focuses on how LoRA works
and how it solves the challenge posed by early adapter-based methods.
Even if you don't use LoRA, this deep dive should provide a framework
for you to explore other finetuning methods.[]{#ch07.html_id1433
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html33"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1434 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html32a" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1435
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html32"
data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::

:::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="LoRA"}
::::::::::::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_lora_1730159634259834 .dedication}
#### LoRA {.calibre36}

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html35a .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="adapters" secondary="LoRA"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html35 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="finetuning" secondary="techniques" tertiary="LoRA"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html36 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="LoRA (low-rank adaptation)"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html37 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="parameter-efficient finetuning" secondary="LoRA"
data-type="indexterm"}Unlike the original adapter method by [Houlsby et
al. (2019)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1902.00751){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) ([Hu et
al., 2021](https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.09685){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}) incorporates additional parameters in
a way that doesn't incur extra inference latency. Instead of introducing
additional layers to the base model, LoRA uses modules that can be
merged back to the original layers.

You can apply LoRA to individual weight matrices. Given a weight matrix,
LoRA decomposes this matrix into the product of two smaller matrices,
then updates these two smaller matrices before merging them back to the
original matrix.

Consider the weight matrix *W* of the dimension *n* × *m*. LoRA works as
follows:

1.  First, choose the dimension of the smaller matrices. Let *r* be the
    chosen value. Construct two matrices: *A* (dimension *n* × *r*) and
    *B* (dimension *r* × *m*). Their product is *W*~*AB*~, which is of
    the same dimension as *W*. *r* is the LoRA *rank*.

2.  Add *W*~*AB*~ to the original weight matrix *W* to create a new
    weight matrix *W*ʹ. Use *W*ʹ in place of *W* as part of the model.
    You can use a hyperparameter ɑ to determine how much *W*~*AB*~
    should contribute to the new matrix:
    $Wâ = W + \frac{\alpha}{r}W_{AB}$

3.  During finetuning, update only the parameters in *A* and *B*. *W* is
    kept intact.

[Figure 7-11](#ch07.html_ch07b_figure_8_1730159634220345){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} visualizes
this process.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch07.html_ch07b_figure_8_1730159634220345" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0711.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A diagram of a diagram Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 7-11. </span>To
apply LoRA to a weight matrix W, decompose it into the product of two
matrices A and B. During finetuning, only A and B are updated. W is kept
intact.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

::: {.calibre22 .note data-type="note"}
####### Note {.calibre23}

[]{#ch07.html_id1436 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="low-rank factorization"
data-type="indexterm"}LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is built on the concept
of *low-rank factorization*, a long-standing dimensionality reduction
technique. The key idea is that you can factorize a large matrix into a
product of two smaller matrices to reduce the number of parameters,
which, in turn, reduces both the computation and memory requirements.
For example, a `9 × 9`{.calibre26} matrix can be factorized into the
product of two matrices of dimensions `9 × 1`{.calibre26} and
`1 × 9`{.calibre26}. The original matrix has 81 parameters, but the two
product matrices have only 18 parameters combined.

The number of columns in the first factorized matrix and the number of
columns in the second factorized matrix correspond to the rank of the
factorization. The original matrix is *full-rank*, while the two smaller
matrices represent a low-rank approximation.

While factorization can significantly reduce the number of parameters,
it's lossy because it only approximates the original matrix. The higher
the rank, the more information from the original matrix the
factorization can preserve.
:::

Like the original adapter method, LoRA is parameter-efficient and
sample-efficient. The factorization enables LoRA to use even fewer
trainable parameters. The LoRA paper showed that, for GPT-3, LoRA
achieves comparable or better performance with full finetuning on
several tasks while using only \~4.7M trainable parameters, 0.0027% of
full finetuning.

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Why does LoRA work?"}
::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_why_does_lora_work_1730159634259868 .dedication}
##### Why does LoRA work? {.calibre47}

[]{#ch07.html_id1437 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="LoRA (low-rank adaptation)"
secondary="mechanism of operation"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1438 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="parameter-efficient finetuning" secondary="LoRA"
tertiary="how it works" data-type="indexterm"}Parameter-efficient
methods like LoRA have become so popular that many people take them for
granted. *But why is parameter efficiency possible at all?* If a model
requires a lot of parameters to learn certain behaviors during
pre-training, shouldn't it also require a lot of parameters to change
its behaviors during finetuning?

The same question can be raised for data. If a model requires a lot of
data to learn a behavior, shouldn't it also require a lot of data to
meaningfully change this behavior? How is it possible that you need
millions or billions of examples to pre-train a model, but only a few
hundreds or thousands of examples to finetune it?

Many papers have argued that while LLMs have many parameters, they have
very low intrinsic dimensions; see [Li et al.
(2018)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.08838){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}; [Aghajanyan et al.
(2020)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.13255){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}; and [Hu et al.
(2021)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.09685){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. They showed that *pre-training
implicitly minimizes the model's intrinsic dimension*. Surprisingly,
larger models tend to have lower intrinsic dimensions after
pre-training. This suggests that pre-training acts as a compression
framework for downstream tasks. In other words, the better trained an
LLM is, the easier it is to finetune the model using a small number of
trainable parameters and a small amount of data.

You might wonder, if low-rank factorization works so well, *why don't we
use LoRA for pre-training as well?* Instead of pre-training a large
model and applying low-rank factorization only during finetuning, could
we factorize a model from the start for pre-training? Low-rank
pre-training can significantly reduce the model's number of parameters,
significantly reducing the model's pre-training time and cost.

Throughout the 2010s, many people tried training low-rank neural
networks, exemplified in studies such as "Low-Rank Matrix Factorization
for Deep Neural Network Training with High-Dimensional Output Targets"
([Sainath et al., 2013](https://oreil.ly/xzdiG){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), "Semi-Orthogonal Low-Rank Matrix
Factorization for Deep Neural Networks" ([Povey et al.,
2018](https://oreil.ly/LHLNz){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), and "Speeding up Convolutional Neural Networks
with Low Rank Expansions" ([Jaderberg et al.,
2014](https://oreil.ly/BR63I){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}).

Low-rank factorization has proven to be effective at smaller scales. For
example, by applying various factorization strategies, including
replacing 3 × 3 convolution with 1 × 1 convolution, SqueezeNet ([Iandola
et al., 2016](https://arxiv.org/abs/1602.07360){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}) achieves AlexNet-level accuracy on
ImageNet using 50 times fewer parameters.

More recent attempts to train low-rank LLMs include ReLoRA ([Lialin et
al., 2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.05695){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}) and GaLore ([Zhao et al.,
2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.03507){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}). ReLoRA works for transformer-based models of up
to 1.3B parameters. GaLore achieves performance comparable to that of a
full-rank model at 1B parameters and promising performance at 7B
parameters.

It's possible that one day not too far in the future, researchers will
develop a way to scale up low-rank pre-training to hundreds of billions
of parameters. However, if [Aghajanyan et al.'s
argument](https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.13255){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} is correct---that pre-training
implicitly compresses a model's intrinsic dimension---full-rank
pre-training is still necessary to sufficiently reduce the model's
intrinsic dimension to a point where low-rank factorization can work. It
would be interesting to study exactly how much full-rank training is
necessary before it's possible to switch to low-rank
[training.]{.keep-together}
:::
::::

::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="LoRA configurations"}
:::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_lora_configurations_1730159634259897 .dedication}
##### LoRA configurations {.calibre47}

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html38 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="LoRA (low-rank adaptation)"
secondary="configurations"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html39 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="parameter-efficient finetuning" secondary="LoRA"
tertiary="configurations" data-type="indexterm"}To apply LoRA, you need
to decide what weight matrices to apply LoRA to and the rank of each
factorization. This section will discuss the considerations for each of
these decisions.

LoRA can be applied to each individual weight matrix. The efficiency of
LoRA, therefore, depends not only on what matrices LoRA is applied to
but also on the model's architecture, as different architectures have
different weight matrices.

While there have been examples of LoRA with other architectures, such as
convolutional neural networks ([Dutt et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.08252){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}; [Zhong et al.,
2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.17868){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}; [Aleem et al.,
2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.04964){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), LoRA has been primarily used for transformer
models.^[24](#ch07.html_id1439){#ch07.html_id1439-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ LoRA
is most commonly applied to the four weight matrices in the attention
modules: the query (*W*~*q*~), key (*W*~*k*~), value (*W*~*v*~), and
output projection (*W*~*o*~) matrices.

Typically, LoRA is applied uniformly to all matrices of the same type
within a model. For example, applying LoRA to the query matrix means
applying LoRA to all query matrices in the model.

Naively, you can apply LoRA to all these attention matrices. However,
often, you're constrained by your hardware's memory and can accommodate
only a fixed number of trainable parameters. Given a fixed budget of
trainable parameters, what matrices should you apply LoRA to, to
maximize performance?

When finetuning GPT-3 175B, Hu et al. (2021) set their trainable
parameter budget at 18M, which is 0.01% of the model's total number of
parameters. This budget allows them to apply LoRA to the following:

1.  One matrix with the rank of 8

2.  Two matrices with the rank of 4

3.  All four matrices with the rank of 2

::: {.calibre22 .note data-type="note"}
####### Note {.calibre23}

GPT-3 175B has 96 transformer layers with a model dimension of 12,288.
Applying LoRA with rank = 2 to all four matrices would yield (12,288 × 2
× 2) × 4 = 196,608 trainable parameters per layer, or 18,874,368
trainable parameters for the whole model.
:::

They found that applying LoRA to all four matrices with rank = 2 yields
the best performance on the WikiSQL ([Zhong et al.,
2017](https://arxiv.org/abs/1709.00103){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}) and MultiNLI (Multi-Genre Natural Language
Inference) benchmarks ([Williams et al.,
2017](https://oreil.ly/mqHMU){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}).
[Table 7-5](#ch07.html_ch07b_table_3_1730159634233616){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows
their results. However, the authors suggested that if you can choose
only two attention matrices, the query and value matrices generally
yield the best results.

+----------+------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|          | Number of trainable parameters = 18M                                   |
+==========+========+========+========+========+============+============+==========+
| Weight   | W~q~   | W~k~   | W~v~   | W~o~   | W~q~, W~k~ | W~q~, W~v~ | W~q~,    |
| type     |        |        |        |        |            |            | W~k~,    |
|          |        |        |        |        |            |            | W~v~,    |
|          |        |        |        |        |            |            | W~o~     |
+----------+--------+--------+--------+--------+------------+------------+----------+
| Rank r   | 8      | 8      | 8      | 8      | 4          | 4          | 2        |
+----------+--------+--------+--------+--------+------------+------------+----------+
| WikiSQL  | 70.4   | 70.0   | 73.0   | 73.2   | 71.4       | **73.7**   | **73.7** |
| (± 0.5%) |        |        |        |        |            |            |          |
+----------+--------+--------+--------+--------+------------+------------+----------+
| MultiNLI | 91.0   | 90.8   | 91.0   | 91.3   | 91.3       | 91.3       | **91.7** |
| (± 0.1%) |        |        |        |        |            |            |          |
+----------+--------+--------+--------+--------+------------+------------+----------+

: [Table 7-5. ]{.keep-together}LoRA performance with the budget of 18M
trainable parameters. Results from LoRA (Hu et al., 2021).
{#ch07b_table_3_1730159634233616 .stafflist_table}

[]{#ch07.html_id1440 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="feedforward layer"}Empirical observations suggest that applying
LoRA to more weight matrices, including the feedforward matrices, yields
better results. For example, Databricks showed that the biggest
performance boost they got was from applying LoRA to all feedforward
layers ([Sooriyarachchi, 2023](https://oreil.ly/zzREV){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}). [Fomenko et al.
(2024)](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.05086v1){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} noted that feedforward-based LoRA can
be complementary to attention-based LoRA, though attention-based LoRA
typically offers greater efficacy within memory constraints.

The beauty of LoRA is that while its performance depends on its rank,
studies have shown that *a small r, such as between 4 and 64, is usually
sufficient for many use cases*. A smaller *r* means fewer LoRA
parameters, which translates to a lower memory footprint.

The LoRA authors observed that, to their surprise, increasing the value
of *r* doesn't increase finetuning performance. This observation is
consistent with Databricks' report that "increasing *r* beyond a certain
value may not yield any discernible increase in quality of model output"
(Sooriyarachchi, 2023).^[25](#ch07.html_id1441){#ch07.html_id1441-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^ Some argue that a higher *r* might even hurt as it
can lead to overfitting. However, in some cases, a higher rank might be
necessary. [Raschka (2023)](https://oreil.ly/A-d5f){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} found that *r* = 256
achieved the best performance on his tasks.

Another LoRA hyperparameter you can configure is the value $\alpha$ that
determines how much the product *W*~*AB*~ should contribute to the new
matrix during merging: $Wâ = W + \frac{\alpha}{r}W_{AB}$. In practice,
I've often seen ɑ chosen so that the ratio $\alpha\!:\! r$ is typically
between 1:8 and 8:1, but the optimal ratio varies. For example, if *r*
is small, you might want $\alpha$ to be larger, and if *r* is large, you
might want $\alpha$ to be smaller. Experimentation is needed to
determine the best $(r,\alpha)$ combination for your use
case.[]{#ch07.html_id1442 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html39"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1443 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html38" data-type="indexterm"}
::::
:::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Serving LoRA adapters"}
::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_serving_lora_adapters_1730159634259927 .dedication}
##### Serving LoRA adapters {.calibre47}

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html40 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="LoRA (low-rank adaptation)"
secondary="LoRA adapters service"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html41 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="parameter-efficient finetuning" secondary="LoRA"
tertiary="LoRA adapters service" data-type="indexterm"}LoRA not only
lets you finetune models using less memory and data, but it also
simplifies serving multiple models due to its modularity. To understand
this benefit, let's examine how to serve a LoRA-finetuned model.

In general, there are two ways to serve a LoRA-finetuned model:

1.  Merge the LoRA weights *A* and *B* into the original model to create
    the new matrix Wʹ prior to serving the finetuned model. Since no
    extra computation is done during inference, no extra latency is
    added.

2.  Keep *W*, *A*, and *B* separate during serving. The process of
    merging *A* and *B* back to *W* happens during inference, which adds
    extra latency.

The first option is generally better if you have only one LoRA model to
serve, whereas the second is generally better for *multi-LoRA
serving---*serving multiple LoRA models that share the same base model.
[Figure 7-12](#ch07.html_ch07b_figure_9_1730159634220354){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} visualizes
multi-LoRA serving if you keep the LoRA adapters separate.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch07.html_ch07b_figure_9_1730159634220354" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0712.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A diagram of a flowchart Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 7-12.
</span>Keeping LoRA adapters separate allows reuse of the same full-rank
matrix <em>W</em> in multi-LoRA serving.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

For multi-LoRA serving, while option 2 adds latency overhead, it
significantly reduces the storage needed. Consider the scenario in which
you finetune a model for each of your customers using LoRA. With 100
customers, you end up with 100 finetuned models, all sharing the same
base model. With option 1, you have to store 100 full-rank matrices
*W*ʹ. With option 2, you only have to store one full-rank matrix *W*,
and 100 sets of smaller matrices (*A*, *B*).

To put this in perspective, let's say that the original matrix *W* is of
the dimension `4096 × 4096 `{.calibre26}(16.8M parameters). If the
LoRA's rank is 8, the number of parameters in *A* and *B* is
`4096 × 8 × 2 = 65,536`{.calibre26}:

- In option 1, 100 full-rank matrices *W*ʹ totals
  `16.8M × 100 = 1.68B`{.calibre26} parameters.

- In option 2, one full-rank matrix *W* and 100 sets of small matrices
  (*A*, *B*) totals: `16.8M + 65,536 × 100 = 23.3M`{.calibre26}
  parameters.

Option 2 also makes it faster to switch between tasks. Let's say you're
currently serving customer *X* using this customer's model. To switch to
serving customer *Y*, instead of loading this customer's full weight
matrix, you only need to load Y's LoRA adapter, which can significantly
reduce the loading time. While keeping *A* and *B* separate incurs
additional latency, there are optimization techniques to minimize the
added latency. The [book's GitHub
repository](https://github.com/chiphuyen/aie-book){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} contains a walkthrough of how to do
so.

Multi-LoRA serving makes it easy to combine multiple specialized models.
Instead of having one big powerful model for multiple tasks, you can
have one LoRA adapter for each task. For example, Apple used multiple
[LoRA adapters](https://oreil.ly/vfXqE){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} to adapt the same 3B-parameter base model to
different iPhone features (2024). They utilized quantization techniques
to further reduce the memory footprint of this base model and adapters,
allowing the serving of all of them on-device.

The modularity of LoRA adapters means that LoRA adapters can be shared
and reused. There are publicly available finetuned LoRA adapters that
you can use the way you'd use pre-trained models. You can find them on
[Hugging Face](https://oreil.ly/T08JJ){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}^[26](#ch07.html_id1444){#ch07.html_id1444-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^ or initiatives like
[AdapterHub](https://adapterhub.ml){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}.

You might be wondering: "LoRA sounds great, but what's the catch?" The
main drawback of LoRA is that it doesn't offer performance as strong as
full finetuning. [It's also]{.keep-together} more challenging to do than
full finetuning as it involves modifying the model's implementation,
which requires an understanding of the model's architecture and coding
skills. However, this is usually only an issue for less popular base
models. PEFT frameworks---such as [Hugging Face's
PEFT](https://github.com/huggingface/peft){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4},
[Axolotl](https://github.com/axolotl-ai-cloud/axolotl){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4},
[unsloth](https://github.com/unslothai/unsloth){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, and
[LitGPT](https://github.com/Lightning-AI/litgpt){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}---likely support LoRA for popular base
models right out of the box.[]{#ch07.html_id1445 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html41" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1446
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html40"
data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Quantized LoRA"}
::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_quantized_lora_1730159634259954 .dedication}
##### Quantized LoRA {.calibre47}

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html42 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="LoRA (low-rank adaptation)"
secondary="quantized LoRA (QLoRA)"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html43 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="parameter-efficient finetuning" secondary="LoRA"
tertiary="quantized LoRA"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html44 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="QLoRA (quantized LoRA)"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html45 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="quantized LoRA (QLoRA)" data-type="indexterm"}The rapid rise of
LoRA has led to the development of numerous LoRA variations. Some aim to
reduce the number of trainable parameters even further. However, as
illustrated in
[Table 7-6](#ch07.html_ch07b_table_4_1730159634233626){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}, the
memory of a LoRA adapter is minimal compared to the memory of the
model's weights. Reducing the number of LoRA parameters decreases the
overall memory footprint by only a small percentage.

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
                    Model's weights   LoRA trainable    LoRA adapter
                    memory\           params\           memory\
                    (16 bits)         (r=2, query & key (16 bits)
                                      matrices)         
  ----------------- ----------------- ----------------- -----------------
  Llama 2 (13B)     26 GB             3.28M             6.55 MB

  GPT-3 (175B)      350 GB            18.87M            37.7 MB
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

  : [Table 7-6. ]{.keep-together}The memory needed by LoRA weights
  compared to that needed by the model's weights.
  {#ch07b_table_4_1730159634233626 .stafflist_table}

Rather than trying to reduce LoRA's number of parameters, you can reduce
the memory usage more effectively by quantizing the model's weights,
activations, and/or gradients during finetuning. An early promising
quantized version of LoRA is QLoRA ([Dettmers et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.14314){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10
.pcalibre4}).^[27](#ch07.html_id1447){#ch07.html_id1447-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^ In the original LoRA paper, during finetuning, the
model's weights are stored using 16 bits. QLoRA stores the model's
weights in 4 bits but dequantizes (converts) them back into BF16 when
computing the forward and backward pass.

The 4-bit format that QLoRA uses is NF4 (NormalFloat-4), which quantizes
values based on the insight that pre-trained weights usually follow a
normal distribution with a median of zero. On top of 4-bit quantization,
QLoRA also uses paged optimizers to automatically transfer data between
the CPU and GPU when the GPU runs out of memory, especially with long
sequence lengths. These techniques allow a 65B-parameter model to be
finetuned on a single 48 GB GPU.

The authors finetuned a variety of models, including Llama 7B to 65B, in
the 4-bit mode. The resulting family of models, called Guanaco, showed
competitive performance on both public benchmarks and comparative
evaluation. []{#ch07.html_id1448 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="Elo"}[Table 7-7](#ch07.html_ch07b_table_5_1730159634233637){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows the
Elo ratings of Guanaco models, GPT-4, and ChatGPT in May 2023, as judged
by GPT-4. While Guanaco 65B didn't outperform GPT-4, it was often
preferred to ChatGPT.

  Model         Size    Elo
  ------------- ------- ----------
  GPT-4         \-      1348 ± 1
  Guanaco 65B   41 GB   1022 ± 1
  Guanaco 33B   21 GB   992 ± 1
  Vicuna 13B    26 GB   974 ± 1
  ChatGPT       \-      966 ± 1
  Guanaco 13B   10 GB   916 ± 1
  Bard          \-      902 ± 1
  Guanaco 7B    6 GB    879 ± 1

  : [Table 7-7. ]{.keep-together}Elo ratings of Guanaco models compared
  to popular models in May 2023 using GPT-4 as a judge. The experiment
  is from QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023).
  {#ch07b_table_5_1730159634233637 .stafflist_table}

The main limitation of QLoRA is that NF4 quantization is expensive.
While QLoRA can reduce the memory footprint, it might increase training
time due to the extra time required by quantization and dequantization
steps.

Due to its memory-saving promise, quantized LoRA is an active area of
research. Other than QLoRA, quantized LoRA works include QA-LoRA ([Xu et
al., 2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.14717){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), ModuLoRA ([Yin et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.16119){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), and IR-QLoRA ([Qin et al.,
2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.05445){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4})[]{#ch07.html_id1449 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html45" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1450
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html44"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1451 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html43" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1452
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html42"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1453 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html37" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1454
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html36"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1455 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html35a" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1456
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html35"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1457 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html31" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1458
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch07.html30"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_id1459 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch07.html29" data-type="indexterm"}.
:::
::::
:::::::::::::
::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Model Merging and Multi-Task Finetuning"}
::::::::::::::::: {#ch07.html_ch07b_model_merging_and_multi_task_finetuning_1730159634260035 .dedication}
### Model Merging and Multi-Task Finetuning {.calibre35}

[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html46 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="finetuning"
secondary="techniques"
tertiary="model merging and multi-task finetuning"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch07.html_ch07.html47 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model merging" data-type="indexterm"}If finetuning allows you
to create a custom model by altering a single model, model merging
allows you to create a custom model by combining multiple models. Model
merging offers you greater flexibility than finetuning alone. You can
take two available models and merge them together to create a new,
hopefully more useful, model. You can also finetune any or all of the
constituent models before merging them together.

While you don't have to further finetune the merged model, its
performance can often be improved by finetuning. Without finetuning,
model merging can be done without GPUs, making merging particularly
attractive to indie model developers that don't have access to a lot of
compute.

The goal of model merging is to create a single model that provides more
value than using all the constituent models separately. The added value
can come from improved performance. For example, if you have two models
that are good at different things on the same task, you can merge them
into a single model that is better than both of them on that task.
Imagine one model that can answer the first 60% of the questions and
another model that can answer the last 60% of the questions. Combined,
perhaps they can answer 80% of the questions.

The added value can also come from a reduced memory footprint, which
leads to reduced costs. For example, if you have two models that can do
different tasks, they can be merged into one model that can do both
tasks but with fewer parameters. This is particularly attractive for
