second with more users, shifting the workload from being bandwidth-bound
to compute-bound.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch09.html_ch09_figure_5_1730130962952692" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0905.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A graph of a number of users Description automatically generated with medium confidence" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 9-5.
</span>Bandwidth utilization for Llama 2-70B in FP16 across three
different chips shows a decrease in MBU as the number of concurrent
users increases. Image from “LLM Training and Inference with Intel Gaudi
2 AI Accelerators” (<a href="https://oreil.ly/tOOOD"
class="pcalibre7 pcalibre5 pcalibre6 calibre10 pcalibre4">Databricks,
2024</a>).</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Utilization metrics are helpful to track your system's efficiency.
Higher utilization rates for similar workloads on the same hardware
generally mean that your services are becoming more efficient. However,
*the goal isn't to get the chips with the highest utilization*. What you
really care about is how to get your jobs done faster and cheaper. A
higher utilization rate means nothing if the cost and latency both
increase[]{#ch09.html_id1639 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html28"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1640 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html27" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1641
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html26"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1642 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html25" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1643
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html24"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1644 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html23" data-type="indexterm"}.[]{#ch09.html_id1645
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html11"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1646 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html10" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1647
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html9"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1648 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html9a" data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::
:::::::::
::::::::::

::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="AI Accelerators"}
:::::::::::: {#ch09.html_ch09_ai_accelerators_1730130963007351 .dedication}
### AI Accelerators {.calibre35}

[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html29 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="accelerators"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html30 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="inference optimization" secondary="understanding"
tertiary="AI accelerators" data-type="indexterm"}How fast and cheap
software can run depends on the hardware it runs on. While there are
optimization techniques that work across hardware, understanding
hardware allows for deeper optimization. This section looks at hardware
from an inference perspective, but it can be applied to training as
well.

The development of AI models and hardware has always been intertwined.
The lack of sufficiently powerful computers was one of the contributing
factors to the first AI winter in the
1970s.^[13](#ch09.html_id1649){#ch09.html_id1649-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

The revival of interest in deep learning in 2012 was also closely tied
to compute. One commonly acknowledged reason for the popularity of
AlexNet ([Krizhevsky et al., 2012](https://oreil.ly/Yv4V7){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}) is that it was the first
paper to successfully use
[GPUs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graphics_processing_unit){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, graphics processing units,
to train neural
networks.^[14](#ch09.html_id1650){#ch09.html_id1650-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ Before
GPUs, if you wanted to train a model at AlexNet's scale, you'd have to
use thousands of CPUs, like the one [Google released just a few months
before AlexNet](https://oreil.ly/Xpwco){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}. Compared to thousands of CPUs, a couple of GPUs
were a lot more accessible to PhD students and researchers, setting off
the deep learning research boom.

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="What’s an accelerator?"}
::: {#ch09.html_ch09_what_s_an_accelerator_1730130963007426 .dedication}
#### What's an accelerator? {.calibre36}

[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html31 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="accelerators"
secondary="defined" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html32
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="inference optimization"
secondary="AI accelerators" tertiary="defined" data-type="indexterm"}An
accelerator is a chip designed to accelerate a specific type of
computational workload. An AI accelerator is designed for AI workloads.
The dominant type of AI accelerator is GPUs, and the biggest economic
driver during the AI boom in the early 2020s is undoubtedly NVIDIA.

The main difference between CPUs and GPUs is that CPUs are designed for
general-purpose usage, whereas GPUs are designed for parallel
processing:

- CPUs have a few powerful cores, typically up to 64 cores for high-end
  consumer machines. While many CPU cores can handle multi-threaded
  workloads effectively, they excel at tasks requiring high
  single-thread performance, such as running an operating system,
  managing I/O (input/output) operations, or handling complex,
  sequential processes.

- GPUs have thousands of smaller, less powerful cores optimized for
  tasks that can be broken down into many smaller, independent
  calculations, such as graphics rendering and machine learning. The
  operation that constitutes most ML workloads is matrix multiplication,
  which is highly
  parallelizable.^[15](#ch09.html_id1651){#ch09.html_id1651-marker
  .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
  data-type="noteref"}^

While the pursuit of efficient parallel processing increases
computational capabilities, it imposes challenges on memory design and
power consumption.

The success of NVIDIA GPUs has inspired many accelerators designed to
speed up AI workloads, including [Advanced Micro Devices (AMD)'s newer
generations of
GPUs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_AMD_graphics_processing_units){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, Google's TPU ([Tensor
Processing
Unit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tensor_Processing_Unit){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), [Intel's Habana
Gaudi](https://oreil.ly/oDQOk){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, [Graphcore's Intelligent Processing
Unit](https://oreil.ly/6ySTY){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} (IPU), [Groq's Language Processing
Unit](https://oreil.ly/R7gXn){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} (LPU), [Cerebras'
Wafer-Scale](https://oreil.ly/ACIty){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} [Quant Processing
Unit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_quantum_processors){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (QPU), and many more being
introduced.

While many chips can handle both training and inference, one big theme
emerging is specialized chips for inference. A survey by [Desislavov et
al. (2023)](https://oreil.ly/qSpMK){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} shares that inference can exceed the cost of
training in commonly used systems, and that inference accounts for up to
90% of the machine learning costs for deployed AI systems.

As discussed in [Chapter 7](#ch07.html_ch07){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}, training demands
much more memory due to backpropagation and is generally more difficult
to perform in lower precision. Furthermore, training usually emphasizes
throughput, whereas inference aims to minimize latency.

Consequently, chips designed for inference are often optimized for lower
precision and faster memory access, rather than large memory capacity.
Examples of such chips include the Apple [Neural
Engine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neural_Engine){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, [AWS
Inferentia](https://oreil.ly/42LSB){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, and [MTIA](https://oreil.ly/XH2bh){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (Meta Training and
Inference Accelerator). Chips designed for edge computing, like
[Google's Edge TPU](https://oreil.ly/m8daG){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} and the [NVIDIA Jetson
Xavier](https://oreil.ly/PRZSQ){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, are also typically geared toward inference.

There are also chips specialized for different model architectures, such
as chips specialized for the
transformer.^[16](#ch09.html_id1652){#ch09.html_id1652-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ Many
chips are designed for data centers, with more and more being designed
for consumer devices (such as phones and laptops).

Different hardware architectures have different memory layouts and
specialized compute units that evolve over time. These units are
optimized for specific data types, such as scalars, vectors, or tensors,
as shown in
[Figure 9-6](#ch09.html_ch09_figure_6_1730130962952710){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch09.html_ch09_figure_6_1730130962952710" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0906.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A diagram of a computer Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 9-6.
</span>Different compute primitives. Image inspired by <a
href="https://arxiv.org/abs/1802.04799"
class="pcalibre7 pcalibre5 pcalibre6 calibre10 pcalibre4">Chen et al.
(2018)</a>.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

A chip might have a mixture of different compute units optimized for
various data types. For example, GPUs traditionally supported vector
operations, but many modern GPUs now include tensor cores optimized for
matrix and tensor computations. TPUs, on the other hand, are designed
with tensor operations as their primary compute primitive. To
efficiently operate a model on a hardware architecture, its memory
layout and compute primitives need to be taken into account.

A chip's specifications contain many details that can be useful when
evaluating this chip for each specific use case. However, the main
characteristics that matter across use cases are computational
capabilities, memory size and bandwidth, and power consumption. I'll use
GPUs as examples to illustrate these
characteristics.[]{#ch09.html_id1653 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html32" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1654
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html31"
data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Computational capabilities"}
::: {#ch09.html_ch09_computational_capabilities_1730130963007489 .dedication}
#### Computational capabilities {.calibre36}

[]{#ch09.html_id1655 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="accelerators"
secondary="computational capabilities"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1656 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="computational capabilities, of AI accelerators"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1657 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="inference optimization" secondary="AI accelerators"
tertiary="computational capabilities"
data-type="indexterm"}Computational capabilities are typically measured
by the number of operations a chip can perform in a given time. The most
common metric is *FLOP/s*, often written as FLOPS, which measures the
*peak* number of floating-point operations per second. In reality,
however, it's very unlikely that an application can achieve this peak
FLOP/s. The ratio between the actual FLOP/s and the theoretical FLOP/s
is one *utilization* metric.

The number of operations a chip can perform in a second depends on the
numerical precision---the higher the precision, the fewer operations the
chip can execute. Think about how adding two 32-bit numbers generally
requires twice the computation of adding two 16-bit numbers. The number
of 32-bit operations a chip can perform in a given time is not exactly
half that of 16-bit operations because of different chips' optimization.
For an overview of numerical precision, revisit ["Numerical
Representations"](#ch07.html_ch07b_numerical_representations_1730159634259493){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

[Table 9-2](#ch09.html_ch09_table_2_1730130962971057){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows the
FLOP/s specs for different precision formats for [NVIDIA H100 SXM
chips](https://oreil.ly/bNAOG){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}.

+------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Numerical precision                                  | teraFLOP/s (trillion FLOP/s)     |
|                                                      | with sparsity                    |
+======================================================+==================================+
| TF32 Tensor                                          | 989                              |
| Core^[a](#ch09.html_id1658){#ch09.html_id1658-marker |                                  |
| .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10          |                                  |
| .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^                     |                                  |
+------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| BFLOAT16 Tensor Core                                 | 1,979                            |
+------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| FP16 Tensor Core                                     | 1,979                            |
+------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| FP8 Tensor Core                                      | 3,958                            |
+------------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| ^[a](#ch09.html_id1658-marker){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}^ |
| Recall from [Chapter 7](#ch07.html_ch07){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .calibre56 .pcalibre6    |
| .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} that TF32 is a 19-bit, not 32-bit, format.                 |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

: [Table 9-2. ]{.keep-together}FLOP/s specs for NVIDIA H100 SXM chips.
{#ch09_table_2_1730130962971057 .stafflist_table}
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Memory size and bandwidth"}
::: {#ch09.html_ch09_memory_size_and_bandwidth_1730130963007548 .dedication}
#### Memory size and bandwidth {.calibre36}

[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html33 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="accelerators"
secondary="memory size and bandwidth"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html34 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="inference optimization" secondary="AI accelerators"
tertiary="memory size and bandwidth"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html35 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="memory bottlenecks" secondary="size and bandwidth"
data-type="indexterm"}Because a GPU has many cores working in parallel,
data often needs to be moved from the memory to these cores, and,
therefore, data transfer speed is important. Data transfer is crucial
when working with AI models that involve large weight matrices and
training data. These large amounts of data need to be moved quickly to
keep the cores efficiently occupied. Therefore, GPU memory needs to have
higher bandwidth and lower latency than CPU memory, and thus, GPU memory
requires more advanced memory technologies. This is one of the factors
that makes GPU memory more expensive than CPU memory.

[]{#ch09.html_id1659 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="DDR SDRAM (doubled data rate synchronous dynamic random-access memory)"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1660 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="doubled data rate synchronous dynamic random-access memory (DDR SDRAM)"
data-type="indexterm"}To be more specific, CPUs typically use [DDR
SDRAM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DDR_SDRAM){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (Double Data Rate Synchronous Dynamic
Random-Access Memory), which has a 2D structure. GPUs, particularly
high-end ones, often use
[HBM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High_Bandwidth_Memory){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (high-bandwidth memory),
which has a 3D stacked
structure.^[17](#ch09.html_id1661){#ch09.html_id1661-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

An accelerator's memory is measured by its *size and bandwidth*. These
numbers need to be evaluated within the system an accelerator is part
of. An accelerator, such as a GPU, typically interacts with three levels
of memory, as visualized in
[Figure 9-7](#ch09.html_ch09_figure_7_1730130962952731){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}:

CPU memory (DRAM)

:   []{#ch09.html_id1662 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="CPU memory (DRAM)"
    data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1663 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
    .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
    primary="DRAM (CPU memory)" data-type="indexterm"}Accelerators are
    usually deployed alongside CPUs, giving them access to the CPU
    memory (also known as system memory, host memory, or just CPU DRAM).

:   CPU memory usually has the lowest bandwidth among these memory
    types, with data transfer speeds ranging from 25 GB/s to 50 GB/s.
    CPU memory size varies. Average laptops might have around 16--64 GB,
    whereas high-end workstations can have one TB or more.

GPU high-bandwidth memory (HBM)

:   []{#ch09.html_id1664 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
    primary="high-bandwidth memory (HBM)" data-type="indexterm"}This is
    the memory dedicated to the GPU, located close to the GPU for faster
    access than CPU memory.

:   HBM provides significantly higher bandwidth, with data transfer
    speeds typically ranging from 256 GB/s to over 1.5 TB/s. This speed
    is essential for efficiently handling large data transfers and
    high-throughput tasks. A consumer GPU has around 24--80 GB of HBM.

GPU on-chip SRAM

:   []{#ch09.html_id1665 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="GPU on-chip SRAM"
    data-type="indexterm"}Integrated directly into the chip, this memory
    is used to store frequently accessed data and instructions for
    nearly instant access. It includes L1 and L2 caches made of SRAM,
    and, in some architectures, L3 caches as well. These caches are part
    of the broader on-chip memory, which also includes other components
    like register files and shared memory.

:   RAM has extremely high data transfer speeds, often exceeding 10
    TB/s. The size of GPU SRAM is small, typically 40 MB or under.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch09.html_ch09_figure_7_1730130962952731" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0907.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A colorful pyramid with multiple layers Description automatically generated with medium confidence" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 9-7. </span>The
memory hierarchy of an AI accelerator. The numbers are for reference
only. The actual numbers vary for each chip.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

A lot of GPU optimization is about how to make the most out of this
memory hierarchy. However, as of this writing, popular frameworks such
as PyTorch and TensorFlow don't yet allow fine-grained control of memory
access. This has led many AI researchers and engineers to become
interested in GPU programming languages such as
[CUDA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CUDA){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (originally Compute Unified Device
Architecture), [OpenAI's
Triton](https://github.com/triton-lang/triton){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, and
[ROCm](https://github.com/ROCm/ROCm){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} (Radeon Open Compute). The latter is AMD's open
source alternative to NVIDIA's proprietary CUDA.[]{#ch09.html_id1666
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html35"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1667 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html34" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1668
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html33"
data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::

::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Power consumption"}
:::: {#ch09.html_ch09_power_consumption_1730130963007605 .dedication}
#### Power consumption {.calibre36}

[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html36 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="accelerators"
secondary="power consumption"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html37 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="inference optimization" secondary="AI accelerators"
tertiary="power consumption"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html38 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="power consumption" data-type="indexterm"}Chips rely on
transistors to perform computation. Each computation is done by
transistors switching on and off, which requires energy. A GPU can have
billions of transistors---an NVIDIA A100 has [54
billion](https://oreil.ly/5vRsP){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} transistors, while an NVIDIA H100 has [80
billion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hopper_(microarchitecture)){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. When an accelerator is
used efficiently, billions of transistors rapidly switch states,
consuming a substantial amount of energy and generating a nontrivial
amount of heat. This heat requires cooling systems, which also consume
electricity, adding to data centers' overall energy consumption.

Chip energy consumption threatens to have a staggering impact on the
[environment](https://oreil.ly/RqY-3){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}, increasing the pressure on companies to invest
in technologies for [green data
centers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Green_data_center){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. An NVIDIA H100 running at
its peak for a year consumes approximately 7,000 kWh. For comparison,
the average US household's annual electricity consumption is 10,000 kWh.
That's why electricity is a bottleneck to scaling up
compute.^[18](#ch09.html_id1669){#ch09.html_id1669-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

Accelerators typically specify their power consumption under *maximum
power draw* or a proxy metric *TDP (thermal design power):*

- Maximum power draw indicates the peak power that the chip could draw
  under full load.

- *TDP* represents the maximum heat a cooling system needs to dissipate
  when the chip operates under typical workloads. While it's not an
  exact measure of power consumption, it's an indication of the expected
  power draw. For CPUs and GPUs, the maximum power draw can be roughly
  1.1 to 1.5 times the TDP, though the exact relationship varies
  depending on the specific architecture and workload.

If you opt for cloud providers, you won't need to worry about cooling or
electricity. However, these numbers can still be of interest to
understand the impact of accelerators on the environment and the overall
electricity demand.

<aside data-type="sidebar" epub:type="sidebar" class="calibre20">

::: {#ch09.html_id1670 .sidebar}
## Selecting Accelerators {.calibre21}

What accelerators to use depends on your workload. If your workloads are
compute-bound, you might want to look for chips with more FLOP/s. If
your workloads are memory-bound, shelling out money for chips with
higher bandwidth and more memory will make your life easier.

When evaluating which chips to buy, there are three main questions:

- Can the hardware run your workloads?

- How long does it take to do so?

- How much does it cost?

FLOP/s, memory size, and memory bandwidth are the three big numbers that
help you answer the first two questions. The last question is
straightforward. Cloud providers' pricing is typically usage-based and
fairly similar across providers. If you buy your hardware, the cost can
be calculated based on the initial price and ongoing power
[]{#ch09.html_id1671 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html38"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1672 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html37" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1673
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html36"
data-type="indexterm"}consumption[]{#ch09.html_id1674 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="" startref="ch09.html30"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1675 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html29" data-type="indexterm"}.[]{#ch09.html_id1676
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html1"
data-type="indexterm"}
:::

</aside>
::::
:::::
::::::::::::
:::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect1" pdf-bookmark="Inference Optimization "}
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#ch09.html_ch09_inference_optimization_1730130963007697 .dedication}
## Inference Optimization {.calibre4}

[]{#ch09.html_id1677 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="inference optimization"
secondary="at model/hardware/service levels" secondary-sortas="model"
data-type="indexterm"}Inference optimization can be done at the model,
hardware, or service level. To illustrate their differences, consider
archery. Model-level optimization is like crafting better arrows.
Hardware-level optimization is like training a stronger and better
archer. Service-level optimization is like refining the entire shooting
process, including the bow and aiming conditions.

Ideally, optimizing a model for speed and cost shouldn't change the
model's quality. However, many techniques might cause model degradation.
[Figure 9-8](#ch09.html_ch09_figure_8_1730130962952759){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows the
same Llama models' performance on different benchmarks, served by
different inference service providers.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch09.html_ch09_figure_8_1730130962952759" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0908.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A graph of different types of numbers Description automatically generated with medium confidence" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 9-8. </span>An
inference service provider might use optimization techniques that can
alter a model’s behavior, causing different providers to have slight
model quality variations. The experiment was conducted by <a
href="https://oreil.ly/5hFSF"
class="pcalibre7 pcalibre5 pcalibre6 calibre10 pcalibre4">Cerebras
(2024)</a>.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

Since hardware design is outside the scope of this book, I'll discuss
techniques at the model and service levels. While the techniques are
discussed separately, keep in mind that, in production, optimization
typically involves techniques at more than one level.

::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Model Optimization"}
:::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#ch09.html_ch09_model_optimization_1730130963007804 .dedication}
### Model Optimization {.calibre35}

[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html40 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="inference optimization"
secondary="model optimization"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html41 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model optimization" data-type="indexterm"}Model-level
optimization aims to make the model more efficient, often by modifying
the model itself, which can alter its behavior. As of this writing, many
foundation models follow the transformer architecture and include an
autoregressive language model component. These models have three
characteristics that make inference resource-intensive: model size,
autoregressive decoding, and the attention mechanism. Let's discuss
approaches to address these challenges.

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Model compression"}
::: {#ch09.html_ch09_model_compression_1730130963007882 .dedication}
#### Model compression {.calibre36}

[]{#ch09.html_id1678 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="inference optimization"
secondary="model optimization" tertiary="model compression"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1679 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model compression" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1680
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="model optimization"
secondary="model compression" data-type="indexterm"}Model compression
involves techniques that reduce a model's size. Making a model smaller
can also make it faster. This book has already discussed two model
compression techniques: quantization and distillation. Quantization,
reducing the precision of a model to reduce its memory footprint and
increase its throughput, is discussed in
[Chapter 7](#ch07.html_ch07){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}. Model distillation, training a small model
to mimic the behavior of the large model, is discussed in
[Chapter 8](#ch08.html_ch08_dataset_engineering_1730130932019888){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

[]{#ch09.html_id1681 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="distillation" secondary="model distillation"}Model distillation
suggests that it's possible to capture a large model's behaviors using
fewer parameters. Could it be that within the large model, there exists
a subset of parameters capable of capturing the entire model's behavior?
This is the core concept behind pruning.

Pruning, in the context of neural networks, has two meanings. One is to
remove entire nodes of a neural network, which means changing its
architecture and reducing its number of parameters. Another is to find
parameters least useful to predictions and set them to zero.
[]{#ch09.html_id1682 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="sparse models"}In this case, pruning doesn't reduce the total
number of parameters, only the number of non-zero parameters. This makes
the model more sparse, which both reduces the model's storage space and
speeds up computation.

Pruned models can be used as-is or be further finetuned to adjust the
remaining parameters and restore any performance degradation caused by
the pruning process. Pruning can help discover promising model
architectures ([Liu et al.,
2018](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.05270){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}). These pruned architectures, smaller than the
pre-pruned architectures, can also be trained from scratch ([Zhu et al.,
2017](https://arxiv.org/abs/1710.01878){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}).

In the literature, there have been many encouraging pruning results. For
example, [Frankle and Carbin (2019)](https://oreil.ly/qwlHE){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} showed that pruning
techniques can reduce the non-zero parameter counts of certain trained
networks by over 90%, decreasing memory footprints and improving speed
without compromising accuracy. However, in practice, as of this writing,
pruning is less common. It's harder to do, as it requires an
understanding of the original model's architecture, and the performance
boost it can bring is often much less than that of other approaches.
Pruning also results in sparse models, and not all hardware
architectures are designed to take advantage of the resulting sparsity.

*Weight-only quantization is by far the most popular approach since it's
easy to use, works out of the box for many models, and is extremely
effective.* Reducing a model's precision from 32 bits to 16 bits reduces
its memory footprint by half. However, we're close to the limit of
quantization---we can't go lower than 1 bit per value. Distillation is
also common because it can result in a smaller model whose behavior is
comparative to that of a much larger one for your needs.
:::
::::

:::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Overcoming the autoregressive decoding bottleneck"}
::::::::: {#ch09.html_ch09_overcoming_the_autoregressive_decoding_bottleneck_1730130963007948 .dedication}
#### Overcoming the autoregressive decoding bottleneck {.calibre36}

[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html42a .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="decoding"
secondary="autoregressive decoding bottleneck"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html42
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="autoregressive decoding bottleneck"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html43 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="bottlenecks" secondary="autoregressive decoding"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html44 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="inference optimization" secondary="model optimization"
tertiary="autoregressive decoding bottleneck"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html45 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model optimization"
secondary="autoregressive decoding bottleneck" data-type="indexterm"}As
discussed in
[Chapter 2](#ch02.html_ch02_understanding_foundation_models_1730147895571359){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"},
autoregressive language models generate one token after another. If it
takes 100 ms to generate one token, a response of 100 tokens will take
[10 s]{.keep-together}.^[19](#ch09.html_id1683){#ch09.html_id1683-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^ This process is not just slow, it's also
expensive. Across model API providers, an output token costs
approximately two to four times an input token. In an experiment,
Anyscale found that a single output token can have the same impact on
latency as 100 input tokens ([Kadous et al.,
2023](https://oreil.ly/QYdG8){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}). Improving the autoregressive generation process
by a small percentage can significantly improve user experience.

As the space is rapidly evolving, new techniques are being developed to
overcome this seemingly impossible bottleneck. Perhaps one day, there
will be architectures that don't have this bottleneck. The techniques
covered here are to illustrate what the solution might look like, but
the techniques are still evolving.

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Speculative decoding"}
::: {#ch09.html_ch09_speculative_decoding_1730130963008014 .dedication}
##### Speculative decoding {.calibre47}

[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html46 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="autoregressive decoding bottleneck"
secondary="speculative decoding"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html47 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model optimization"
secondary="autoregressive decoding bottleneck"
tertiary="speculative decoding"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html48 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="speculative decoding" data-type="indexterm"}Speculative
decoding (also called speculative sampling) uses a faster but less
powerful model to generate a sequence of tokens, which are then verified
by the target model. The target model is the model you want to use. The
faster model is called the draft or proposal model because it proposes
the draft output.

Imagine the input tokens are *x*~1~, *x*~2~, ..., *x*~*t*~:

1.  The draft model generates a sequence of *K* tokens: *x*~*t*\ +\ 1~,
    *x*~*t*\ +\ 2~, ..., *x*~*t*\ +\ *K*~.

2.  The target model verifies these *K* generated tokens in parallel.

3.  The target model *accepts* the longest subsequence of draft tokens,
    from left to right, which the target model agrees to use.

4.  Let's say the target model accepts *j* draft tokens, *x*~*t*\ +\ 1~,
    *x*~*t*\ +\ 2~, ..., *x*~*t*\ +\ *j*~. The target model then
    generates one extra token, *x*~*t*\ +\ *j*\ +\ 1~.

The process returns to step 1, with the draft model generating *K*
tokens conditioned on *x*~1~, *x*~2~, ..., *x*~*t*~, *x*~*t*\ +\ 1~,
*x*~*t*\ +\ 2~, ..., *x*~*t*\ +\ *j*~. The process is visualized in
[Figure 9-9](#ch09.html_ch09_figure_9_1730130962952786){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

If no draft token is accepted, this loop produces only one token
generated by the target model. If all draft tokens are accepted, this
loop produces *K* + 1 tokens, with *K* generated by the draft model and
one by the target model.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch09.html_ch09_figure_9_1730130962952786" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0909.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A diagram of words Description automatically generated with medium confidence" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 9-9. </span>A
draft model generates a sequence of K tokens, and the main model accepts
the longest subsequence that it agrees with. The image is from
“Blockwise Parallel Decoding for Deep Autoregressive Models” (<a
href="https://arxiv.org/abs/1811.03115"
class="pcalibre7 pcalibre5 pcalibre6 calibre10 pcalibre4">Stern et al.,
2018</a>).</h6>
</div>
</figure>

If all draft sequences are rejected, the target model must generate the
entire response in addition to verifying it, potentially leading to
increased latency. However, this can be avoided because of these three
insights:

1.  The time it takes for the target model to verify a sequence of
    tokens is less than the time it takes to generate it, because
    verification is parallelizable, while generation is sequential.
    Speculative decoding effectively turns the computation profile of
    decoding into that of prefilling.

2.  In an output token sequence, some tokens are easier to predict than
    others. It's possible to find a weaker draft model capable of
    getting these easier-to-predict tokens right, leading to a high
    acceptance rate of the draft tokens.

3.  Decoding is memory bandwidth-bound, which means that during the
    coding process, there are typically idle FLOPs that can be used for
    free verification.^[20](#ch09.html_id1684){#ch09.html_id1684-marker
    .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
    data-type="noteref"}^

Acceptance rates are domain-dependent. For texts that follow specific
structures like code, the acceptance rate is typically higher. Larger
values of *K* mean fewer verifying calls for the target model but a low
acceptance rate of the draft tokens. The draft model can be of any
architecture, though ideally it should share the same vocabulary and
tokenizer as the target model. You can train a custom draft model or use
an existing weaker model.

For example, to speed up the decoding process of Chinchilla-70B,
DeepMind trained a 4B-parameter draft model of the same architecture
([Chen et al., 2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.01318){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}). The draft model can
generate a token eight times faster than the target model (1.8 ms/token
compared to 14.1 ms/token). This reduces the overall response latency by
more than half without compromising response quality. A similar speed-up
was achieved for T5-XXL ([Laviathan et al.,
2022](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.17192){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}).

This approach has gained traction because it's relatively easy to
implement and doesn't change a model's quality. For example, it's
possible to do so in [50 lines of code in
PyTorch](https://oreil.ly/IaPOB){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}. It's been incorporated into popular inference
frameworks such as [vLLM](https://oreil.ly/uzg1s){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4},
[TensorRT-LLM](https://github.com/NVIDIA/TensorRT-LLM){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, and
[llama.cpp](https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp/pull/2926){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}.[]{#ch09.html_id1685
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html48"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1686 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html47" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1687
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html46"
data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Inference with reference"}
::: {#ch09.html_ch09_inference_with_reference_1730130963008072 .dedication}
##### Inference with reference {.calibre47}

[]{#ch09.html_id1688 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="autoregressive decoding bottleneck"
secondary="inference with reference"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1689 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="inference with reference"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1690 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model optimization"
secondary="autoregressive decoding bottleneck"
tertiary="inference with reference" data-type="indexterm"}Often, a
response needs to reference tokens from the input. For example, if you
ask your model a question about an attached document, the model might
repeat a chunk of text verbatim from the document. Another example is if
you ask the model to fix bugs in a piece of code, the model might reuse
the majority of the original code with minor changes. Instead of making
the model generate these repeated tokens, what if we copy these tokens
from the input to speed up the generation? This is the core idea behind
inference with reference.

Inference with reference is similar to speculative decoding, but instead
of using a model to generate draft tokens, it selects draft tokens from
the input. The key challenge is to develop an algorithm to identify the
most relevant text span from the context at each decoding step. The
simplest option is to find a text span that matches the current tokens.

Unlike speculative decoding, inference with reference doesn't require an
extra model. However, it's useful only in generation scenarios where
there's a significant overlap between contexts and outputs, such as in
retrieval systems, coding, or multi-turn conversations. In "Inference
with Reference: Lossless Acceleration of Large Language Models" ([Yang
et al., 2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.04487){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), this technique helps achieve two
times generation speedup in such use cases.

Examples of how inference with reference works are shown in
[Figure 9-10](#ch09.html_ch09_figure_10_1730130962952808){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

<figure class="width">
<div id="ch09.html_ch09_figure_10_1730130962952808" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0910.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A screenshot of a diagram Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 9-10.
</span>Two examples of inference with reference. The text spans that are
successfully copied from the input are in red and green. Image from Yang
et al. (2023). The image is licensed under CC BY 4.0.</h6>
</div>
</figure>
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Parallel decoding"}
::: {#ch09.html_ch09_parallel_decoding_1730130963008129 .dedication}
##### Parallel decoding {.calibre47}

[]{#ch09.html_id1691 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="autoregressive decoding bottleneck"
secondary="parallel decoding" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1692
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="model optimization"
secondary="autoregressive decoding bottleneck"
tertiary="parallel decoding" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1693
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="parallel decoding"
data-type="indexterm"}Instead of making autoregressive generation faster
with draft tokens, some techniques aim to break the sequential
dependency. Given an existing sequence of tokens *x*~1~,
*x*~2~,...,*x*~*t*~, these techniques attempt to generate
*x*~*t*\ +\ 1~, *x*~*t*\ +\ 2~,...,*x*~*t*\ +\ *k*~ simultaneously. This
means that the model generates *x*~*t*\ +\ 2~ before it knows that the
token before it is *x*~*t*\ +\ 1~.

This can work because the knowledge of the existing sequence often is
sufficient to predict the next few tokens. For example, given "the cat
sits", without knowing that the next token is "on", "under", or
"behind", you might still predict that the word after it is "the".

The parallel tokens can be generated by the same decoder, as in
Lookahead decoding ([Fu et al.,
2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.02057){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), or by different decoding heads, as in Medusa
([Cai et al., 2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.10774){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}). In Medusa, the original
model is extended with multiple decoding heads, and each head is a small
neural network layer that is then trained to predict a future token at a
specific position. If the original model is trained to predict the next
token *x*~*t*\ +\ 1~, the *k*^*th*^ head will predict the token
*x*~*t*\ +\ *k*\ +\ 1~. These heads are trained together with the
original model, but the original model is frozen. NVIDIA claimed Medusa
helped boost Llama 3.1 token generation by up to 1.9× on their HGX H200
GPUs ([Eassa et al., 2024](https://oreil.ly/FWYf5){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}).

However, because these tokens aren't generated sequentially, they need
to be verified to make sure that they fit together. An essential part of
parallel decoding is verification and integration. Lookahead decoding
uses the [Jacobi
method](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jacobi_method){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4}^[21](#ch09.html_id1694){#ch09.html_id1694-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ to
verify the generated tokens, which works as follows:

1.  K future tokens are generated in parallel.

2.  These *K* tokens are verified for coherence and consistency with the
    context.

3.  If one or more tokens fail verification, instead of aggregating all
    *K* future tokens, the model regenerates or adjusts only these
    failed tokens.

The model keeps refining the generated tokens until they all pass
verification and are integrated into the final output. This family of
parallel decoding algorithms is also called Jacobi decoding.

On the other hand, Medusa uses a tree-based attention mechanism to
verify and integrate tokens. Each Medusa head produces several options
for each position. These options are then organized into a tree-like
structure to select the most promising combination. The process is
visualized in
[Figure 9-11](#ch09.html_ch09_figure_11_1730130962952823){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch09.html_ch09_figure_11_1730130962952823" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0911.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A diagram of a model Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 9-11. </span>In
Medusa (Cai et al., 2024), each head predicts several options for a
token position. The most promising sequence from these options is
selected. Image adapted from the paper, which is licensed under CC BY
4.0.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

While the perspective of being able to circumvent sequential dependency
is appealing, parallel decoding is not intuitive, and some techniques,
like Medusa, can be challenging to implement. []{#ch09.html_id1695
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html45"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1696 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html44" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1697
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html43"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1698 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html42a" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1699
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html42"
data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::
:::::::::
::::::::::

:::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Attention mechanism optimization"}
::::::::::: {#ch09.html_ch09_attention_mechanism_optimization_1730130963008191 .dedication}
#### Attention mechanism optimization {.calibre36}

[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html49 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="attention mechanisms"
secondary="optimization" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html50
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="inference optimization"
secondary="model optimization"
tertiary="attention mechanism optimization"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html51 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model optimization"
secondary="attention mechanism optimization"
data-type="indexterm"}Recall from
[Chapter 2](#ch02.html_ch02_understanding_foundation_models_1730147895571359){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} that
generating the next token requires the key and value vectors for all
previous tokens. This means that the following applies:

- Generating token *x*~*t*~ requires the key and value vectors for
  tokens *x*~1~, *x*~2~, ..., *x*~*t*\ --\ 1~.

- Generating token *x*~*t*\ +\ 1~ requires the key and value vectors for
  tokens *x*~1~, *x*~2~, ...,*x*~*t*\ --\ 1~, *x*~*t*~.

When generating token *x*~*t*\ +\ 1~, instead of computing the key and
value vectors for tokens *x*~1~, *x*~2~, ..., *x*~*t*\ --\ 1~ again, you
reuse these vectors from the previous step. This means that you'll need
to compute the key and value vectors for only the most recent token,
*x*~*t*~. The cache that stores key and value vectors for reuse is
called the KV cache. []{#ch09.html_ch09.html51a .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
data-type="indexterm" primary="key-value (KV) cache"}The newly computed
key and value vectors are then added to the KV cache, which is
visualized in
[Figure 9-12](#ch09.html_ch09_figure_12_1730130962952844){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch09.html_ch09_figure_12_1730130962952844" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0912.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A diagram of a graph Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 9-12. </span>To
avoid recomputing the key and value vectors at each decoding step, use a
KV cache to store these vectors to reuse.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

::: {.calibre22 .note data-type="note"}
####### Note {.calibre23}

A KV cache is used only during inference, not training. During training,
because all tokens in a sequence are known in advance, next token
generation can be computed all at once instead of sequentially, as
during inference. Therefore, there's no need for a KV cache.
:::

Because generating a token requires computing the attention scores with
all previous tokens, the number of attention computations grows
exponentially with sequence
length.^[22](#ch09.html_id1700){#ch09.html_id1700-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^ The KV
cache size, on the other hand, grows linearly with sequence length.

The KV cache size also grows with larger batch sizes. A Google paper
calculated that for a 500B+ model with multi-head attention, batch size
512, and context length 2048, the KV cache totals 3TB [(Pope et al.,
2022)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.05102){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}. This is three times the size of that
model's weights.

The KV cache size is ultimately limited by the available hardware
storage, creating a bottleneck for running applications with long
context. A large cache size also takes time to load into memory, which
can be an issue for applications with strict latency.

The computation and memory requirements of the attention mechanism are
one of the reasons why it's so hard to have longer context.

Many techniques have been developed to make the attention mechanism more
efficient. In general, they fall into three buckets: redesigning the
attention mechanism, optimizing the KV cache, and writing kernels for
attention computation.

<aside data-type="sidebar" epub:type="sidebar" class="calibre20">

::: {#ch09.html_ch09_calculating_the_kv_cache_size_1730130963008252 .sidebar}
## Calculating the KV Cache Size {.calibre21}

[]{#ch09.html_id1701 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="inference optimization"
secondary="KV cache size calculation" data-type="indexterm"}The memory
needed for the KV cache, without any optimization, is calculated as
[follows:]{.keep-together}

- 2 × *B* × *S* × *L* × *H* × *M*

<!-- -->

- *B*: batch size

- *S*: sequence length

- *L*: number of transformer layers

- *H*: model dimension

- *M*: memory needed for the cache's numerical representation (e.g.,
  FP16 or FP32).

This value can become substantial as the context length increases. For
example, LLama 2 13B has 40 layers and a model dimension of 5,120. With
a batch size of 32, sequence length of 2,048, and 2 bytes per value, the
memory needed for its KV cache, without any optimization, is 2 × 32 ×
2,048 × 40 × 5,120 × 2 = 54 GB.
:::

</aside>

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Redesigning the attention mechanism"}
::: {#ch09.html_ch09_redesigning_the_attention_mechanism_1730130963008309 .dedication}
##### Redesigning the attention mechanism {.calibre47}

[]{#ch09.html_id1702 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="attention mechanisms"
secondary="optimization" tertiary="attention mechanism redesign"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1703 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="attention mechanisms" secondary="redesign"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1704 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model optimization"
secondary="attention mechanism optimization"
tertiary="attention mechanism redesign" data-type="indexterm"}These
techniques involve altering how the attention mechanism works. Even
though these techniques help optimize inference, because they change a
model's architecture directly, they can be applied only during training
or finetuning.

For example, when generating a new token, instead of attending to all
previous tokens, *local windowed attention* attends only to a fixed size
window of nearby tokens ([Beltagy et al.,
2020](https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.05150v2){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}). This reduces the effective sequence
length to a fixed size window, reducing both the KV cache and the
attention computation. If the average sequence length is 10,000 tokens,
attending to a window size of 1,000 tokens reduces the KV cache size by
10 times.

Local windowed attention can be interleaved with global attention, with
local attention capturing nearby context; the global attention captures
task-specific information across the document.

[]{#ch09.html_id1705 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="cross-layer attention"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1706 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="multi-query attention" data-type="indexterm"}Both *cross-layer
attention* ([Brandon et al.,
2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.12981?ref=research.character.ai){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}) and *multi-query
attention* ([Shazeer,
2019](https://arxiv.org/abs/1911.02150?ref=research.character.ai){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}) reduce the memory
footprint of the KV cache by reducing the number of key-value pairs.
Cross-layer attention shares key and value vectors across adjacent
layers. Having three layers sharing the same key-value vectors means
reducing the KV cache three times. On the other hand, multi-query
attention shares key-value vectors across query heads.

[]{#ch09.html_id1707 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="grouped-query attention"
data-type="indexterm"}*Grouped-query attention* ([Ainslie et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.13245){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}) is a generalization of multi-query attention.
Instead of using only one set of key-value pairs for all query heads,
its grouped-query attention puts query heads into smaller groups and
shares key-value pairs only among query heads in the same group. This
allows for a more flexible balance between the number of query heads and
the number of key-value pairs.

Character.AI, an AI chatbot application, shares that their average
conversation has a dialogue history of [180
messages](https://oreil.ly/nLt6A){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} (2024). Given the typically long sequences, the
primary bottleneck for inference throughput is the KV cache size. Three
attention mechanism designs---multi-query attention, interleaving local
attention and global attention, and cross-layer attention---help them
*reduce KV cache by over 20 times*. More importantly, this significant
KV cache reduction means that memory is no longer a bottleneck for them
for serving large batch sizes.
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Optimizing the KV cache size"}
::: {#ch09.html_ch09_reduce_the_kv_cache_size_1730130963008365 .dedication}
##### Optimizing the KV cache size {.calibre47}

[]{#ch09.html_id1708 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="model optimization"
secondary="attention mechanism optimization"
tertiary="KV cache size optimization" data-type="indexterm"}The way the
KV cache is managed is critical in mitigating the memory bottleneck
during inference and enabling a larger batch size, especially for
applications with long context. Many techniques are actively being
developed to reduce and manage the KV cache.

One of the fastest growing inference frameworks,
[vLLM](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, gained popularity for introducing
PagedAttention, which optimizes memory management by dividing the KV
cache into non-contiguous blocks, reducing fragmentation, and enabling
flexible memory sharing to improve LLM serving efficiency ([Kwon et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.06180){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}).

Other techniques include KV cache quantization ([Hooper et al.,
2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.18079){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}; [Kang et al.,
2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.05527){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), adaptive KV cache compression ([Ge et al.,
2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.01801){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}), and selective KV cache ([Liu et al.,
2024](https://oreil.ly/ixtBl){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}).[]{#ch09.html_id1709 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html51a" data-type="indexterm"}
:::
::::

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect4" pdf-bookmark="Writing kernels for attention computation"}
::: {#ch09.html_ch09_writing_kernels_for_attention_computation_1730130963008422 .dedication}
##### Writing kernels for attention computation {.calibre47}

[]{#ch09.html_id1710 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="attention mechanisms"
secondary="optimization"
tertiary="wiring kernels for attention computation"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1711 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="kernels" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1712 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="model optimization"
secondary="attention mechanism optimization"
tertiary="write kernels for attention computation"
data-type="indexterm"}Instead of changing the mechanism design or
optimizing the storage, this approach looks into how attention scores
are computed and finds ways to make this computation more efficient.
This approach is the most effective when it takes into account the
hardware executing the computation. The code optimized for a specific
chip is called a kernel. Kernel writing will be discussed further in the
next section.

One of the most well-known kernels optimized for attention computation
is
[FlashAttention](https://github.com/Dao-AILab/flash-attention){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (Dao et al., 2022). This
kernel fused together many operations commonly used in a
transformer-based model to make them run faster, as shown in
[]{#ch09.html_id1713 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html51"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1714 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html50" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1715
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html49"
data-type="indexterm"}[Figure 9-13](#ch09.html_ch09_figure_13_1730130962952862){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch09.html_ch09_figure_13_1730130962952862" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0913.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A graph of a graph with text Description automatically generated with medium confidence" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 9-13.
</span>FlashAttention is a kernel that fuses together several common
operators. Adapted from an original image licensed under BSD
3-Clause.</h6>
</div>
</figure>
:::
::::
:::::::::::
::::::::::::

::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Kernels and compilers"}
:::: {#ch09.html_ch09_kernels_and_compilers_1730130963008481 .dedication}
#### Kernels and compilers {.calibre36}

[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html52 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="inference optimization"
secondary="model optimization" tertiary="kernels and compilers"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html53 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="kernels" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html54
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="model optimization"
secondary="kernels and compilers" data-type="indexterm"}Kernels are
specialized pieces of code optimized for specific hardware accelerators,
such as GPUs or TPUs. They are typically written to perform
computationally intensive routines that need to be executed repeatedly,
often in parallel, to maximize the performance of these accelerators.

Common AI operations, including matrix multiplication, attention
computation, and convolution operation, all have specialized kernels to
make their computation more efficient on different
hardware.^[23](#ch09.html_id1716){#ch09.html_id1716-marker .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="noteref"}^

Writing kernels requires a deep understanding of the underlying hardware
architecture. This includes knowledge about how the memory hierarchy is
structured (such as caches, global memory, shared memory, and registers)
and how data is accessed and moved between these different levels.

Moreover, kernels are typically written in lower-level programming
languages like CUDA (for NVIDIA GPUs), Triton (a language developed by
OpenAI for writing custom kernels), and ROCm (for AMD GPUs). These
languages allow fine-grained control over thread management and memory
access but are also harder to learn than the languages that most AI
engineers are familiar with, like Python.

Due to this entry barrier, writing kernels used to be a dark art
practiced by a few. Chip makers like NVIDIA and AMD employ optimization
engineers to write kernels to make their hardware efficient for AI
workloads, whereas AI frameworks like PyTorch and TensorFlow employ
kernel engineers to optimize their frameworks on different accelerators.

However, with the rising demand for inference optimization and the
ubiquity of accelerators, more AI engineers have taken an interest in
writing kernels. There are many great online tutorials for kernel
writing. Here, I'll cover four common techniques often used to speed up
computation:

Vectorization

:   []{#ch09.html_id1717 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="vectorization"
    data-type="indexterm"}Given a loop or a nested loop, instead of
    processing one data element at a time, simultaneously execute
    multiple data elements that are contiguous in memory. This reduces
    latency by minimizing data I/O operations.

Parallelization

:   []{#ch09.html_id1718 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="parallelization"
    data-type="indexterm"}Divide an input array (or n-dimensional array)
    into independent chunks that can be processed simultaneously on
    different cores or threads, speeding up the computation.

Loop tiling

:   []{#ch09.html_id1719 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="loop tiling"
    data-type="indexterm"}Optimize the data accessing order in a loop
    for the hardware's memory layout and cache. This optimization is
    hardware-dependent. An efficient CPU tiling pattern may not work
    well on GPUs.

Operator fusion

:   []{#ch09.html_id1720 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
    .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="operator fusion"
    data-type="indexterm"}Combine multiple operators into a single pass
    to avoid redundant memory access. For example, if two loops operate
    over the same array, they can be fused into one, reducing the number
    of times data is read and written.

:   While vectorization, parallelization, and loop tiling can be applied
    broadly across different models, operator fusion requires a deeper
    understanding of a model's specific operators and architecture. As a
    result, operator fusion demands more attention from optimization
    engineers.

Kernels are optimized for a hardware architecture. This means that
whenever a new hardware architecture is introduced, new kernels need to
be developed. For example,
[FlashAttention](https://github.com/Dao-AILab/flash-attention){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (Dao et al., 2022) was
originally developed primarily for NVIDIA A100 GPUs. Later on,
FlashAttention-3 was introduced for H100 GPUs ([Shah et al.,
2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.08608){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}).

A model script specifies a series of operations that need to be
performed to execute that model. To run this code on a piece of
hardware, such as a GPU, it has to be converted into a language
compatible with that hardware. This process is called *lowering*.
[]{#ch09.html_id1721 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="compilers"
data-type="indexterm"}A tool that *lowers* code to run a specific
hardware is called a compiler. Compilers bridge ML models and the
hardware they run on. During the lowering process, whenever possible,
these operations are converted into specialized kernels to run faster on
the target hardware.

<aside data-type="sidebar" epub:type="sidebar" class="calibre20">

::: {#ch09.html_ch09_inference_optimization_case_study_from_pytorch_1730130963008570 .sidebar}
## Inference Optimization Case Study from PyTorch {.calibre21}

[]{#ch09.html_id1722 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm" primary="Llama"
secondary="inference optimization"}[]{#ch09.html_id1723 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="inference optimization" secondary="case study from PyTorch"
data-type="indexterm"}[Figure 9-14](#ch09.html_ch09_figure_14_1730130962952879){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"} shows how
much throughput improvement the PyTorch team could give to Llama-7B
through the following optimization steps ([PyTorch,
2023](https://oreil.ly/_5Nqa){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4}):

1.  Call torch.compile to compile the model into more efficient kernels.

2.  Quantize the model weights to INT8.

3.  Further quantize the model weights to INT4.

4.  Add speculative decoding.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch09.html_ch09_figure_14_1730130962952879" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0914.png" class="calibre60 pcalibre8"
alt="A graph with numbers and a bar Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre61"><span class="keep-together">Figure 9-14.
</span>Throughput improvement by different optimization techniques in
PyTorch. Image from PyTorch (2023).</h6>
</div>
</figure>

The experiment was run on an A100 GPU with 80 GB of memory. It was
unclear how these optimization steps impact the model's output
quality.[]{#ch09.html_id1724 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html41"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1725 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html40" data-type="indexterm"}
:::

</aside>

Compilers can be standalone tools, such as [Apache
TVM](https://github.com/apache/tvm){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6
.calibre10 .pcalibre4} and [MLIR](https://mlir.llvm.org){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (Multi-Level Intermediate
Representation) or integrated into ML and inference frameworks, like
[`torch.compile`{.calibre26}](https://oreil.ly/6bjVM){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (a feature in PyTorch),
[XLA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accelerated_Linear_Algebra){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4} (Accelerated Linear
Algebra, originally developed by TensorFlow, with an open source version
called [OpenXLA](https://github.com/openxla/xla){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}), and the compiler built into the
[TensorRT](https://github.com/NVIDIA/TensorRT){.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4}, which is optimized for NVIDIA GPUs.
AI companies might have their own compilers, with their proprietary
kernels designed to speed up their own workloads[]{#ch09.html_id1726
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html54"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1727 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary=""
startref="ch09.html53" data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_id1728
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
contenteditable="false" primary="" startref="ch09.html52"
data-type="indexterm"}.^[24](#ch09.html_id1729){#ch09.html_id1729-marker
.pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4
data-type="noteref"}^
::::
:::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::: {.section .praise data-type="sect2" pdf-bookmark="Inference Service Optimization"}
::::::::::: {#ch09.html_ch09_inference_service_optimization_1730130963008735 .dedication}
### Inference Service Optimization {.calibre35}

[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html55 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="inference optimization"
secondary="inference service optimization"
data-type="indexterm"}[]{#ch09.html_ch09.html56 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5
.pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="inference service optimization" data-type="indexterm"}Most
service-level optimization techniques focus on resource management.
Given a fixed amount of resources (compute and memory) and dynamic
workloads (inference requests from users that may involve different
models), the goal is to efficiently allocate resources to these
workloads to optimize for latency and cost. Unlike many model-level
techniques, service-level techniques don't modify models and shouldn't
change the output quality.

:::: {.section .praise data-type="sect3" pdf-bookmark="Batching"}
::: {#ch09.html_ch09_batching_1730130963008799 .dedication}
#### Batching {.calibre36}

[]{#ch09.html_id1730 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" primary="inference optimization"
secondary="inference service optimization" tertiary="batching"
data-type="indexterm"}One of the easiest ways to reduce your cost is
batching. In production, your inference service might receive multiple
requests simultaneously. Instead of processing each request separately,
batching the requests that arrive around the same time together can
significantly reduce the service's throughput. If processing each
request separately is like everyone driving their own car, batching is
like putting them together on a bus. A bus can move more people, but it
can also make each person's journey longer. However, if you do it
intelligently, the impact on latency can be minimal.

The three main techniques for batching are: static batching, dynamic
batching, and continuous batching.

[]{#ch09.html_id1731 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="batching" secondary="static"}[]{#ch09.html_id1732 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="static batching" data-type="indexterm"}The simplest batching
technique is *static batching*. The service groups a fixed number of
inputs together in a batch. It's like a bus that waits until every seat
is filled before departing. The drawback of static batching is that all
requests have to wait until the batch is full to be executed. Thus the
first request in a batch is delayed until the batch's last request
arrives, no matter how late the last request is.

[]{#ch09.html_id1733 .pcalibre7 .pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10
.pcalibre4 contenteditable="false" data-type="indexterm"
primary="batching" secondary="dynamic"}[]{#ch09.html_id1734 .pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 contenteditable="false"
primary="dynamic batching" data-type="indexterm"}*Dynamic batching*, on
the other hand, sets a maximum time window for each batch. If the batch
size is four and the window is 100 ms, the server processes the batch
either when it has four requests or when 100 ms has passed, whichever
happens first. It's like a bus that leaves on a fixed schedule or when
it's full. This approach keeps latency under control, so earlier
requests aren't held up by later ones. The downside is that batches may
not always be full when processed, possibly leading to wasted compute.
Static batching and dynamic batching are visualized in
[Figure 9-15](#ch09.html_ch09_figure_15_1730130962952896){.pcalibre7
.pcalibre5 .pcalibre6 .calibre10 .pcalibre4 data-type="xref"}.

<figure class="calibre37">
<div id="ch09.html_ch09_figure_15_1730130962952896" class="figure">
<img src="OEBPS/assets/aien_0915.png" class="calibre38"
alt="A screenshot of a computer Description automatically generated" />
<h6 class="calibre39"><span class="keep-together">Figure 9-15.
</span>Dynamic batching keeps the latency manageable but might be less
compute-efficient.</h6>
</div>
</figure>

In naive batching implementations, all batch requests have to be
